title: Nitrates + PDE5 inhibitor interaction
stem:
A 58-year-old man with chronic stable angina asks for a “fast-acting” medication he can carry for sudden chest tightness.
PMH: CAD with prior PCI, BPH.
Meds: aspirin 81 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, metoprolol tartrate 50 mg twice daily, tadalafil 5 mg daily.
Vitals today: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 62 bpm.
question: Which medication is contraindicated while he is taking tadalafil due to risk of profound hypotension?

rationales.A: Correct. Nitrates (including sublingual nitroglycerin) combined with PDE5 inhibitors like tadalafil can cause dangerous, potentially life‑threatening hypotension due to synergistic vasodilation.
rationales.B: Ranolazine can be used for chronic angina symptom control and does not share the same severe interaction with PDE5 inhibitors.
rationales.C: Low-dose aspirin is appropriate for secondary prevention in CAD and does not cause the nitrate–PDE5 interaction.
rationales.D: Statins are standard for ASCVD risk reduction and do not create the profound hypotension interaction seen with nitrates + PDE5 inhibitors.
rationales.E: Beta-blockers can be used in stable angina/CAD and do not have the same contraindication with tadalafil.

takeaway: Always screen for PDE5 inhibitor use (sildenafil/tadalafil) before giving nitrates—combining them can precipitate severe hypotension.

title: Optimizing HFrEF therapy
stem:
A 63-year-old woman has chronic HFrEF (EF 30%) with NYHA class II symptoms despite guideline-directed therapy.
Meds: lisinopril 20 mg daily, carvedilol 25 mg twice daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily.
Labs: K 4.6 mEq/L, SCr 1.0 mg/dL.
Vitals: BP 118/70 mmHg, HR 64 bpm.
question: Which medication change provides additional mortality benefit for symptomatic HFrEF in this stable patient?

answer_choices.text: Replace lisinopril with sacubitril/valsartan (after an ACE inhibitor washout)
Stop carvedilol and start diltiazem for “better rate control”
Increase furosemide dose for mortality benefit
Start amiodarone prophylactically to prevent arrhythmias

rationales.B: Correct. Sacubitril/valsartan (an ARNI) is recommended for symptomatic chronic HFrEF to reduce morbidity and mortality, typically replacing an ACE inhibitor (with a 36-hour washout to reduce angioedema risk).
rationales.A: Digoxin can reduce hospitalizations in select patients but does not provide the same mortality benefit as switching to an ARNI.
rationales.C: Non-dihydropyridine CCBs like diltiazem can worsen systolic HF due to negative inotropy and are generally avoided in HFrEF.
rationales.D: Loop diuretics improve congestion and symptoms but have not shown mortality benefit; they are titrated for euvolemia, not survival.
rationales.E: Amiodarone is not started prophylactically for all HFrEF patients; it’s used for specific arrhythmia indications and carries significant toxicity risks.

takeaway: In stable symptomatic HFrEF, switching ACEi/ARB to ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) is a key step for additional survival benefit (with ACEi washout).

title: ACE inhibitor cough → switch to ARB
stem:
A 52-year-old man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes has new microalbuminuria. He was started on lisinopril 10 mg daily.
Two weeks later he reports a persistent dry cough that began after starting the medication. He denies facial swelling or trouble breathing.
question: Which medication is the best substitute to maintain kidney protection and blood pressure control?

rationales.C: Correct. ARBs (e.g., losartan) provide similar renal and cardiovascular benefits as ACE inhibitors and are the typical alternative when ACE inhibitor–induced cough occurs.
rationales.A: Dihydropyridine CCBs lower BP but do not provide the same albuminuria-lowering renal benefits as ACEi/ARBs.
rationales.B: Thiazide diuretics lower BP but are not the primary choice for diabetic albuminuria renal protection.
rationales.D: Beta-blockers can treat BP but are not first-line for diabetic nephropathy protection and may mask hypoglycemia.
rationales.E: Non-dihydropyridine CCBs can lower BP/HR but are not preferred for albuminuria-lowering renal protection.

takeaway: ACEi cough → switch to an ARB (renal protection preserved). ACEi angioedema is different and requires extra caution.

title: Diuretic choice in advanced CKD with edema
stem:
A 70-year-old woman has uncontrolled hypertension and worsening leg edema.
PMH: CKD stage 4, HTN.
Meds: amlodipine 10 mg daily.
Labs: SCr 2.6 mg/dL (eGFR ~22 mL/min/1.73m²), K 4.3 mEq/L.
Vitals: BP 158/84 mmHg, HR 76 bpm.
question: Which diuretic is most appropriate to improve edema and help control blood pressure at this level of kidney function?

rationales.C: Correct. Loop diuretics (e.g., furosemide) remain effective at lower eGFR and are preferred for volume overload/edema in advanced CKD.
rationales.A: Hydrochlorothiazide is less effective for diuresis when eGFR is significantly reduced (often <30 mL/min/1.73m²).
rationales.B: Chlorthalidone can lower BP and may have some effect in CKD, but for significant edema/volume overload at eGFR ~22, a loop diuretic is typically more effective.
rationales.D: Spironolactone can worsen hyperkalemia risk in CKD and is not the first choice for edema control in advanced CKD.
rationales.E: Diltiazem is not a diuretic and will not directly relieve volume overload.

takeaway: For significant edema with eGFR <30, think loop diuretic (furosemide/torsemide/bumetanide).

title: Hyperkalemia on MRA in HFrEF
stem:
A 68-year-old man with HFrEF (EF 35%) comes for follow-up. He feels well, but routine labs are abnormal.
Meds: lisinopril 20 mg daily, carvedilol 25 mg twice daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily.
Labs today: K 6.0 mEq/L, SCr 1.2 mg/dL.
ECG: peaked T waves.
question: Which medication should be stopped first to address hyperkalemia while preserving as much HF mortality benefit as possible?

rationales.B: Correct. Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone/eplerenone) can significantly raise potassium. With K ≥5.5–6, stopping the MRA is a common first step while urgently addressing hyperkalemia.
rationales.A: Beta-blockers are foundational mortality-reducing therapy in HFrEF and are not typical first discontinuation for hyperkalemia.
rationales.C: Loop diuretics lower potassium and relieve congestion; they are not the cause of hyperkalemia and may help treat it.
rationales.D: Statins do not meaningfully increase potassium.
rationales.E: Aspirin does not meaningfully increase potassium in this context.

takeaway: Spironolactone/eplerenone improve HFrEF outcomes but can cause hyperkalemia—monitor K/SCr and hold the MRA if potassium becomes dangerously high.

title: Rate control in AF with HFrEF
stem:
A 71-year-old woman with chronic HFrEF (EF 30%) and persistent atrial fibrillation presents with palpitations.
Vitals: BP 110/72 mmHg, HR 142 bpm (irregularly irregular).
She is not volume overloaded.
question: Which medication should be avoided for rate control because it can worsen systolic heart failure?

rationales.D: Correct. Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (diltiazem/verapamil) have negative inotropic effects and can worsen HFrEF.
rationales.A: Evidence-based beta-blockers like metoprolol succinate can control rate and improve outcomes in HFrEF when tolerated.
rationales.B: Digoxin can help with rate control in AF (especially at rest) and does not depress contractility like non-DHP CCBs.
rationales.C: Amiodarone may be used when other agents are ineffective/contraindicated; it does not have the same negative inotropy as diltiazem.
rationales.E: Carvedilol is an evidence-based beta-blocker in HFrEF and can help with rate control.

takeaway: In AF with HFrEF, avoid diltiazem/verapamil; use evidence-based beta-blockers and/or digoxin depending on hemodynamics.

title: Statin + macrolide interaction
stem:
A 57-year-old man takes simvastatin 40 mg nightly for hyperlipidemia. He is prescribed clarithromycin for acute bacterial sinusitis.
He asks if he can take both medications together.
question: What is the best recommendation to reduce the risk of severe statin-associated myopathy/rhabdomyolysis?

answer_choices.text: Continue simvastatin and clarithromycin without changes
Hold simvastatin during clarithromycin therapy and restart afterward
Double the simvastatin dose while on clarithromycin
Switch simvastatin to lovastatin while on clarithromycin
Add gemfibrozil to prevent muscle symptoms

rationales.B: Correct. Clarithromycin is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor and can markedly increase simvastatin concentrations, raising the risk of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis. Holding simvastatin during the antibiotic course (or switching to a non–CYP3A4 statin) is appropriate.
rationales.A: Co-administration significantly increases myopathy risk; no-change is unsafe.
rationales.C: Increasing the simvastatin dose would further increase toxicity risk.
rationales.D: Lovastatin is also primarily metabolized by CYP3A4 and would have a similar interaction risk.
rationales.E: Gemfibrozil increases statin myopathy risk and is not used to 'prevent' statin muscle toxicity.

takeaway: Macrolides (especially clarithromycin) can increase CYP3A4-metabolized statins (simvastatin/lovastatin) → myopathy risk. Hold or switch statin.

title: Severe hypertriglyceridemia
stem:
A 46-year-old woman has fasting triglycerides of 1,200 mg/dL on repeat testing.
PMH: Type 2 diabetes, obesity.
Meds: metformin 1,000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 20 mg nightly.
She denies alcohol use. No history of pancreatitis.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to add primarily to reduce triglycerides and prevent pancreatitis?

rationales.A: Correct. Fibrates (e.g., fenofibrate) are effective for marked triglyceride lowering and are commonly used to reduce pancreatitis risk when TG are very high (e.g., >500–1000 mg/dL).
rationales.B: Ezetimibe primarily lowers LDL cholesterol, not triglycerides to the same degree needed here.
rationales.C: PCSK9 inhibitors primarily lower LDL cholesterol; they are not first-line for severe hypertriglyceridemia.
rationales.D: Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet agent and does not lower triglycerides.
rationales.E: Ranolazine is used for angina symptom control and does not address triglycerides.

takeaway: Very high TG levels (especially >1000) raise pancreatitis risk—fibrates are a classic triglyceride-lowering option.

title: P2Y12 inhibitor adverse effect
stem:
A 60-year-old man had an NSTEMI and drug-eluting stent placed 2 weeks ago.
Meds: aspirin 81 mg daily + ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg nightly.
He reports new bothersome shortness of breath that started after ticagrelor initiation. Workup shows no heart failure or pulmonary cause.
question: Which medication is the best alternative to maintain dual antiplatelet therapy while addressing the suspected ticagrelor side effect?

answer_choices.text: Stop aspirin and continue ticagrelor alone
Add apixaban to ticagrelor and aspirin

rationales.B: Correct. Ticagrelor can cause dyspnea. Switching to another P2Y12 inhibitor such as clopidogrel can maintain DAPT while mitigating the adverse effect.
rationales.A: Stopping aspirin early after stenting can increase stent thrombosis risk; standard DAPT includes aspirin unless a specific strategy is chosen for bleeding risk.
rationales.C: Warfarin is an anticoagulant and is not a substitute for a P2Y12 inhibitor for stent protection.
rationales.D: Adding anticoagulation increases bleeding risk and is not indicated solely for ticagrelor-associated dyspnea.
rationales.E: Rivaroxaban is an anticoagulant and does not replace aspirin for standard DAPT in this scenario.

takeaway: If ticagrelor causes intolerable dyspnea, clopidogrel is a common alternative P2Y12 inhibitor to continue DAPT post-PCI.

title: Mechanical heart valve anticoagulation
stem:
A 66-year-old woman has atrial fibrillation and a mechanical mitral valve replacement.
She asks about “newer blood thinners” because she is tired of INR checks.
question: Which anticoagulant is indicated for stroke/systemic embolism prevention in this patient?

rationales.D: Correct. Mechanical heart valves require vitamin K antagonist therapy (warfarin). DOACs are not indicated for mechanical valves.
rationales.A: Apixaban is used for nonvalvular AF and VTE but is not indicated for mechanical valves.
rationales.B: Rivaroxaban is also a DOAC and not indicated for mechanical valves.
rationales.C: Enoxaparin may be used short-term (e.g., bridging) but is not standard for indefinite long-term anticoagulation of mechanical valves.
rationales.E: Antiplatelet therapy alone is insufficient to prevent valve-related thromboembolism.

takeaway: Mechanical valve = warfarin (not DOACs). This is a classic board-style exception.

title: Warfarin + antibiotic
stem:
A 76-year-old woman with a history of DVT takes warfarin with a stable INR around 2.4.
She has dysuria and urinary frequency. UA suggests uncomplicated cystitis. She has a documented sulfonamide allergy (hives).
Renal function: eGFR 65 mL/min/1.73m².
question: Which antibiotic is the best choice for uncomplicated cystitis while minimizing warfarin interaction and avoiding sulfa?

rationales.B: Correct. Nitrofurantoin is a common first-line agent for uncomplicated cystitis and has relatively minimal effect on warfarin compared with many other antibiotics.
rationales.A: TMP/SMX is contraindicated due to her sulfonamide allergy and can significantly increase INR via CYP inhibition.
rationales.C: Fluoroquinolones can increase INR and are generally avoided when a safer first-line option exists.
rationales.D: Levofloxacin can also raise INR and is not preferred for uncomplicated cystitis when alternatives are available.
rationales.E: Clarithromycin can substantially increase INR and is not used for uncomplicated cystitis.

takeaway: In a warfarin patient with sulfa allergy and uncomplicated cystitis (adequate renal function), nitrofurantoin is often a clean choice.

title: Diabetes medication and acute kidney injury
stem:
A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with fever and hypotension and is diagnosed with sepsis.
Home meds include metformin 1,000 mg twice daily.
Labs: SCr 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.0), eGFR ~28 mL/min/1.73m², lactate 3.8 mmol/L.
question: Which medication should be held due to increased risk of lactic acidosis in this setting?

rationales.A: Correct. In acute illness with hypoperfusion, sepsis, and AKI (eGFR <30), metformin should be held due to increased risk of metformin-associated lactic acidosis.
rationales.B: Sitagliptin may require renal dose adjustment but is not classically associated with lactic acidosis.
rationales.C: Sulfonylureas can cause hypoglycemia, but the specific concern here (AKI + lactate) points to metformin.
rationales.D: GLP-1 receptor agonists are not associated with lactic acidosis; they can cause GI effects and rare pancreatitis.
rationales.E: SGLT2 inhibitors should be used cautiously in acute illness (risk of dehydration/euglycemic DKA), but the hallmark lactic acidosis risk in AKI is metformin.

takeaway: Sepsis/AKI = hold metformin. Reassess renal function before restarting.

title: T2DM + HFrEF
stem:
A 59-year-old man has type 2 diabetes and chronic HFrEF (EF 35%). He is currently on metformin.
A1c is 7.8%. eGFR is 55 mL/min/1.73m². He has mild lower-extremity edema but no acute decompensation.
question: Which medication is best to add for glycemic control with proven heart failure benefit?

rationales.B: Correct. SGLT2 inhibitors such as empagliflozin reduce HF hospitalization (and provide cardiovascular/renal benefits) in patients with diabetes and heart failure.
rationales.A: Pioglitazone can cause fluid retention and may worsen heart failure.
rationales.C: Sulfonylureas can lower A1c but have hypoglycemia risk and no HF outcome benefit.
rationales.D: DPP-4 inhibitors lower A1c modestly but do not provide the same HF outcome benefits as SGLT2 inhibitors.
rationales.E: Insulin is effective for glucose control but is not the preferred next step for HF-specific benefit in a stable outpatient.

takeaway: In T2DM + HF, SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin/dapagliflozin) are high-yield because they improve HF outcomes beyond A1c lowering.

title: T2DM with obesity and suboptimal A1c
stem:
A 41-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes (A1c 8.2%) and obesity (BMI 36) is on metformin and wants a medication that helps with weight loss.
She has no history of pancreatitis and no personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma.
question: Which medication is the best next option to improve glycemic control and promote weight loss?

rationales.C: Correct. GLP-1 receptor agonists such as semaglutide lower A1c and are associated with clinically meaningful weight loss.
rationales.A: Sulfonylureas can cause weight gain and hypoglycemia.
rationales.B: Pioglitazone often causes weight gain and fluid retention.
rationales.D: Insulin is effective but commonly leads to weight gain and is not the preferred next add-on when weight loss is a major goal.
rationales.E: DPP-4 inhibitors are weight-neutral and generally provide less A1c reduction than GLP-1 receptor agonists.

takeaway: When weight loss is a priority in T2DM, GLP-1 receptor agonists are a high-yield choice.

title: Sulfonylurea hypoglycemia
stem:
A 69-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with shakiness and sweating.
Fingerstick glucose in clinic is 48 mg/dL.
Meds: metformin 1,000 mg twice daily and glipizide 10 mg daily.
He reports he skipped lunch today.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible for his hypoglycemia?

rationales.B: Correct. Sulfonylureas (glipizide, glimepiride) increase insulin secretion regardless of meals and are a common cause of hypoglycemia, especially when a meal is missed.
rationales.A: Metformin is not typically associated with hypoglycemia when used alone.
rationales.C: Pioglitazone is not commonly associated with hypoglycemia when used without insulin/secretagogues.
rationales.D: DPP-4 inhibitors have low hypoglycemia risk unless combined with insulin/sulfonylureas.
rationales.E: SGLT2 inhibitors have low hypoglycemia risk unless combined with insulin/secretagogues.

takeaway: Sulfonylureas are a classic cause of hypoglycemia—especially in older adults and when meals are skipped.

title: Graves disease in first trimester
stem:
A 28-year-old woman (10 weeks pregnant) is diagnosed with Graves disease.
She has tachycardia, weight loss, and a suppressed TSH with elevated free T4.
question: Which antithyroid medication is preferred during the first trimester of pregnancy?

rationales.B: Correct. PTU is preferred in the first trimester because methimazole has been associated with specific congenital anomalies when used early in pregnancy.
rationales.A: Methimazole is generally preferred outside the first trimester due to lower hepatotoxicity risk, but PTU is preferred in the first trimester.
rationales.C: Levothyroxine treats hypothyroidism, not Graves hyperthyroidism.
rationales.D: Liothyronine is not used to treat hyperthyroidism and can worsen symptoms.
rationales.E: Steroids are not first-line chronic therapy for Graves disease (though they may be used in select thyroid storm/ophthalmopathy contexts).

takeaway: Pregnancy high-yield: PTU in 1st trimester; methimazole typically preferred later due to PTU hepatotoxicity risk.

title: Hypothyroidism in CAD
stem:
A 67-year-old man with coronary artery disease presents with fatigue and weight gain.
Labs: TSH 18 mIU/L (high), free T4 low.
question: Which medication is preferred for long-term thyroid hormone replacement in this patient?

rationales.C: Correct. Levothyroxine (T4) is the standard therapy for hypothyroidism. In older adults/CAD, start low and titrate to avoid precipitating angina/arrhythmias.
rationales.A: Liothyronine (T3) has a faster onset and higher risk of cardiac adverse effects; it is not routine first-line replacement, especially in CAD.
rationales.B: Methimazole treats hyperthyroidism, not hypothyroidism.
rationales.D: PTU treats hyperthyroidism, not hypothyroidism.
rationales.E: Dexamethasone does not replace thyroid hormone.

takeaway: Most hypothyroidism = levothyroxine. In CAD/elderly, start low and go slow.

title: COPD exacerbation
stem:
A 63-year-old man with COPD presents with increased dyspnea and sputum production.
O2 sat 91% on room air. He has no signs of pneumonia on chest X-ray.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to reduce airway inflammation during this acute COPD exacerbation?

rationales.A: Correct. A short course of systemic corticosteroid (e.g., prednisone) is standard in acute COPD exacerbations to reduce inflammation and shorten recovery.
rationales.B: Montelukast is used as an asthma controller and is not standard for acute COPD exacerbation management.
rationales.C: Intranasal steroids treat allergic rhinitis, not COPD exacerbations.
rationales.D: Roflumilast is used for chronic COPD management in select patients, not as an acute exacerbation steroid substitute.
rationales.E: Tiotropium is a maintenance bronchodilator and is not the primary acute anti-inflammatory therapy.

takeaway: Acute COPD exacerbation: bronchodilators + a short systemic steroid course (prednisone is classic).

title: MRSA pneumonia in a patient on an SSRI
stem:
A 55-year-old woman is hospitalized with severe community-acquired pneumonia. Sputum Gram stain and risk factors raise concern for MRSA.
PMH: depression.
Meds: sertraline 100 mg daily.
question: Which antibiotic is preferred for suspected MRSA pneumonia while minimizing the risk of serotonin syndrome?

rationales.B: Correct. Vancomycin is an appropriate MRSA pneumonia agent and avoids the MAOI-like interaction risk that linezolid has with SSRIs.
rationales.A: Linezolid can precipitate serotonin syndrome when combined with serotonergic agents (e.g., sertraline). It may still be used with close monitoring/temporary SSRI hold in select cases, but vancomycin is a safer choice here.
rationales.C: Ceftriaxone does not reliably cover MRSA.
rationales.D: Azithromycin does not reliably cover MRSA.
rationales.E: Doxycycline is not preferred as monotherapy for severe hospitalized MRSA pneumonia.

takeaway: Linezolid + SSRI/SNRI = serotonin syndrome risk. If MRSA coverage is needed, IV vancomycin is often the cleaner choice.

title: SVT termination
stem:
A 24-year-old woman presents with sudden palpitations and lightheadedness.
ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 190 bpm consistent with AVNRT.
She is stable (BP 112/70) and vagal maneuvers are unsuccessful.
question: Which medication is most appropriate for rapid termination of this arrhythmia?

rationales.A: Correct. Adenosine is given as a rapid IV push for acute termination of AVNRT due to transient AV nodal blockade and an extremely short half-life.
rationales.B: Amiodarone is typically used for ventricular arrhythmias or rhythm control strategies; it is not first-line for stable AVNRT termination.
rationales.C: Digoxin is not used for acute termination of AVNRT in the ED.
rationales.D: Warfarin is an anticoagulant and does not terminate SVT.
rationales.E: Ranolazine is an antianginal and does not terminate SVT.

takeaway: Stable AVNRT that fails vagal maneuvers → adenosine (rapid IV push).

title: Digoxin toxicity risk
stem:
A 79-year-old man with atrial fibrillation and HFrEF takes digoxin and furosemide.
He presents with nausea, weakness, and “yellow-green” vision changes.
Labs: K 3.1 mEq/L (low).
question: Which medication should be administered to help reduce the risk of digoxin-related ventricular arrhythmias in this patient?

rationales.A: Correct. Hypokalemia increases digoxin binding to Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase and raises arrhythmia risk. Potassium repletion (e.g., potassium chloride) is an important immediate step (along with holding digoxin and assessing severity).
rationales.B: Aspirin does not treat digoxin toxicity or correct potassium abnormalities.
rationales.C: Naloxone reverses opioid effects, not digoxin toxicity.
rationales.D: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, not digoxin toxicity.
rationales.E: Clopidogrel does not treat digoxin toxicity.

takeaway: Loop diuretics can cause hypokalemia → higher digoxin toxicity risk. Always check and correct K/Mg when digoxin toxicity is suspected.

title: Antidepressant to avoid in seizure disorder
stem:
A 32-year-old woman with a history of epilepsy (well-controlled on levetiracetam) presents with depressive symptoms.
She asks about starting bupropion because a friend said it “helps with energy.”
question: Which antidepressant option should generally be avoided because it can lower the seizure threshold?

rationales.A: Correct. Bupropion is associated with dose-dependent seizure risk and is generally avoided in patients with seizure disorders or eating disorders.
rationales.B: SSRIs are commonly used in depression and do not have the same seizure-threshold–lowering risk as bupropion.
rationales.C: Escitalopram is an SSRI and is not classically contraindicated in epilepsy (monitor as clinically appropriate).
rationales.D: SNRIs can be used with monitoring; they are not the classic 'avoid' agent for seizures compared with bupropion.
rationales.E: Mirtazapine is not the classic seizure-threshold–lowering antidepressant and can be used in many patients.

takeaway: High-yield contraindication: bupropion lowers seizure threshold—avoid in seizure disorder.

title: Procedural sedation reversal
stem:
A 40-year-old man receives midazolam for a brief endoscopy. After the procedure, he remains excessively sedated with shallow breathing.
He has no history of chronic benzodiazepine use and no seizure disorder.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to reverse the sedative effects?

rationales.B: Correct. Flumazenil is a benzodiazepine receptor antagonist that can reverse benzodiazepine-induced sedation in appropriate patients (not chronically dependent, no seizure risk).
rationales.A: Naloxone reverses opioid effects, not benzodiazepines.
rationales.C: Epinephrine is used for anaphylaxis, not sedation reversal.
rationales.D: Diphenhydramine can worsen sedation and does not reverse benzodiazepines.
rationales.E: Atropine treats symptomatic bradycardia and certain poisonings; it does not reverse benzodiazepines.

takeaway: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, but avoid in chronic benzo users or seizure disorders (can precipitate withdrawal/seizures).

title: Opioid overdose
stem:
A 29-year-old man is brought to the ED after being found unresponsive. He has pinpoint pupils and a respiratory rate of 6/min.
Friends report he took “pain pills.”
question: Which medication should be given immediately to reverse the life-threatening effect?

rationales.B: Correct. Naloxone is an opioid antagonist used to reverse opioid-induced respiratory depression.
rationales.A: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, not opioids.
rationales.C: Aprepitant is an antiemetic for chemotherapy-induced nausea, not overdose reversal.
rationales.D: Adenosine treats certain SVTs, not opioid toxicity.
rationales.E: Epinephrine is used for anaphylaxis/cardiac arrest, not first-line for opioid reversal.

takeaway: Classic triad of opioid overdose: CNS depression, respiratory depression, miosis → naloxone.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Chronic pain in CKD/HF
stem:
A 74-year-old woman with CKD stage 3 and heart failure has chronic osteoarthritis knee pain.
Meds: lisinopril, furosemide, carvedilol.
She asks what she can take over-the-counter for pain.
question: Which analgesic is the safest first-line option to recommend?

rationales.D: Correct. Acetaminophen is generally preferred for osteoarthritis pain when NSAIDs pose high risk (CKD, HF).
rationales.A: NSAIDs can worsen kidney function, increase BP, and cause fluid retention—problematic in CKD and HF.
rationales.B: Same NSAID risks (renal/GI/HF).
rationales.C: Indomethacin has higher CNS/GI adverse effect burden and still carries renal/HF risks.
rationales.E: Ketorolac has significant GI/renal toxicity risk and is inappropriate for chronic use.

takeaway: CKD/HF + pain: acetaminophen is usually the safest initial option; NSAIDs can worsen renal function and HF.

title: Acute gout flare with CKD + drug interaction
stem:
A 72-year-old man presents with acute podagra (hot, swollen, very painful first MTP joint).
PMH: CKD stage 4, atrial fibrillation.
Meds: apixaban and clarithromycin (started yesterday for bronchitis).
Renal function: eGFR 20 mL/min/1.73m².
question: Which medication is most appropriate to treat this acute gout flare?

rationales.E: Correct. Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone) are a good option for acute gout when NSAIDs are risky (CKD, anticoagulation) and colchicine is unsafe due to severe CKD and strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitor use (clarithromycin increases colchicine toxicity).
rationales.A: NSAIDs like indomethacin are avoided in advanced CKD and can increase bleeding risk with anticoagulation.
rationales.B: Same NSAID limitations (renal and bleeding risk).
rationales.C: Colchicine toxicity risk is increased in severe CKD and with clarithromycin (drug interaction), making it unsafe here.
rationales.D: Starting allopurinol during an acute attack is not the preferred immediate flare treatment; it can be initiated later with proper prophylaxis once the flare is controlled.

takeaway: Acute gout treatment depends on comorbidities: CKD/anticoagulation/drug interactions often push you toward short-course prednisone.

title: Chronic gout with CAD
stem:
A 64-year-old man has recurrent gout attacks and now has tophi.
PMH: myocardial infarction 2 years ago.
Uric acid: 9.8 mg/dL.
He is starting long-term urate-lowering therapy.
question: Which medication is preferred given his established coronary artery disease?

rationales.B: Correct. Allopurinol is a first-line urate-lowering therapy for chronic gout and is generally preferred over febuxostat in many patients with established cardiovascular disease.
rationales.A: Febuxostat is another xanthine oxidase inhibitor but has been associated with cardiovascular safety concerns; it is often reserved for patients who cannot tolerate allopurinol.
rationales.C: Indomethacin treats acute flares, not long-term urate lowering.
rationales.D: Colchicine helps prevent/treat flares but does not lower uric acid adequately as monotherapy.
rationales.E: Chronic systemic steroid monotherapy is not appropriate due to significant adverse effects.

takeaway: Long-term gout management aims to lower urate (allopurinol is classic first-line). Acute-flare meds are different from urate-lowering therapy.

title: DMARD teratogenicity
stem:
A 29-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis is well-controlled on methotrexate.
She plans to try to conceive in the next few months and asks which of her medications is unsafe in pregnancy.
question: Which medication should be discontinued before conception due to high teratogenic risk?

rationales.A: Correct. Methotrexate is highly teratogenic and must be stopped well before conception.
rationales.B: Hydroxychloroquine is commonly continued in pregnancy for autoimmune disease control and is not the classic 'must stop' teratogen.
rationales.C: Adalimumab may be used in pregnancy in select cases; it is not the classic 'must stop before conception' drug like methotrexate.
rationales.D: Short courses of prednisone may be used when needed; it is not the primary teratogenic concern compared with methotrexate.
rationales.E: Azathioprine is sometimes used during pregnancy for select indications; it is not the classic preconception 'stop immediately' medication like methotrexate.

takeaway: Pregnancy planning is a classic time to screen for teratogens—methotrexate is a major 'must stop' medication.

title: Transplant drug toxicity from azole interaction
stem:
A 50-year-old kidney transplant recipient takes tacrolimus.
He develops oral/esophageal candidiasis and is started on fluconazole.
Within a week he reports tremor and his creatinine rises. Tacrolimus trough level is markedly elevated.
question: Which medication most likely precipitated tacrolimus toxicity via a drug–drug interaction?

rationales.C: Correct. Azole antifungals (including fluconazole) inhibit CYP3A4/P-gp and can increase tacrolimus concentrations, leading to nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity.
rationales.A: Nystatin is minimally absorbed (topical/oral swish) and does not significantly affect tacrolimus levels.
rationales.B: Terbinafine has fewer CYP3A4 effects than azoles and is less likely to cause a large tacrolimus level increase.
rationales.D: Amoxicillin does not typically raise tacrolimus levels via CYP3A4 inhibition.
rationales.E: Valacyclovir does not significantly inhibit tacrolimus metabolism.

takeaway: Transplant high-yield: azoles (fluconazole/ketoconazole) can raise tacrolimus levels—monitor levels and adjust dose.

title: C. difficile colitis
stem:
A 64-year-old woman develops profuse watery diarrhea 5 days after finishing clindamycin for a dental infection.
Stool testing is positive for C. difficile toxin.
She is hemodynamically stable.
question: Which medication is most appropriate first-line therapy from the options below?

rationales.B: Correct. Oral vancomycin treats C. difficile in the gut lumen; the oral formulation is used for colitis.
rationales.A: IV vancomycin does not achieve adequate colonic concentrations for C. difficile colitis.
rationales.C: Ciprofloxacin is not used to treat C. difficile and can worsen antibiotic-associated diarrhea risk.
rationales.D: Antimotility agents like loperamide are generally avoided in untreated infectious colitis due to risk of worsening/toxic megacolon.
rationales.E: Amoxicillin/clavulanate is not used for C. difficile and is itself associated with diarrhea risk.

takeaway: C. difficile colitis: treat in the gut—oral vancomycin (not IV).

title: Asthma controller
stem:
A 19-year-old college student has asthma symptoms 4–5 days per week and uses her albuterol inhaler daily.
She has nighttime symptoms once weekly.
She has no history of COPD and no smoking history.
question: Which medication is the best next step as a daily controller to reduce symptoms and exacerbations?

rationales.B: Correct. Inhaled corticosteroids are first-line controller therapy for persistent asthma to reduce airway inflammation and exacerbations.
rationales.A: Albuterol is a rescue medication; daily use signals inadequate control and need for controller therapy.
rationales.C: LABA/LAMA combinations are primarily COPD therapies and are not first-line asthma monotherapy.
rationales.D: Tiotropium can be an add-on in some asthma patients but is not first-line compared with initiating an ICS.
rationales.E: Chronic daily systemic steroids have major long-term toxicity and are not appropriate as the initial controller step.

takeaway: Persistent asthma = controller therapy. Start with an inhaled corticosteroid when rescue inhaler use becomes frequent.

title: GERD in a patient on clopidogrel
stem:
A 62-year-old man with coronary stent is taking aspirin and clopidogrel.
He has daily GERD symptoms despite lifestyle measures. Endoscopy shows erosive esophagitis requiring a proton pump inhibitor.
question: Which acid-suppressing medication is preferred because it has less potential to reduce clopidogrel activation?

rationales.B: Correct. Among PPIs, pantoprazole is commonly preferred with clopidogrel because it has less CYP2C19 inhibition compared with omeprazole (which can reduce clopidogrel activation).
rationales.A: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 and can reduce conversion of clopidogrel to its active metabolite.
rationales.C: Famotidine can help GERD, but erosive esophagitis often requires a PPI for healing; the question asks for the preferred PPI option.
rationales.D: Sucralfate can help mucosal protection but is not the most effective therapy to heal erosive esophagitis compared with a PPI.
rationales.E: Lansoprazole is a PPI but pantoprazole is a common 'go-to' when clopidogrel is part of therapy.

takeaway: If a patient needs a PPI while on clopidogrel, pantoprazole is a common preferred option over omeprazole.

title: Motion sickness prevention
stem:
A 36-year-old woman is going on a 7-day cruise and has a strong history of motion sickness.
She asks for a medication that can prevent nausea before it starts.
question: Which medication is most appropriate for motion sickness prevention?

rationales.C: Correct. Scopolamine transdermal patch is effective for motion sickness prevention (vestibular-mediated nausea).
rationales.A: Ondansetron is effective for many nausea causes but is not the classic first-choice prophylaxis for motion sickness.
rationales.B: Metoclopramide is used for gastroparesis-related nausea and can cause extrapyramidal effects.
rationales.D: Aprepitant is mainly for chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting.
rationales.E: Pantoprazole treats acid-related disorders and does not prevent motion sickness.

takeaway: Motion sickness is vestibular—anticholinergics like scopolamine are high-yield for prevention.

title: Migraine in CAD/anticoagulated patient
stem:
A 60-year-old man has migraine headaches.
PMH: prior myocardial infarction; atrial fibrillation on apixaban.
He needs an acute migraine medication that will not cause coronary vasoconstriction and avoids NSAID bleeding risk.
question: Which medication is the best choice for acute migraine treatment in this patient?

rationales.D: Correct. Rimegepant (a CGRP receptor antagonist) treats acute migraine without the vasoconstriction of triptans and avoids NSAID-related bleeding risk.
rationales.A: Triptans (sumatriptan) cause vasoconstriction and are generally avoided in patients with CAD.
rationales.B: Rizatriptan is also a triptan and shares CAD contraindication concerns.
rationales.C: NSAIDs increase bleeding risk (especially with anticoagulation) and can be problematic in CAD/renal/GI risk patients.
rationales.E: Propranolol is typically used for migraine prevention, not acute abortive therapy.

takeaway: Triptans are vasoconstrictors—avoid in CAD. CGRP antagonists (e.g., rimegepant) are an important non-vasoconstrictive option.

title: Epilepsy in pregnancy
stem:
A 27-year-old woman with generalized epilepsy becomes pregnant (6 weeks gestation).
She is currently controlled on valproic acid and wants to minimize fetal risk.
question: Which antiepileptic is a preferred alternative from the options below due to a more favorable pregnancy safety profile?

rationales.D: Correct. Lamotrigine is often preferred in pregnancy compared with valproate due to lower risk of major congenital malformations (though levels may change and monitoring is needed).
rationales.A: Valproic acid has a high risk of neural tube defects and other teratogenic effects; it is generally avoided in pregnancy when alternatives exist.
rationales.B: Phenytoin is associated with fetal hydantoin syndrome and is not preferred if safer options exist.
rationales.C: Carbamazepine can increase neural tube defect risk and is not preferred over lamotrigine in many cases.
rationales.E: Topiramate has teratogenic risk (e.g., cleft lip/palate) and is not a preferred first alternative here.

takeaway: High-yield teratogen: valproate. Lamotrigine is commonly used as a safer alternative in pregnancy (with monitoring).

title: Mild Alzheimer disease
stem:
A 74-year-old man has progressive short-term memory loss over 18 months.
Neurocognitive testing is consistent with mild Alzheimer disease.
He is independent in basic activities of daily living.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to start to help with cognitive symptoms?

rationales.A: Correct. Donepezil (a cholinesterase inhibitor) is commonly used for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease to help cognitive symptoms.
rationales.B: Memantine is typically used in moderate-to-severe Alzheimer disease or as add-on therapy; it is not the classic first medication for mild disease.
rationales.C: Meclizine is used for vertigo/motion sickness and can worsen cognition due to anticholinergic effects.
rationales.D: Melatonin may help sleep but does not treat core cognitive symptoms of Alzheimer disease.
rationales.E: Topiramate can cause cognitive slowing and is not used to treat Alzheimer disease.

takeaway: Mild–moderate Alzheimer disease: cholinesterase inhibitor (donepezil) is a classic first option; avoid anticholinergics that worsen cognition.

title: BPH
stem:
A 62-year-old man has BPH with bothersome nocturia, weak stream, and hesitancy for 6 months.
Digital rectal exam suggests mildly enlarged prostate. PSA is normal.
He wants a medication that improves urinary symptoms quickly.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to start for faster symptom relief?

rationales.C: Correct. Alpha-1 blockers like tamsulosin relax smooth muscle in the bladder neck/prostate and typically improve symptoms within days to weeks.
rationales.A: Finasteride (5-alpha reductase inhibitor) can shrink the prostate but takes months to improve symptoms and is most helpful with larger prostates.
rationales.B: Dutasteride is similar to finasteride and also takes months for symptom improvement.
rationales.D: Oxybutynin treats overactive bladder; it can worsen urinary retention in BPH.
rationales.E: Mirabegron treats overactive bladder; it does not address bladder outlet obstruction and may not relieve weak stream/hesitancy.

takeaway: BPH symptom speed: alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin) work fast; 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride/dutasteride) work slowly by shrinking prostate.
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: Overactive bladder in a patient with cognitive concerns
stem:
A 76-year-old woman has overactive bladder with urgency and urge incontinence.
PMH: mild cognitive impairment; she is on donepezil.
Vitals: BP 126/74 mmHg.
She reports constipation and dry mouth easily with medications.
question: Which medication is the best choice to treat her overactive bladder while minimizing anticholinergic cognitive burden?

rationales.C: Correct. Mirabegron (a beta-3 agonist) treats overactive bladder without anticholinergic effects, making it preferable when cognitive adverse effects are a concern.
rationales.A: Oxybutynin is strongly anticholinergic and can worsen cognition, dry mouth, and constipation.
rationales.B: Solifenacin is also an antimuscarinic and can contribute to anticholinergic cognitive burden and constipation/dry mouth.
rationales.D: Tamsulosin treats BPH-related obstruction, not overactive bladder urgency incontinence in this scenario.
rationales.E: Finasteride treats BPH by shrinking prostate tissue and is not used for overactive bladder.

takeaway: Older adults/cognitive impairment: minimize anticholinergics. Mirabegron is a high-yield OAB alternative.
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: Osteoporosis
stem:
A 68-year-old woman is diagnosed with osteoporosis on DEXA scan.
She is started on a weekly oral bisphosphonate.
She has no esophageal motility disorders and can sit/stand upright.
question: Which medication below is the oral bisphosphonate that must be taken with plain water and the patient must remain upright afterward to reduce esophagitis risk?

rationales.C: Correct. Oral alendronate can cause esophagitis; it should be taken first thing in the morning with plain water, and the patient should remain upright for at least 30 minutes.
rationales.A: Denosumab is an injectable monoclonal antibody and does not have the same oral esophagitis administration issue.
rationales.B: Teriparatide is an injectable anabolic agent, not an oral bisphosphonate.
rationales.D: Estrogen therapy is not a bisphosphonate and carries different risks/benefits.
rationales.E: Vitamin D supports bone health but is not a bisphosphonate and does not cause esophagitis like oral bisphosphonates can.

takeaway: Oral bisphosphonates are effective but administration matters—upright positioning reduces esophageal irritation.

title: Open-angle glaucoma in asthma
stem:
A 58-year-old woman is diagnosed with primary open-angle glaucoma.
PMH: asthma requiring albuterol inhaler use several times per week.
question: Which medication is the best choice to lower intraocular pressure while avoiding bronchospasm risk?

rationales.B: Correct. Prostaglandin analogs (latanoprost/bimatoprost) are common first-line options for open-angle glaucoma and do not cause beta-blocker–related bronchospasm.
rationales.A: Ophthalmic timolol is a beta-blocker and can still cause systemic beta-blockade/bronchospasm in susceptible patients.
rationales.C: This combination contains timolol, so it carries the same asthma/bronchospasm concern.
rationales.D: Atropine is not used to lower intraocular pressure in open-angle glaucoma.
rationales.E: Brimonidine can lower IOP but latanoprost is a classic first-line option and avoids beta-blocker issues.

takeaway: Even eye drops can have systemic effects—avoid ophthalmic beta-blockers in asthma; prostaglandin analogs are a common first-line glaucoma choice.

title: Localized impetigo
stem:
A 7-year-old child has several small honey-crusted lesions around the nose.
He is afebrile and otherwise well. The lesions are localized.
question: Which medication is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

rationales.A: Correct. Topical mupirocin is commonly used for localized impetigo (often due to Staph aureus or Strep pyogenes).
rationales.B: Terbinafine is an antifungal and does not treat bacterial impetigo.
rationales.C: Acyclovir treats herpesvirus infections, not impetigo.
rationales.D: High-potency topical steroids can worsen infection and are not appropriate.
rationales.E: Topical steroids treat inflammatory dermatoses and can worsen infection.

takeaway: Honey-crusted localized impetigo → topical antibiotic (mupirocin).

title: Anaphylaxis
stem:
A 23-year-old woman with a peanut allergy accidentally eats a cookie containing peanuts.
Within minutes she develops wheezing, hives, and throat tightness.
question: Which medication should be administered immediately as first-line treatment?

rationales.B: Correct. Epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis and should be given immediately (IM) for airway/breathing/circulation compromise.
rationales.A: Antihistamines can help hives/itching but do not treat airway compromise or shock and are not first-line.
rationales.C: Albuterol can help bronchospasm but does not address systemic anaphylaxis and is not first-line.
rationales.D: Steroids have delayed onset and are adjunctive, not first-line.
rationales.E: H2 blockers are adjunctive for hives and are not first-line.

takeaway: Anaphylaxis = epinephrine first, then adjuncts (antihistamines, bronchodilators, steroids).

title: SSRI discontinuation symptoms
stem:
A 35-year-old woman stopped her antidepressant abruptly and now has dizziness, “brain zaps,” irritability, and flu-like symptoms.
The symptoms started 2 days after stopping the medication.
question: Which antidepressant is most associated with discontinuation symptoms due to a short half-life?

rationales.B: Correct. Paroxetine has a relatively short half-life among SSRIs and is classically associated with discontinuation syndrome if stopped abruptly.
rationales.A: Fluoxetine has a long half-life and is less likely to cause discontinuation symptoms.
rationales.C: Bupropion can cause withdrawal symptoms but SSRI discontinuation syndrome is most classically tied to short half-life SSRIs like paroxetine.
rationales.D: SNRIs can cause discontinuation symptoms, but paroxetine is a classic board-style answer among SSRIs.
rationales.E: Sertraline can cause discontinuation symptoms but generally less than paroxetine; the question is aiming for the most associated agent.

takeaway: Short half-life antidepressants (paroxetine; also venlafaxine) → higher discontinuation syndrome risk; taper rather than stop abruptly.

title: QT prolongation risk
stem:
A 48-year-old woman with a history of congenital long QT syndrome presents with nausea from viral gastroenteritis.
She requests an antiemetic.
question: Which antiemetic should be avoided because it can prolong the QT interval?

rationales.A: Correct. Ondansetron can prolong the QT interval and should be used cautiously/avoided in patients with congenital long QT or high arrhythmia risk.
rationales.B: Scopolamine does not have the same QT-prolonging risk profile as ondansetron (though it has anticholinergic effects).
rationales.C: Aprepitant is not a common QT-prolonging antiemetic; it is primarily used for chemotherapy-induced nausea.
rationales.D: Sucralfate is for mucosal protection in GI disorders and is not a QT-prolonging antiemetic.
rationales.E: Docusate is a stool softener and is unrelated to QT prolongation.

takeaway: High-yield adverse effect: ondansetron can prolong QT—think about EKG risk factors before choosing it.

title: Anticoagulation bridge around surgery
stem:
A 58-year-old man with a history of DVT is on chronic warfarin therapy.
He is scheduled for an elective surgery and will need temporary interruption of warfarin.
The team plans perioperative bridging for high thrombosis risk.
question: Which medication is commonly used for bridging anticoagulation when warfarin is held?

rationales.A: Correct. Low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin) is commonly used for bridging when warfarin is temporarily held in patients at high thrombosis risk.
rationales.B: Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet, not a warfarin bridge for VTE prevention.
rationales.C: Aspirin is not a substitute for anticoagulation in high-risk VTE scenarios.
rationales.D: Statins do not provide anticoagulation bridging.
rationales.E: Ezetimibe does not provide anticoagulation bridging.

takeaway: Warfarin interruption sometimes requires bridging—LMWH (enoxaparin) is a classic bridging agent.

title: Resistant hypertension
stem:
A 55-year-old man has hypertension despite adherence to:
- Lisinopril 20 mg daily
- Amlodipine 10 mg daily
Home BPs average 152/92 mmHg.
Labs: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, SCr 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR 95).
question: Which medication is the best next add-on agent from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Chlorthalidone is a thiazide-like diuretic with a longer duration of action and strong BP-lowering effect—commonly preferred as the thiazide choice when intensifying therapy for hypertension in patients with preserved kidney function.
rationales.B: Loop diuretics (furosemide) are typically reserved for significant edema/heart failure or advanced CKD; they are not the usual next step for uncomplicated resistant HTN with normal renal function.
rationales.C: Spironolactone can be excellent for resistant hypertension, but the typical step in triple therapy optimization is adding a thiazide-like diuretic first when renal function/potassium allow; spironolactone is often the next add-on after that.
rationales.D: Adding a second CCB (diltiazem) is not standard for resistant HTN and increases bradycardia/AV block risk when combined with other rate-lowering agents.
rationales.E: Metoprolol tartrate is not the preferred next add-on in uncomplicated HTN unless there is a compelling indication (CAD, arrhythmia, etc.).

takeaway: For uncontrolled HTN on ACEi/ARB + CCB, adding a thiazide-like diuretic (chlorthalidone) is a classic next step when kidney function is adequate.

title: Recurrent calcium kidney stones
stem:
A 44-year-old woman has recurrent calcium oxalate kidney stones despite increased hydration and dietary changes.
24-hour urine shows hypercalciuria.
PMH: hypertension.
Labs: SCr 0.8 mg/dL, K 4.0 mEq/L.
question: Which medication can help prevent recurrent calcium stones by reducing urinary calcium excretion?

rationales.A: Correct. Thiazide diuretics like hydrochlorothiazide reduce urinary calcium excretion and can help prevent recurrent calcium-containing stones.
rationales.B: Loop diuretics increase urinary calcium excretion and can worsen hypercalciuria.
rationales.C: Losartan lowers blood pressure but does not specifically reduce urinary calcium to prevent stones.
rationales.D: Amlodipine lowers blood pressure but does not address hypercalciuria.
rationales.E: Spironolactone is not used to prevent calcium stones and can raise potassium.

takeaway: Thiazides decrease urinary calcium—high-yield for recurrent calcium stone prevention (when appropriate).

title: HFrEF
stem:
A 61-year-old man has chronic HFrEF (EF 30%) and is stable without volume overload.
He is taking lisinopril and furosemide.
The cardiology team wants to start a beta-blocker with proven mortality benefit in HFrEF.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Metoprolol succinate (extended-release) is an evidence-based beta-blocker that improves survival in HFrEF (along with carvedilol and bisoprolol).
rationales.B: Metoprolol tartrate is not the guideline-preferred formulation for mortality benefit in HFrEF.
rationales.C: Atenolol is not one of the beta-blockers proven to improve survival in HFrEF.
rationales.D: Propranolol is nonselective and not used as an evidence-based HFrEF mortality therapy.
rationales.E: Diltiazem can worsen systolic HF due to negative inotropic effects.

takeaway: HFrEF has specific beta-blockers with outcome data—metoprolol succinate (not tartrate), carvedilol, and bisoprolol.

title: Asthma + beta-blockers
stem:
A 26-year-old woman with asthma needs medication for performance anxiety before public speaking.
She uses albuterol as needed and has had prior wheezing with certain medications.
question: Which medication is most likely to trigger bronchospasm due to nonselective beta-blockade?

rationales.A: Correct. Propranolol is a nonselective beta-blocker (blocks β1 and β2) and can precipitate bronchospasm in patients with asthma.
rationales.B: Atenolol is relatively β1-selective, making bronchospasm less likely than with nonselective agents (though caution is still warranted).
rationales.C: Metoprolol is relatively β1-selective and generally has lower bronchospasm risk than nonselective beta-blockers.
rationales.D: Bisoprolol is β1-selective and has lower bronchospasm risk than nonselective agents.
rationales.E: Diltiazem is not a beta-blocker.

takeaway: Nonselective beta-blockers (propranolol) can worsen asthma—high-yield contraindication/precaution.

title: AF rate control when beta-blockers are not ideal
stem:
A 68-year-old man with persistent atrial fibrillation needs rate control.
PMH: COPD with frequent albuterol use.
Echocardiogram: EF 60% (no HFrEF).
Vitals: BP 128/76 mmHg, HR 132 bpm (irregular).
question: Which medication is an appropriate rate-control option in this patient?

rationales.A: Correct. Diltiazem (a non-dihydropyridine CCB) slows AV nodal conduction and is commonly used for AF rate control when EF is preserved.
rationales.B: Nifedipine is a dihydropyridine CCB that does not provide AV nodal blockade for AF rate control.
rationales.C: Ranolazine is an antianginal and does not provide reliable AF rate control.
rationales.D: Flecainide is used for rhythm control (not primary rate control) and is generally reserved for patients without structural heart disease.
rationales.E: Sublingual nitroglycerin treats angina; it does not control AF rate.

takeaway: AF rate control: use AV nodal blockers (beta-blockers, diltiazem/verapamil, or digoxin). Dihydropyridine CCBs (nifedipine) don’t slow AV node.

title: Prinzmetal (vasospastic) angina
stem:
A 47-year-old woman has episodic chest pain at rest, often in the early morning.
ECG during pain shows transient ST-segment elevation that resolves when symptoms stop.
Coronary angiography shows no significant fixed stenosis.
question: Which medication is most appropriate for prevention of these episodes?

rationales.A: Correct. Vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina is treated with vasodilators—calcium channel blockers (like nifedipine) are a mainstay for prevention.
rationales.B: Beta-blockers are not first-line for vasospastic angina and can sometimes worsen coronary spasm in susceptible patients.
rationales.C: Same issue as other beta-blockers—does not address coronary vasospasm.
rationales.D: Digoxin does not treat coronary vasospasm.
rationales.E: Hydrochlorothiazide does not treat angina.

takeaway: Prinzmetal angina = coronary spasm → CCBs (nifedipine/amlodipine) and nitrates.

title: Chronic stable angina prophylaxis
stem:
A 62-year-old man has chronic stable angina with predictable exertional chest discomfort.
He already carries sublingual nitroglycerin for acute episodes but needs a medication to reduce attack frequency.
question: Which medication is most appropriate for long-term angina prophylaxis?

rationales.A: Correct. Isosorbide mononitrate is a long-acting nitrate used to prevent angina episodes (prophylaxis).
rationales.B: Sublingual nitroglycerin is used for rapid relief of acute angina, not daily prophylaxis.
rationales.C: Aspirin is important for secondary prevention of ASCVD events but does not directly prevent angina episodes the same way long-acting antianginals do.
rationales.D: Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet agent used for thrombosis prevention, not angina prophylaxis.
rationales.E: Adenosine is used for acute termination of certain SVTs, not angina prophylaxis.

takeaway: Angina meds: SL nitro = acute relief; long-acting nitrates (isosorbide mono/di) = prophylaxis.

title: Hypertensive pulmonary edema with ischemic chest pain
stem:
A 71-year-old man presents with severe dyspnea and chest pressure.
Exam: diffuse crackles, accessory muscle use.
Vitals: BP 198/110 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, O2 sat 86% RA.
ECG shows ischemic changes.
question: Which medication can rapidly reduce preload/afterload and improve pulmonary edema and ischemic chest pain?

rationales.A: Correct. IV nitroglycerin provides rapid titratable venodilation (and at higher doses arterial dilation), helping relieve pulmonary edema and ischemic chest pain in hypertensive acute heart failure syndromes.
rationales.B: Sublingual nitroglycerin can help acute angina, but IV nitroglycerin is preferred for ongoing titration in severe hypertensive pulmonary edema.
rationales.C: Isosorbide dinitrate is longer-acting and not as titratable for an acute unstable presentation.
rationales.D: Ranolazine is for chronic angina and is not used as an acute decompensation rescue therapy.
rationales.E: Warfarin is an anticoagulant and will not acutely relieve pulmonary edema or ischemia.

takeaway: When you need rapid, titratable vasodilation in acute pulmonary edema with hypertension/ischemia, think IV nitroglycerin.

title: Very high LDL despite statin + ezetimibe
stem:
A 49-year-old man with heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia has ASCVD.
He is taking rosuvastatin 40 mg nightly and ezetimibe 10 mg daily.
Despite adherence, LDL-C remains 168 mg/dL.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to add next to achieve further LDL reduction?

rationales.A: Correct. PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab/alirocumab) provide large additional LDL reductions and are used when LDL remains high despite maximally tolerated statin + ezetimibe, especially in ASCVD/familial hypercholesterolemia.
rationales.B: Fenofibrate primarily lowers triglycerides and has modest LDL effects; it is not the best next add-on for markedly elevated LDL.
rationales.C: Icosapent ethyl targets triglycerides and cardiovascular risk in certain patients; it is not primarily used to lower LDL substantially.
rationales.D: Gemfibrozil lowers triglycerides and increases statin myopathy risk; not a preferred LDL-lowering add-on.
rationales.E: Ranolazine is an antianginal and does not lower LDL.

takeaway: If LDL remains very high on high-intensity statin + ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors are the next major LDL-lowering lever.
module_tags: Module 2b Non-Statin Lipids (Ezetimibe, PCSK9, Fibrates, Omega-3)

title: ASCVD + persistent triglycerides
stem:
A 67-year-old man with coronary artery disease is on atorvastatin 80 mg nightly.
Recent labs: LDL-C 58 mg/dL, triglycerides 240 mg/dL (fasting), A1c 7.3%.
He is already following diet and exercise recommendations.
question: Which medication can be added to reduce cardiovascular risk in an ASCVD patient with persistently elevated triglycerides?

rationales.A: Correct. Icosapent ethyl (EPA) is used in select high-risk patients (e.g., ASCVD) with elevated triglycerides despite statin therapy to reduce cardiovascular events.
rationales.B: Gemfibrozil lowers triglycerides but has higher interaction/myopathy risk with statins and is not the classic first add-on for CV event reduction in this scenario.
rationales.C: Ezetimibe primarily lowers LDL; his LDL is already well controlled.
rationales.D: PCSK9 inhibitors lower LDL substantially; his LDL is already at goal range.
rationales.E: Hydrochlorothiazide is an antihypertensive diuretic and does not reduce triglyceride-driven CV risk.

takeaway: When LDL is controlled on statin but triglycerides remain elevated in ASCVD, icosapent ethyl is a high-yield CV risk–reduction add-on.
module_tags: Module 2b Non-Statin Lipids (Ezetimibe, PCSK9, Fibrates, Omega-3)

title: Fibrate choice with statin
stem:
A 58-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and mixed dyslipidemia is taking atorvastatin 40 mg nightly.
Triglycerides remain 520 mg/dL despite lifestyle changes.
The clinician wants to add a fibrate while minimizing statin-associated myopathy risk.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Fenofibrate is generally preferred over gemfibrozil when combined with a statin because it has a lower risk of severe statin-associated myopathy/rhabdomyolysis.
rationales.B: Gemfibrozil significantly increases statin myopathy risk and is typically avoided with statins when alternatives exist.
rationales.C: Ezetimibe primarily lowers LDL and will not sufficiently lower very high triglycerides to reduce pancreatitis risk.
rationales.D: PCSK9 inhibitors lower LDL, not triglycerides to the needed degree here.
rationales.E: Switching statins does not address the need for substantial triglyceride lowering.

takeaway: If a fibrate is needed with a statin, fenofibrate is usually the safer pairing than gemfibrozil.
module_tags: Module 2b Non-Statin Lipids (Ezetimibe, PCSK9, Fibrates, Omega-3)

title: Warfarin interaction
stem:
A 73-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation has stable INRs around 2.3.
He is started on an antibiotic for suspected diverticulitis with anaerobic coverage.
Three days later his INR is 5.1.
question: Which medication most likely caused the INR increase?

rationales.A: Correct. Metronidazole can inhibit warfarin metabolism and substantially increase INR/bleeding risk.
rationales.B: Amoxicillin may rarely affect INR, but it is less classically associated with large INR elevations than metronidazole.
rationales.C: Cephalexin can sometimes alter gut flora, but metronidazole is a classic high-yield interaction that increases INR.
rationales.D: Nitrofurantoin has relatively minimal interaction with warfarin compared with many other antibiotics.
rationales.E: Nystatin is minimally absorbed and does not meaningfully affect warfarin metabolism.

takeaway: Warfarin has many antibiotic interactions—metronidazole is a classic 'INR goes up' culprit.

title: QT prolongation & torsades risk
stem:
A 62-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation is being considered for antiarrhythmic therapy.
Baseline ECG shows QTc 520 ms.
K is 4.1 mEq/L, Mg is 2.0 mg/dL.
question: Which medication is most likely to worsen QT prolongation and precipitate torsades de pointes?

rationales.A: Correct. Sotalol has class III antiarrhythmic effects that prolong repolarization (QT interval), increasing torsades risk—especially when baseline QTc is already prolonged.
rationales.B: Flecainide primarily widens QRS via sodium channel blockade; it is not the classic torsades/QT-prolongation culprit compared with sotalol.
rationales.C: Dronedarone can affect QT but torsades risk is most classically highlighted with QT-prolonging class III agents like sotalol (and others not in this list).
rationales.D: Digoxin does not typically cause torsades via QT prolongation; its toxicity is more associated with various arrhythmias especially with electrolyte disturbances.
rationales.E: Metoprolol does not prolong QT in the classic torsades way.

takeaway: Sotalol is a high-yield QT-prolonger → torsades risk. Always think baseline QT and electrolytes before starting.

title: Structural heart disease
stem:
A 69-year-old man has symptomatic paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and wants rhythm control.
PMH: prior myocardial infarction with coronary stent.
Echo: EF 50%.
question: Which medication should be avoided due to increased proarrhythmic risk in patients with coronary artery disease/structural heart disease?

rationales.A: Correct. Flecainide (class Ic) is generally avoided in patients with structural heart disease (including CAD/prior MI) due to proarrhythmic risk.
rationales.B: Amiodarone is often used when structural heart disease limits other rhythm-control options (though it has notable long-term toxicities).
rationales.C: Sotalol may be used in selected patients but requires QT monitoring; it is not the classic 'contraindicated in CAD' agent in the way flecainide is.
rationales.D: Dronedarone has specific contraindications (notably symptomatic HF), but the vignette’s key red flag for the listed drugs is flecainide with prior MI.
rationales.E: Metoprolol is used for rate control and is not contraindicated by CAD.

takeaway: Class Ic agents (flecainide) are for patients WITHOUT structural heart disease—prior MI/CAD is a classic 'do not use' flag.

title: Antiarrhythmic contraindication in heart failure
stem:
A 72-year-old woman with persistent atrial fibrillation is being evaluated for rhythm control.
PMH: HFrEF (EF 25%) with a hospitalization for decompensated HF 3 weeks ago.
question: Which medication is contraindicated in this patient?

rationales.A: Correct. Dronedarone is contraindicated in patients with symptomatic heart failure or recent decompensation due to increased risk of adverse outcomes.
rationales.B: Amiodarone may be used for rhythm control in patients with HFrEF when other agents are unsuitable, despite long-term toxicity concerns.
rationales.C: Digoxin can be used in HFrEF and AF for rate control (especially at rest) though it requires careful monitoring.
rationales.D: Metoprolol succinate is an evidence-based beta-blocker that improves outcomes in HFrEF when tolerated.
rationales.E: Warfarin can be used for stroke prevention in AF (and is not contraindicated by HFrEF).

takeaway: Dronedarone is a classic 'avoid' in symptomatic/recently decompensated HF.

title: Mealtime glucose coverage
stem:
A 33-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes uses basal-bolus insulin.
She wants a mealtime insulin that starts working quickly to cover carbohydrates eaten at meals.
question: Which insulin is the best choice for rapid prandial coverage?

rationales.A: Correct. Insulin lispro is a rapid-acting insulin analog used for mealtime (prandial) coverage.
rationales.B: Insulin glargine is long-acting basal insulin, not for immediate mealtime spikes.
rationales.C: Insulin detemir is also long-acting basal insulin.
rationales.D: Regular insulin is short-acting and can be used at meals, but it has a slower onset than rapid-acting analogs and typically must be taken earlier before eating.
rationales.E: Metformin is not insulin and is not used in type 1 diabetes as the primary prandial agent.

takeaway: Rapid-acting insulins (lispro/aspart) are designed for meals; glargine/detemir are basal.

title: Basal glucose control
stem:
A 52-year-old man with type 2 diabetes is starting basal insulin to improve fasting blood glucose.
He prefers an insulin with a relatively flat 24-hour profile.
question: Which medication is the best basal insulin choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Insulin glargine is a long-acting basal insulin commonly dosed once daily.
rationales.B: Insulin lispro is rapid-acting (mealtime).
rationales.C: Insulin aspart is rapid-acting (mealtime).
rationales.D: Regular insulin is short-acting and not a flat 24-hour basal insulin.
rationales.E: Glipizide is a sulfonylurea, not basal insulin.

takeaway: Basal insulin = glargine or detemir; prandial insulin = lispro/aspart/regular.

title: DKA
stem:
A 21-year-old man with type 1 diabetes presents with abdominal pain, vomiting, and Kussmaul respirations.
Labs: glucose 520 mg/dL, anion gap 28, serum ketones positive, pH 7.12.
question: Which insulin is used as a continuous IV infusion for treatment of diabetic ketoacidosis?

rationales.A: Correct. Regular insulin is used for IV insulin infusions in DKA due to predictable pharmacokinetics and established protocols.
rationales.B: Glargine is a basal insulin and is not used alone as an IV infusion for acute DKA correction.
rationales.C: Detemir is also basal insulin and not used as the primary IV infusion insulin in DKA.
rationales.D: Rapid-acting analogs are generally used subcutaneously; standard DKA protocols use IV regular insulin.
rationales.E: Sitagliptin is an oral diabetes agent and is not used for DKA management.

takeaway: DKA is an emergency—IV regular insulin is the classic infusion insulin (along with fluids and careful electrolyte management).

title: Thiazolidinedione fluid retention risk
stem:
A 65-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (A1c 7.9%) is being considered for an additional oral agent.
PMH: heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 30%) and chronic lower extremity edema.
question: Which medication should generally be avoided because it can cause fluid retention and worsen heart failure?

rationales.A: Correct. Pioglitazone (a TZD) can cause edema and fluid retention and may worsen heart failure.
rationales.B: Empagliflozin has heart failure benefits and is often preferred in patients with diabetes and HF.
rationales.C: Metformin is commonly used in T2DM (avoid/hold in severe renal dysfunction or acute illness).
rationales.D: Sitagliptin is generally weight neutral and does not cause the same fluid retention as TZDs.
rationales.E: Semaglutide can aid weight loss and glycemic control; it does not cause HF fluid retention like pioglitazone.

takeaway: TZDs (pioglitazone) can worsen edema/HF—avoid in symptomatic heart failure.

title: Adrenal crisis
stem:
A 38-year-old woman with known primary adrenal insufficiency presents with vomiting, severe weakness, and hypotension.
Vitals: BP 82/50 mmHg, HR 118 bpm.
Labs: Na 126 mEq/L, K 5.8 mEq/L, glucose 58 mg/dL.
question: Which medication should be administered urgently?

rationales.A: Correct. Hydrocortisone is commonly used for adrenal crisis because it provides glucocorticoid activity (and some mineralocorticoid effect) and is standard in stress-dose treatment.
rationales.B: Prednisone can provide glucocorticoid effect, but hydrocortisone is the classic acute stress-dose agent in adrenal crisis scenarios.
rationales.C: Dexamethasone provides glucocorticoid effect but lacks mineralocorticoid activity; hydrocortisone is preferred in primary adrenal crisis.
rationales.D: Inhaled fluticasone does not treat systemic adrenal crisis.
rationales.E: Levothyroxine treats hypothyroidism and is not the immediate life-saving therapy here.

takeaway: Adrenal crisis is life-threatening: give stress-dose steroids—hydrocortisone is classic.

title: Croup
stem:
A 3-year-old child presents with barking cough, hoarseness, and inspiratory stridor consistent with viral croup.
Oxygen saturation is 96% on room air.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to reduce upper-airway inflammation and improve symptoms?

rationales.A: Correct. Dexamethasone is commonly used (often as a single dose) for croup to reduce airway inflammation and improve symptoms.
rationales.B: Prednisone is a systemic steroid, but dexamethasone is the classic first choice for croup due to longer duration and common dosing strategies.
rationales.C: Inhaled budesonide may be used in some cases, but dexamethasone is the classic first-line systemic therapy from these options.
rationales.D: Albuterol treats lower-airway bronchospasm and does not treat the upper-airway edema of croup.
rationales.E: Montelukast is an asthma controller and does not acutely treat croup.

takeaway: Croup is upper-airway inflammation—dexamethasone is a classic treatment.

title: Depression with neuropathic pain
stem:
A 50-year-old woman has major depressive disorder and painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy.
She wants one medication that can help both mood and neuropathic pain.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Duloxetine (an SNRI) is commonly used for depression and has an indication/benefit for neuropathic pain (e.g., diabetic neuropathy).
rationales.B: Fluoxetine treats depression but does not specifically treat neuropathic pain the way SNRIs do.
rationales.C: Citalopram treats depression but is not a go-to for neuropathic pain.
rationales.D: Bupropion can help depression and energy but is not a primary neuropathic pain agent.
rationales.E: Risperidone is an antipsychotic and is not first-line for depression with neuropathic pain.

takeaway: SNRIs (duloxetine) are high-yield when depression and neuropathic pain coexist.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Antidepressant discontinuation
stem:
A 29-year-old man abruptly stops his antidepressant and develops dizziness, nausea, “brain zaps,” and irritability within 24–48 hours.
The medication has a short half-life and is known for prominent discontinuation symptoms.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible?

rationales.A: Correct. Venlafaxine has a relatively short half-life and is classically associated with significant discontinuation symptoms if stopped abruptly.
rationales.B: Fluoxetine has a long half-life and is less likely to cause abrupt discontinuation syndrome.
rationales.C: Sertraline can cause discontinuation symptoms, but venlafaxine is more classically associated with prominent symptoms.
rationales.D: Bupropion is not an SSRI/SNRI and is not the classic 'brain zaps' discontinuation syndrome agent.
rationales.E: Mirtazapine can cause withdrawal symptoms but venlafaxine is the classic high-yield answer.

takeaway: Short half-life agents (venlafaxine, paroxetine) → higher discontinuation syndrome risk; taper instead of stopping abruptly.

title: Most anticholinergic/cardiotoxic antidepressants
stem:
A 22-year-old is brought to the ED after a suspected medication overdose.
He is tachycardic with dry mucous membranes and has a widened QRS on ECG.
The clinician suspects a tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose.
question: Which medication is most consistent with this toxidrome?

rationales.A: Correct. TCAs such as amitriptyline can cause anticholinergic toxicity and cardiotoxicity (QRS widening) in overdose.
rationales.B: SSRIs can cause serotonin toxicity but are less associated with the classic anticholinergic + QRS widening pattern of TCAs.
rationales.C: Escitalopram is an SSRI; overdose may cause QT issues but the classic TCA toxidrome is more consistent with amitriptyline.
rationales.D: Bupropion overdose is more associated with seizures and tachycardia rather than the classic anticholinergic/QRS widening TCA pattern.
rationales.E: Buspirone is not associated with TCA-like cardiotoxicity.

takeaway: TCAs (amitriptyline, nortriptyline, doxepin) are high-yield for anticholinergic + cardiac toxicity in overdose.
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: Lithium toxicity precipitated by diuretic
stem:
A 46-year-old woman with bipolar disorder is stable on lithium.
Her PCP starts a new blood pressure medication.
One week later she develops coarse tremor, vomiting, and confusion.
Lithium level is elevated.
question: Which new medication most likely caused the lithium level to rise?

rationales.A: Correct. Thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide) decrease renal lithium clearance and can precipitate lithium toxicity.
rationales.B: Amlodipine does not classically raise lithium levels.
rationales.C: Beta-blockers do not typically cause lithium accumulation (though they may be used to treat tremor).
rationales.D: ARBs can affect renal handling of lithium in some cases, but thiazides are the most classic and high-yield cause of lithium level increases.
rationales.E: Ezetimibe does not affect lithium clearance.

takeaway: Lithium has narrow therapeutic index—thiazides are a classic trigger for lithium toxicity.
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: Generalized anxiety
stem:
A 30-year-old woman has generalized anxiety disorder.
She wants a medication that is not habit-forming and does not cause immediate sedation.
question: Which medication is the best option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Buspirone is a non-benzodiazepine anxiolytic used for GAD; it is non-addictive but takes time to work.
rationales.B: Alprazolam is a benzodiazepine with dependence risk and is not the preferred long-term non-addictive option.
rationales.C: Diazepam is a benzodiazepine with dependence/sedation risk.
rationales.D: Zolpidem is for insomnia, not first-line long-term anxiety control, and has abuse/behavioral risks.
rationales.E: Hydroxyzine can help anxiety but is sedating and anticholinergic—less aligned with her goal to avoid sedation.

takeaway: Buspirone is a high-yield non-benzodiazepine option for GAD (non-addictive, delayed onset).
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: Alcohol withdrawal
stem:
A 52-year-old man with heavy daily alcohol use is admitted with tremor, agitation, diaphoresis, and tachycardia.
He is at risk for alcohol withdrawal seizures.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to treat alcohol withdrawal symptoms and prevent seizures?

rationales.A: Correct. Benzodiazepines are first-line for alcohol withdrawal; diazepam is long-acting and commonly used to prevent withdrawal seizures and delirium tremens.
rationales.B: Buspirone does not treat acute alcohol withdrawal and does not prevent withdrawal seizures.
rationales.C: Zolpidem is not appropriate for alcohol withdrawal seizure prevention.
rationales.D: Hydroxyzine may sedate but does not treat withdrawal physiology or prevent seizures.
rationales.E: Haloperidol may treat agitation/hallucinations but does not prevent withdrawal seizures and can lower seizure threshold.

takeaway: Alcohol withdrawal = benzodiazepines. Diazepam (and lorazepam) are high-yield first-line agents.

title: Convulsive status epilepticus
stem:
A 27-year-old man has continuous generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity for >5 minutes.
IV access is obtained in the ED.
question: Which medication is first-line to stop the seizure acutely?

rationales.A: Correct. IV benzodiazepines are first-line for acute convulsive status epilepticus; lorazepam is commonly used due to effective CNS duration.
rationales.B: Phenytoin is often used as a second-line agent after benzodiazepines for ongoing seizure control.
rationales.C: Levetiracetam can be used as a second-line agent but is not first-line ahead of IV benzodiazepines.
rationales.D: Valproic acid may be used after benzodiazepines, not first-line for immediate termination.
rationales.E: Carbamazepine is not used to acutely stop status epilepticus.

takeaway: Status epilepticus: first stop the seizure (IV lorazepam), then load a longer-acting agent if needed.

title: Orexin antagonist contraindication
