stem: A 34-year-old man with narcolepsy also struggles with insomnia and asks about a newer sleep medication he saw advertised.
question: Which medication should be avoided because it is an orexin receptor antagonist and is contraindicated in narcolepsy?

rationales.A: Correct. Suvorexant is an orexin receptor antagonist; because narcolepsy involves orexin pathway dysfunction, suvorexant is contraindicated.
rationales.B: Zolpidem is a non-benzodiazepine hypnotic; it is not specifically contraindicated in narcolepsy in the same way as suvorexant (though it has other risks).
rationales.C: Melatonin can be used for circadian-related sleep issues and is not contraindicated due to orexin effects.
rationales.D: Trazodone is sometimes used off-label for sleep and does not have the orexin antagonism contraindication.
rationales.E: Hydroxyzine is sedating but is not an orexin antagonist.

takeaway: Suvorexant blocks orexin—avoid in narcolepsy.

title: ADHD with tics
stem:
An 11-year-old boy has ADHD with significant hyperactivity and impulsivity.
He also has a chronic motor tic disorder. Family prefers to avoid medications that worsen tics or cause appetite suppression.
question: Which medication is a reasonable non-stimulant option that can help ADHD symptoms and may help with tics?

rationales.A: Correct. Guanfacine ER (an alpha-2 agonist) is a non-stimulant ADHD option that can help with hyperactivity/impulsivity and may also be beneficial for tics.
rationales.B: Stimulants can be effective but may worsen tics in some patients and commonly suppress appetite.
rationales.C: Same stimulant concerns (tics/appetite).
rationales.D: Methylphenidate is a stimulant and may worsen tics/appetite issues in some patients.
rationales.E: Dexmethylphenidate is a stimulant and carries similar concerns.

takeaway: Alpha-2 agonists (guanfacine ER) are high-yield non-stimulant ADHD options—especially when tics or appetite issues are concerns.

title: Adult ADHD with substance use history
stem:
A 24-year-old man has ADHD and a history of stimulant misuse.
He wants an ADHD medication with no stimulant properties.
question: Which medication is most appropriate?

rationales.A: Correct. Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant (norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor) used for ADHD and is often considered when stimulant misuse risk is a concern.
rationales.B: Lisdexamfetamine is a stimulant (prodrug) and is not ideal for someone with stimulant misuse history.
rationales.C: Amphetamine/dextroamphetamine is a stimulant and not ideal in misuse risk.
rationales.D: Methylphenidate is a stimulant.
rationales.E: Dexmethylphenidate is a stimulant.

takeaway: Atomoxetine is a key non-stimulant ADHD option when stimulant misuse risk is high.

title: Stimulant prodrug
stem:
A clinician is comparing stimulant options for ADHD.
They want a medication that is a prodrug (activated after ingestion), which can reduce misuse potential via snorting or injection.
question: Which medication fits this description?

rationales.A: Correct. Lisdexamfetamine is a prodrug converted to dextroamphetamine after ingestion, which can reduce certain misuse routes compared with immediate active drugs.
rationales.B: Methylphenidate is active as administered (not a prodrug).
rationales.C: Dexmethylphenidate is active as administered.
rationales.D: Mixed amphetamine salts are active as administered.
rationales.E: Atomoxetine is non-stimulant and not a stimulant prodrug.

takeaway: Lisdexamfetamine is a prodrug stimulant—high-yield differentiation among ADHD meds.

title: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia
stem:
A 38-year-old man with schizophrenia has persistent hallucinations and delusions despite adequate trials of risperidone and olanzapine.
He also has recurrent suicidal behavior.
question: Which medication is most appropriate next?

rationales.A: Correct. Clozapine is indicated for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and has evidence for reducing suicidal behavior, but requires monitoring for agranulocytosis and other adverse effects.
rationales.B: Aripiprazole can treat schizophrenia but is not the classic next step after failure of multiple adequate antipsychotic trials compared with clozapine.
rationales.C: Haloperidol is effective for psychosis but clozapine is the high-yield choice for treatment-resistant cases and suicidality.
rationales.D: Quetiapine can treat psychosis, but clozapine is the key medication for treatment-resistant schizophrenia.
rationales.E: He already failed risperidone.

takeaway: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia (failed ≥2 antipsychotics) → clozapine (with ANC monitoring).

title: Antipsychotic adverse effect
stem:
A 27-year-old woman started an antipsychotic 2 months ago and now reports galactorrhea and amenorrhea.
A pregnancy test is negative. Prolactin level is elevated.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible?

rationales.A: Correct. Risperidone is strongly associated with hyperprolactinemia, leading to galactorrhea, amenorrhea, and sexual dysfunction.
rationales.B: Aripiprazole is a partial dopamine agonist and tends to cause less prolactin elevation; it can even lower prolactin in some cases.
rationales.C: Quetiapine generally has lower prolactin elevation risk than risperidone.
rationales.D: Clozapine is not typically associated with marked prolactin elevation (but has other serious adverse effects).
rationales.E: Olanzapine can cause metabolic effects; prolactin elevation is generally less prominent than with risperidone.

takeaway: Risperidone = high-yield prolactin elevation (galactorrhea/amenorrhea).

title: Antipsychotic with lower metabolic risk
stem:
A 42-year-old man with schizophrenia has obesity and prediabetes.
The team wants an antipsychotic option with lower risk of weight gain and metabolic syndrome.
question: Which medication best fits this goal from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Aripiprazole is generally associated with a more favorable metabolic profile compared with several other atypical antipsychotics.
rationales.B: Olanzapine has high risk of weight gain and metabolic syndrome.
rationales.C: Clozapine also carries high metabolic risk (and other serious adverse effects).
rationales.D: Quetiapine can cause weight gain and metabolic issues.
rationales.E: Risperidone can cause weight gain and prolactin elevation; metabolic risk is generally higher than aripiprazole.

takeaway: Metabolic side effects vary by antipsychotic: olanzapine/clozapine are high-risk; aripiprazole is often lower-risk.

title: Antipsychotic most linked to weight gain
stem:
A 31-year-old woman starts an antipsychotic and gains 18 pounds over 3 months.
Her fasting glucose and triglycerides increase significantly.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible?

rationales.A: Correct. Olanzapine is strongly associated with weight gain, dyslipidemia, and hyperglycemia (metabolic syndrome).
rationales.B: Haloperidol has higher EPS risk; metabolic effects are generally less dramatic than olanzapine.
rationales.C: Aripiprazole tends to have lower metabolic risk compared with olanzapine.
rationales.D: Risperidone can cause weight gain, but olanzapine is among the highest-risk agents.
rationales.E: Buspirone is not an antipsychotic and does not cause this metabolic syndrome pattern.

takeaway: Olanzapine is a classic high-metabolic-risk antipsychotic—monitor weight, glucose, and lipids.

title: Acute agitation in delirium
stem:
A 79-year-old hospitalized man becomes acutely agitated and is pulling at IV lines.
Workup suggests delirium due to a UTI; he is threatening staff and is unsafe.
Nonpharmacologic interventions fail.
question: Which medication is commonly used for short-term control of severe agitation in delirium?

rationales.A: Correct. Haloperidol is commonly used for short-term management of severe agitation in delirium when the patient is unsafe (monitor for QT prolongation and EPS).
rationales.B: Zolpidem can worsen confusion and is not used for delirium agitation control.
rationales.C: Benzodiazepines can worsen delirium in many cases (exception: alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal).
rationales.D: Diphenhydramine is anticholinergic and can worsen delirium.
rationales.E: Donepezil is for chronic cognitive symptoms in Alzheimer disease, not acute delirium agitation.

takeaway: Delirium agitation: avoid anticholinergics and most benzos; haloperidol is a classic short-term option when safety is at risk.

title: Bipolar depression
stem:
A 36-year-old woman with bipolar disorder presents with a depressive episode and significant insomnia.
She asks about an antipsychotic that can help mood symptoms and sleep.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Quetiapine is commonly used in bipolar disorder and is sedating, which may help insomnia in bipolar depression (monitor metabolic effects and sedation).
rationales.B: Haloperidol is used for acute psychosis/agitation and has higher EPS risk; it is not the typical go-to for bipolar depression with insomnia.
rationales.C: Aripiprazole is less sedating and is not the classic single best choice here.
rationales.D: Buspirone treats anxiety, not bipolar depression.
rationales.E: Hydroxyzine can sedate but does not treat bipolar depression.

takeaway: Quetiapine is a high-yield antipsychotic option in bipolar disorder, especially when sedation can be helpful.

title: Transdermal opioid safety
stem:
A 58-year-old man with chronic back pain requests a fentanyl transdermal patch because he heard it lasts several days.
He is opioid-naïve and has only used acetaminophen intermittently.
question: Which medication should NOT be started due to high risk of fatal respiratory depression in opioid-naïve patients?

rationales.A: Correct. Transdermal fentanyl is indicated only for opioid-tolerant patients; starting it in opioid-naïve patients can cause life-threatening respiratory depression.
rationales.B: Acetaminophen is appropriate for mild pain and is not associated with opioid-naïve respiratory depression risk.
rationales.C: Ibuprofen can be used for inflammatory pain if no contraindications exist (renal/GI risks).
rationales.D: Lidocaine patch is a non-opioid topical analgesic option for localized pain/neuropathic pain.
rationales.E: Naproxen is an NSAID option (with GI/renal/CV precautions).

takeaway: High-yield safety rule: fentanyl patches are for opioid-tolerant patients only.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Weak opioid seizure and serotonin risk
stem:
A 40-year-old woman with chronic pain asks for tramadol because she wants to avoid “strong opioids.”
PMH: epilepsy (controlled) and depression treated with sertraline.
question: Which medication should be avoided because it can lower seizure threshold and increase serotonin syndrome risk with sertraline?

rationales.A: Correct. Tramadol can lower seizure threshold and has serotonergic activity—raising serotonin syndrome risk when combined with SSRIs like sertraline.
rationales.B: Acetaminophen does not lower seizure threshold and has no serotonergic effect.
rationales.C: Lidocaine patch is topical and does not cause serotonin syndrome.
rationales.D: Ibuprofen is not serotonergic and does not lower seizure threshold in the classic way tramadol does (though it has GI/renal risks).
rationales.E: Cyclobenzaprine is sedating and anticholinergic-like; it is not the classic seizure/serotonin syndrome interaction drug in this vignette.

takeaway: Tramadol is not “benign”—remember seizure risk and serotonergic interactions.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: High GI-risk osteoarthritis
stem:
A 66-year-old woman with knee osteoarthritis needs an NSAID for pain and inflammation.
PMH: prior peptic ulcer bleed 3 years ago (now healed). No CKD and no heart failure.
She is not on anticoagulation.
question: Which NSAID option below has a lower GI ulcer risk compared with nonselective NSAIDs?

rationales.A: Correct. Celecoxib is COX-2 selective and is associated with lower GI ulcer risk than nonselective NSAIDs (though GI risk is not zero).
rationales.B: Ketorolac carries high GI and renal toxicity risk and is not used for chronic OA pain.
rationales.C: Indomethacin is a nonselective NSAID with notable GI/CNS adverse effects.
rationales.D: Naproxen is nonselective and increases GI bleeding risk.
rationales.E: Ibuprofen is nonselective and increases GI bleeding risk.

takeaway: COX-2 selective NSAIDs (celecoxib) can reduce GI ulcer risk vs traditional NSAIDs—still use caution in high-risk patients.

title: Renal colic
stem:
A 33-year-old man presents with flank pain radiating to the groin. CT confirms a ureteral stone.
He has normal kidney function and no history of peptic ulcer disease.
question: Which medication is an appropriate first-line analgesic option for acute renal colic?

rationales.A: Correct. NSAIDs such as ketorolac are commonly used for acute renal colic pain and can reduce prostaglandin-mediated ureteral spasm and inflammation.
rationales.B: Furosemide is a diuretic and does not treat renal colic pain.
rationales.C: Warfarin is an anticoagulant and does not treat pain; it could worsen bleeding risk.
rationales.D: Omeprazole treats acid-related disorders and does not relieve renal colic pain.
rationales.E: Dicyclomine is an antispasmodic for GI cramping, not ureteral colic pain.

takeaway: Renal colic pain is often treated first with an NSAID like ketorolac when not contraindicated.

title: Neuropathic pain
stem:
A 62-year-old man has postherpetic neuralgia with burning neuropathic pain along a thoracic dermatome.
He wants a non-opioid medication for nerve pain.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to start?

rationales.A: Correct. Gabapentin is commonly used for neuropathic pain such as postherpetic neuralgia.
rationales.B: Opioids can treat pain but are not first-line for chronic neuropathic pain and carry dependence/respiratory risks.
rationales.C: NSAIDs are generally less effective for neuropathic pain than gabapentinoids.
rationales.D: Systemic steroids are not a standard chronic treatment for postherpetic neuralgia pain.
rationales.E: Ciprofloxacin is an antibiotic and does not treat neuropathic pain.

takeaway: Neuropathic pain responds best to neuropathic agents (gabapentin/pregabalin) rather than NSAIDs.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Fibromyalgia
stem:
A 45-year-old woman has fibromyalgia with widespread pain and sleep disruption.
She wants a medication option with evidence for fibromyalgia pain.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Pregabalin is used for neuropathic pain and has evidence/indication for fibromyalgia pain management.
rationales.B: NSAIDs are often less effective for fibromyalgia (non-inflammatory pain syndrome).
rationales.C: Doxycycline is an antibiotic and does not treat fibromyalgia.
rationales.D: Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet drug and does not treat fibromyalgia pain.
rationales.E: Tamsulosin treats BPH-related urinary symptoms, not fibromyalgia.

takeaway: Fibromyalgia is not primarily inflammatory—neuropathic-modulating agents like pregabalin can help.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Paxlovid interaction
stem:
A 64-year-old man with CAD and hyperlipidemia tests positive for COVID-19 and is prescribed nirmatrelvir/ritonavir.
Home meds include simvastatin 40 mg nightly.
question: Which medication should be temporarily held to avoid a dangerous drug interaction?

rationales.A: Correct. Ritonavir is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor and can markedly increase simvastatin levels, raising the risk of severe myopathy/rhabdomyolysis. Simvastatin should be held (or switched) during therapy.
rationales.B: Aspirin does not have the same high-risk CYP3A4 interaction with ritonavir.
rationales.C: Clopidogrel effectiveness can be affected by some drug interactions, but the most dangerous/clear interaction in this list is simvastatin with ritonavir.
rationales.D: Acetaminophen does not have the classic ritonavir CYP3A4 toxicity interaction seen with simvastatin.
rationales.E: Nitroglycerin does not have a major CYP3A4-based toxicity interaction with ritonavir.

takeaway: Ritonavir is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor—simvastatin (and lovastatin) are classic 'hold/avoid' drugs with ritonavir-containing regimens.

title: Ventilator-associated pneumonia
stem:
A 70-year-old man has been mechanically ventilated in the ICU for 6 days after a stroke.
He develops fever (39.1°C), leukocytosis, and increasing purulent secretions.
CXR: new right lower-lobe infiltrate.
The team is starting empiric antibiotics with concern for Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
question: Which antibiotic provides antipseudomonal beta-lactam coverage from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Cefepime (a 4th-generation cephalosporin) provides broad gram-negative coverage including Pseudomonas and is commonly used for empiric HAP/VAP.
rationales.B: Ceftriaxone does not reliably cover Pseudomonas.
rationales.C: Amoxicillin does not cover Pseudomonas and is not appropriate for HAP/VAP empiric therapy.
rationales.D: Azithromycin targets atypicals and some gram-positives but does not cover Pseudomonas and is not sufficient for VAP empiric therapy.
rationales.E: IV vancomycin covers MRSA, not Pseudomonas; it may be added for MRSA risk but does not replace antipseudomonal coverage.

takeaway: HAP/VAP with Pseudomonas concern: choose an antipseudomonal beta-lactam (cefepime is a classic).

title: Perforated appendicitis
stem:
A 33-year-old woman presents with diffuse abdominal pain, fever, and guarding.
CT abdomen shows a perforated appendix with localized abscess.
She is being started on empiric IV antibiotics covering gram-negatives and anaerobes.
question: Which single agent below provides appropriate broad coverage for this intra-abdominal infection?

rationales.A: Correct. Piperacillin/tazobactam covers many gram-negative organisms (including some Pseudomonas) and anaerobes—appropriate for complicated intra-abdominal infections.
rationales.B: Cefepime has strong gram-negative coverage but lacks reliable anaerobic coverage as monotherapy for perforated appendicitis.
rationales.C: Ceftriaxone lacks anaerobic coverage needed here unless combined with an anaerobic agent (e.g., metronidazole).
rationales.D: Metronidazole covers anaerobes but does not provide sufficient gram-negative coverage alone for perforated appendicitis.
rationales.E: Amoxicillin is not appropriate broad empiric therapy for perforated appendicitis.

takeaway: Complicated intra-abdominal infections need BOTH gram-negative and anaerobic coverage—piperacillin/tazobactam is a classic single-agent option.

title: MRSA skin infection in pregnancy with sulfa allergy
stem:
A 28-year-old woman who is 24 weeks pregnant presents with a painful skin abscess on her thigh.
Incision and drainage is performed. She needs an oral antibiotic due to surrounding cellulitis.
Allergies: trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole causes hives.
Local epidemiology suggests community-acquired MRSA is common.
question: Which antibiotic is the best oral option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Clindamycin is an oral option that can cover community-acquired MRSA and is generally compatible with pregnancy.
rationales.B: TMP/SMX is not appropriate given her allergy (hives).
rationales.C: Doxycycline is generally avoided in pregnancy due to fetal tooth/bone effects.
rationales.D: Cephalexin does not reliably cover MRSA.
rationales.E: Amoxicillin does not reliably cover MRSA.

takeaway: When MRSA is likely and pregnancy/sulfa allergy limit choices, clindamycin can be a high-yield oral option.

title: Strep pharyngitis
stem:
A 16-year-old girl has fever, sore throat, tender anterior cervical nodes, and tonsillar exudates.
Rapid antigen test for group A streptococcus is positive.
No medication allergies.
question: Which antibiotic is the best first-line treatment from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Penicillin V is narrow, effective first-line therapy for group A streptococcal pharyngitis when there is no penicillin allergy.
rationales.B: Azithromycin is an alternative in true penicillin allergy, but penicillin V is preferred when possible.
rationales.C: Doxycycline is not first-line for strep pharyngitis.
rationales.D: Ciprofloxacin is not used for strep pharyngitis and has unnecessary broad coverage.
rationales.E: Nitrofurantoin is used for cystitis, not pharyngitis.

takeaway: Positive strep throat + no allergy: penicillin V is a classic narrow, high-yield choice.

title: Acute otitis media
stem:
A 3-year-old boy has fever and ear pain. Otoscopy shows a bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane.
He has no medication allergies and has not received antibiotics in the past 30 days.
question: Which antibiotic is the best first-line treatment?

rationales.A: Correct. High-dose amoxicillin is a standard first-line therapy for uncomplicated acute otitis media in children without recent antibiotics or conjunctivitis.
rationales.B: Amoxicillin/clavulanate is often reserved for cases with recent amoxicillin exposure, concurrent purulent conjunctivitis, or suspected beta-lactamase organisms.
rationales.C: Cefdinir is commonly used in select penicillin-allergic patients; amoxicillin is preferred when no allergy is present.
rationales.D: Azithromycin is generally reserved for true severe beta-lactam allergy but can have higher resistance.
rationales.E: IM/IV ceftriaxone is used for severe/refractory cases or inability to take oral therapy—not typical first-line in an otherwise stable child.

takeaway: Uncomplicated AOM + no allergy + no recent antibiotics: start amoxicillin.

title: Bacterial sinusitis after recent amoxicillin
stem:
A 45-year-old man has 12 days of purulent nasal discharge, facial pain, and fever consistent with acute bacterial sinusitis.
He took amoxicillin for a dental infection 2 weeks ago.
No drug allergies.
question: Which antibiotic is most appropriate now?

rationales.A: Correct. Recent amoxicillin exposure increases the likelihood of beta-lactamase–producing organisms; amoxicillin/clavulanate is a common first-choice in this scenario.
rationales.B: Amoxicillin alone may be insufficient after recent exposure due to beta-lactamase organisms.
rationales.C: Azithromycin is not preferred due to resistance patterns and is typically reserved for certain allergy situations.
rationales.D: Doxycycline can be an alternative in some adults, but the classic next step after recent amoxicillin is amoxicillin/clavulanate if no contraindications exist.
rationales.E: Ceftriaxone is IV/IM and not typically used for routine outpatient sinusitis when oral options are appropriate.

takeaway: Recent amoxicillin? Step up to amoxicillin/clavulanate for suspected bacterial sinusitis.

title: Outpatient pyelonephritis
stem:
A 29-year-old woman has fever (38.5°C), flank pain, and dysuria.
Urinalysis: positive nitrites and leukocyte esterase.
She is stable for outpatient management and is not pregnant.
No allergies. eGFR 90 mL/min/1.73m².
question: Which oral antibiotic is an appropriate choice for outpatient pyelonephritis from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ciprofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone option commonly used for outpatient pyelonephritis when local resistance patterns allow.
rationales.B: Nitrofurantoin is for uncomplicated cystitis and does not achieve adequate renal tissue concentrations for pyelonephritis.
rationales.C: Penicillin V does not cover typical gram-negative uropathogens and is not used for pyelonephritis.
rationales.D: Metronidazole targets anaerobes and does not cover typical pyelonephritis pathogens.
rationales.E: Nystatin is an antifungal and does not treat bacterial pyelonephritis.

takeaway: Nitrofurantoin is cystitis-only; pyelonephritis needs an agent with renal tissue penetration (ciprofloxacin is a common option).

title: Chlamydia in pregnancy
stem:
A 22-year-old woman who is 14 weeks pregnant is diagnosed with Chlamydia trachomatis cervicitis.
She wants treatment that is safe in pregnancy.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Azithromycin is a standard treatment option for chlamydia in pregnancy.
rationales.B: Doxycycline is generally avoided in pregnancy due to effects on fetal teeth and bone.
rationales.C: Ciprofloxacin is not standard therapy for chlamydia and is generally avoided in pregnancy unless no alternatives exist.
rationales.D: Metronidazole treats bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis, not chlamydia.
rationales.E: Penicillin V does not treat chlamydia.

takeaway: Pregnancy changes antibiotic choices—azithromycin is high-yield for chlamydia when doxycycline is avoided.

title: Tick bite prophylaxis
stem:
A 34-year-old man removes an engorged Ixodes tick from his leg.
The tick was likely attached for >36 hours, and he presents within 48 hours of removal.
He lives in an area where Lyme disease is common. He is not allergic to tetracyclines.
question: Which medication is used for prophylaxis in this scenario?

rationales.A: Correct. A single dose of doxycycline is used for Lyme disease prophylaxis in select high-risk Ixodes tick exposures when criteria are met.
rationales.B: Amoxicillin treats Lyme disease in some patients but is not the standard single-dose prophylaxis option.
rationales.C: Azithromycin is not the typical prophylaxis choice for Lyme exposure.
rationales.D: Cefdinir is not used for Lyme prophylaxis.
rationales.E: Metronidazole targets anaerobes and is not used for Lyme prophylaxis.

takeaway: High-risk Ixodes tick exposure + meets criteria → single-dose doxycycline prophylaxis is a classic board-style fact.

title: Influenza within 48 hours
stem:
A 68-year-old man with COPD develops fever, myalgias, and cough for 24 hours.
Influenza A PCR is positive.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to start?

rationales.A: Correct. Oseltamivir is an antiviral used to treat influenza and is most effective when started early (typically within 48 hours), especially in high-risk patients.
rationales.B: Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is used for COVID-19, not influenza.
rationales.C: Acyclovir treats herpes viruses, not influenza.
rationales.D: Azithromycin is an antibiotic and does not treat influenza (a viral illness).
rationales.E: Fluconazole is an antifungal and does not treat influenza.

takeaway: Influenza (especially high-risk patients) → start oseltamivir early.

title: High-risk outpatient COVID
stem:
A 72-year-old woman tests positive for COVID-19 and has symptoms for 2 days (fever, cough, fatigue).
PMH: hypertension and diabetes.
She is stable for outpatient treatment.
question: Which medication is an appropriate oral antiviral option to reduce progression to severe disease (when started early)?

rationales.A: Correct. Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is used for early outpatient COVID-19 in eligible high-risk patients to reduce hospitalization/death risk.
rationales.B: Oseltamivir treats influenza, not COVID-19.
rationales.C: Azithromycin is an antibiotic and does not treat COVID-19.
rationales.D: Fluconazole is an antifungal and does not treat COVID-19.
rationales.E: Valacyclovir treats herpes viruses, not COVID-19.

takeaway: COVID antiviral: nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is a high-yield early outpatient option—always check drug interactions.

title: Recurrent genital herpes suppression
stem:
A 30-year-old man has frequent recurrent genital herpes outbreaks (8 per year).
He wants daily suppressive therapy to reduce outbreak frequency and transmission risk.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below for daily suppression with convenient dosing?

rationales.A: Correct. Valacyclovir is commonly used for HSV suppression and has improved oral bioavailability, allowing convenient dosing.
rationales.B: Acyclovir is effective for HSV but typically requires more frequent dosing; the question asks for convenient daily suppression.
rationales.C: Oseltamivir treats influenza, not HSV.
rationales.D: Fluconazole treats fungal infections, not HSV.
rationales.E: Metronidazole treats anaerobes and certain protozoa, not HSV.

takeaway: For chronic HSV suppression, valacyclovir is a high-yield option due to convenient dosing.

title: HSV encephalitis
stem:
A 56-year-old man presents with fever, confusion, and new-onset focal seizures.
MRI suggests temporal lobe involvement, and CSF PCR is pending for HSV.
He is hospitalized and requires immediate empiric therapy.
question: Which medication should be started urgently from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Acyclovir is the classic urgent therapy for suspected HSV encephalitis (typically IV in practice) while awaiting confirmation.
rationales.B: Valacyclovir is an oral prodrug used for HSV but is not the standard therapy for suspected encephalitis requiring IV treatment.
rationales.C: Oseltamivir treats influenza, not HSV encephalitis.
rationales.D: Fluconazole treats fungal infections, not HSV.
rationales.E: Cefdinir is an antibiotic and does not treat HSV.

takeaway: Suspected HSV encephalitis is an emergency—start acyclovir immediately.

title: Uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis
stem:
A 26-year-old woman has vulvar itching and thick white “cottage cheese” discharge.
Wet mount shows budding yeast.
She is not pregnant and has no liver disease.
question: Which medication is the best single-dose oral treatment from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Oral fluconazole is a classic single-dose treatment for uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis in nonpregnant patients.
rationales.B: Metronidazole treats bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis, not candidiasis.
rationales.C: Acyclovir treats herpes viruses, not yeast infections.
rationales.D: Azithromycin is an antibiotic and does not treat candidiasis.
rationales.E: Nitrofurantoin treats cystitis, not candidiasis.

takeaway: Thick white discharge + yeast → fluconazole (classic single-dose option when not pregnant).

title: Oral thrush
stem:
A 6-month-old infant has white plaques on the tongue and inner cheeks that scrape off with mild erythema underneath.
Diagnosis: oral candidiasis (thrush).
question: Which medication is the best first-line treatment from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Nystatin oral suspension is commonly used for thrush; it is minimally absorbed and acts locally in the mouth.
rationales.B: Fluconazole is an option in refractory cases, but nystatin is a classic first-line topical therapy for uncomplicated thrush.
rationales.C: Terbinafine is used for dermatophyte infections and onychomycosis, not oral thrush.
rationales.D: Metronidazole does not treat Candida.
rationales.E: Valacyclovir treats HSV, not Candida.

takeaway: Thrush is local Candida—nystatin is a classic first-line topical option.

title: Onychomycosis
stem:
A 52-year-old man has thickened, yellow, brittle toenails for 1 year.
KOH prep confirms dermatophyte onychomycosis.
Topical therapies have failed and he wants the most effective oral option.
question: Which medication is most appropriate?

rationales.A: Correct. Oral terbinafine is a highly effective systemic option for dermatophyte onychomycosis.
rationales.B: Nystatin targets Candida and is used locally; it is not effective for dermatophyte nail infection.
rationales.C: Fluconazole targets Candida and some fungi; terbinafine is preferred for dermatophytes in nails.
rationales.D: Metronidazole is an antibacterial/antiprotozoal, not antifungal for dermatophytes.
rationales.E: Acyclovir treats HSV, not fungal nail disease.

takeaway: Dermatophyte nail infection often needs systemic therapy—terbinafine is a high-yield choice.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Bacterial vaginosis
stem:
A 29-year-old woman has thin gray vaginal discharge and a fishy odor.
Wet mount shows clue cells; pH is elevated.
question: Which medication is most appropriate treatment from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Metronidazole is a first-line therapy for bacterial vaginosis.
rationales.B: Fluconazole treats vulvovaginal candidiasis, not BV.
rationales.C: Valacyclovir treats HSV, not BV.
rationales.D: Penicillin V does not treat BV.
rationales.E: Nystatin treats Candida, not BV.

takeaway: Fishy odor + clue cells = BV → metronidazole.

title: Severe penicillin allergy + CAP
stem:
A 58-year-old man presents with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP).
CXR: left lower-lobe infiltrate. He is stable for outpatient treatment.
Allergy: anaphylaxis to penicillin.
question: Which medication is an appropriate single-agent outpatient option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Moxifloxacin is a “respiratory fluoroquinolone” with strong activity against typical CAP pathogens and is used in select patients when beta-lactams cannot be used.
rationales.B: Nitrofurantoin treats cystitis and does not treat pneumonia.
rationales.C: Ciprofloxacin is not a preferred CAP agent because it has weaker activity against Streptococcus pneumoniae compared with respiratory fluoroquinolones.
rationales.D: Ceftriaxone is a beta-lactam given IV/IM and is not ideal for outpatient monotherapy; also cross-reactivity concerns may exist depending on the allergy history.
rationales.E: Penicillin V is not used to treat CAP and is contraindicated given his anaphylaxis history.

takeaway: For CAP, not all fluoroquinolones are the same—moxifloxacin is a classic “respiratory” option; ciprofloxacin is not.

title: MRSA pneumonia
stem:
A 61-year-old man is hospitalized with severe pneumonia and is intubated.
Sputum Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters and MRSA is strongly suspected.
He is on sertraline for depression.
question: Which medication is an appropriate choice for MRSA pneumonia from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. IV vancomycin is a standard therapy for suspected MRSA pneumonia in hospitalized patients.
rationales.B: Oral vancomycin is used for C. difficile colitis and does not treat pneumonia effectively.
rationales.C: Ceftriaxone does not reliably cover MRSA.
rationales.D: Nitrofurantoin is for cystitis and does not treat pneumonia.
rationales.E: Terbinafine is an antifungal and does not treat MRSA.

takeaway: MRSA pneumonia requires systemic MRSA-active therapy—IV vancomycin is classic. Oral vancomycin is for C. diff.

title: MRSA bacteremia with vancomycin nephrotoxicity
stem:
A 66-year-old man has MRSA bacteremia from an infected dialysis catheter.
He was started on IV vancomycin, but his creatinine has increased and vancomycin levels are difficult to manage.
Blood cultures remain positive at 48 hours.
question: Which medication is a reasonable alternative for MRSA bacteremia from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Daptomycin is an IV antibiotic option for MRSA bacteremia/right-sided endocarditis and is often used when vancomycin is not suitable.
rationales.B: Oral vancomycin is not used for bacteremia; it is used for C. difficile colitis.
rationales.C: Azithromycin does not cover MRSA bacteremia.
rationales.D: Cefdinir does not cover MRSA bacteremia.
rationales.E: Nitrofurantoin is for cystitis and does not treat bacteremia.

takeaway: Daptomycin is a high-yield MRSA bacteremia option (but not for pneumonia).

title: COPD maintenance
stem:
A 64-year-old man with COPD has chronic dyspnea and uses his albuterol inhaler most days.
He has had 1 mild exacerbation in the past year but is currently stable.
He wants a daily maintenance inhaler to improve symptoms.
question: Which medication is the best daily maintenance option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Tiotropium is a long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) used as a maintenance bronchodilator in COPD to improve symptoms and reduce exacerbations.
rationales.B: Albuterol is a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) used for rescue relief, not daily maintenance control by itself.
rationales.C: Ipratropium is short-acting (SAMA) and is used for intermittent or acute symptom relief; it is not the preferred once-daily maintenance option.
rationales.D: Inhaled corticosteroids are not first-line monotherapy in COPD maintenance and are typically reserved for select patients (e.g., frequent exacerbations with eosinophilia/asthma overlap).
rationales.E: Montelukast is an asthma/allergy controller and is not standard COPD maintenance therapy.

takeaway: COPD maintenance bronchodilation: LAMA (tiotropium) is a high-yield daily controller choice; albuterol remains rescue.

title: Acute COPD exacerbation
stem:
A 69-year-old man with COPD presents with increased dyspnea and wheezing consistent with an acute exacerbation.
He has already used albuterol with partial relief.
He is hemodynamically stable and is not in respiratory failure.
question: Which inhaled medication is commonly added for short-term bronchodilation during a COPD exacerbation?

rationales.A: Correct. Ipratropium is a short-acting muscarinic antagonist (SAMA) often used with albuterol during acute COPD exacerbations for additional bronchodilation.
rationales.B: Tiotropium is long-acting and used for maintenance rather than rapid, short-term relief in an acute exacerbation.
rationales.C: Montelukast is not standard therapy for acute COPD exacerbations.
rationales.D: Salmeterol is a LABA used for maintenance and is not appropriate for acute relief.
rationales.E: Inhaled corticosteroids are controller therapy and are not a rapid bronchodilator for acute symptom relief.

takeaway: COPD exacerbation bronchodilators: albuterol + ipratropium is a classic acute combo; long-acting agents are maintenance.

title: Asthma step-up
stem:
A 20-year-old woman has asthma and is on budesonide (inhaled) daily.
Despite adherence, she has symptoms 4 days/week and uses albuterol most days.
She has no history of COPD and does not smoke.
question: Which medication is the best next step to improve control from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Escalating to an inhaled corticosteroid/LABA combination (budesonide/formoterol) is a common next step when symptoms persist on an ICS alone.
rationales.B: LABA monotherapy (salmeterol alone) should not be used for asthma because it increases risk of severe asthma outcomes without an ICS.
rationales.C: Albuterol is rescue therapy and frequent use indicates inadequate control; it is not a controller step-up.
rationales.D: Theophylline is rarely used due to narrow therapeutic index and interactions; it is not preferred over ICS/LABA step-up.
rationales.E: Tiotropium can be an add-on in some cases, but the standard step-up from ICS alone is often adding a LABA in a combination inhaler.

takeaway: Persistent asthma on ICS → step up to ICS/LABA (budesonide/formoterol) rather than LABA alone.

title: LABA monotherapy warning in asthma
stem:
A 23-year-old man with asthma asks if he can stop his inhaled corticosteroid and use only a long-acting bronchodilator daily.
He is considering a medication that provides long-lasting bronchodilation but no steroid.
question: Which medication should NOT be used as monotherapy for asthma?

rationales.A: Correct. LABA monotherapy (e.g., salmeterol alone) should be avoided in asthma because it increases the risk of severe asthma outcomes when used without an inhaled corticosteroid.
rationales.B: Inhaled corticosteroids (budesonide) are foundational controller therapy for asthma.
rationales.C: ICS/LABA combinations are appropriate controller options because the LABA is paired with an ICS.
rationales.D: Albuterol is a rescue medication; while not a controller, it is not the specific 'avoid monotherapy' safety issue being tested here.
rationales.E: Montelukast can be used as controller therapy in some patients and does not carry the LABA monotherapy boxed warning issue.

takeaway: High-yield safety point: LABAs (like salmeterol) must be paired with an ICS in asthma—don’t use LABA alone.

title: Severe allergic asthma
stem:
A 35-year-old woman has severe persistent asthma despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroid/LABA therapy.
She has positive allergy testing to dust mites and elevated IgE.
She has frequent exacerbations requiring oral steroids.
question: Which medication is an anti-IgE biologic option for allergic asthma from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Omalizumab is an anti-IgE monoclonal antibody used for moderate-to-severe allergic asthma in appropriate patients.
rationales.B: Dupilumab is a biologic used for certain asthma phenotypes and atopic dermatitis, but the question specifically asks for the anti-IgE agent.
rationales.C: Montelukast is an oral leukotriene receptor antagonist and is not an anti-IgE biologic.
rationales.D: Roflumilast is used for COPD (chronic bronchitis) to reduce exacerbations—not allergic asthma anti-IgE therapy.
rationales.E: Theophylline is a bronchodilator with narrow therapeutic index and is not a biologic therapy.

takeaway: Allergic asthma + elevated IgE: omalizumab is the classic anti-IgE biologic.

title: Asthma with atopic dermatitis
stem:
A 28-year-old man has moderate-to-severe asthma and severe atopic dermatitis.
He has elevated eosinophils and continues to have asthma symptoms despite inhaled controller therapy.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Dupilumab is a biologic used for certain asthma phenotypes and is also used for atopic dermatitis—making it a strong option when both conditions are present.
rationales.B: Omalizumab is anti-IgE and is used in allergic asthma, but the vignette highlights coexisting severe atopic dermatitis and eosinophilia—features that point strongly toward dupilumab.
rationales.C: Montelukast is an oral controller with modest effect and is not as appropriate for severe asthma with significant atopic dermatitis.
rationales.D: Roflumilast is for COPD chronic bronchitis exacerbation reduction, not asthma/atopic dermatitis.
rationales.E: Theophylline is not preferred due to toxicity and does not target the underlying type-2 inflammation.

takeaway: Asthma + atopic dermatitis is a classic place dupilumab fits (type-2 inflammation).

title: COPD chronic bronchitis with frequent exacerbations
stem:
A 60-year-old man with COPD (FEV1 38% predicted) has chronic bronchitis and 3 exacerbations in the past year despite inhaled maintenance therapy.
He is not currently having an acute exacerbation.
question: Which medication can be added to reduce COPD exacerbations in this setting?

rationales.A: Correct. Roflumilast (a PDE-4 inhibitor) can reduce exacerbations in select patients with severe COPD and chronic bronchitis with frequent exacerbations.
rationales.B: Montelukast is not standard COPD therapy.
rationales.C: Omalizumab is for allergic asthma, not COPD exacerbation prevention.
rationales.D: Inhaled corticosteroids may be used in selected COPD patients, but the vignette highlights chronic bronchitis with frequent exacerbations—roflumilast is the key distinguishing add-on option here.
rationales.E: Levalbuterol is short-acting rescue therapy and does not reduce exacerbation frequency as a long-term strategy.

takeaway: Severe COPD + chronic bronchitis + frequent exacerbations: roflumilast is a high-yield add-on.

title: Theophylline toxicity
stem:
A 55-year-old man with COPD takes theophylline.
He is prescribed an antibiotic for pyelonephritis.
Two days later he develops nausea, tremor, insomnia, and tachycardia.
question: Which antibiotic is most likely to have increased theophylline levels via a drug interaction?

rationales.A: Correct. Ciprofloxacin can increase theophylline concentrations by inhibiting metabolism, leading to toxicity symptoms.
rationales.B: Amoxicillin does not typically increase theophylline levels.
rationales.C: Nitrofurantoin does not typically increase theophylline levels.
rationales.D: Metronidazole is not a classic major theophylline interaction compared with ciprofloxacin.
rationales.E: Doxycycline is not a classic major theophylline interaction.

takeaway: Theophylline has many interactions and a narrow therapeutic index—ciprofloxacin is a classic drug that can raise theophylline levels.

title: Allergic rhinitis
stem:
A 32-year-old woman has year-round allergic rhinitis with congestion, sneezing, and itchy nose.
She wants the most effective daily medication for nasal congestion and inflammation.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Intranasal corticosteroids (like fluticasone nasal) are among the most effective therapies for allergic rhinitis, especially congestion.
rationales.B: Azelastine nasal is helpful, but intranasal steroids are generally the most effective monotherapy for persistent symptoms and congestion.
rationales.C: Pseudoephedrine is a decongestant for short-term symptom relief and can raise BP/HR; it is not preferred for long-term daily control.
rationales.D: Diphenhydramine is sedating and anticholinergic and is not preferred for chronic daily allergic rhinitis control.
rationales.E: Loratadine helps sneezing/itching but is often less effective than intranasal steroids for congestion.

takeaway: For persistent allergic rhinitis—especially congestion—intranasal corticosteroids are first-line (fluticasone nasal is classic).

title: Least sedating antihistamine
stem:
A 40-year-old airline pilot has seasonal allergic rhinitis and wants a daytime antihistamine with minimal sedation.
He must avoid impaired alertness at work.
question: Which medication best fits his goal?

rationales.A: Correct. Fexofenadine is a second-generation antihistamine with very low CNS penetration and is among the least sedating options.
rationales.B: Diphenhydramine is a first-generation antihistamine and is strongly sedating.
rationales.C: Cetirizine is second-generation but can cause some sedation in a subset of patients.
rationales.D: Levocetirizine can also cause some sedation.
rationales.E: Loratadine is generally non-sedating, but fexofenadine is classically considered the least sedating option.

takeaway: If sedation matters (drivers/pilots), choose a low-sedation second-gen antihistamine—fexofenadine is a high-yield pick.

title: Quick-onset heartburn relief
stem:
A 48-year-old man has mild GERD symptoms (burning epigastric discomfort) 2–3 times per week after spicy foods.
He wants an as-needed medication that works faster than a PPI for occasional symptoms.
question: Which medication is an H2 receptor antagonist option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Famotidine is an H2 receptor antagonist that can provide relatively quick symptom relief for intermittent GERD/heartburn.
rationales.B: Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) used for acid suppression but is not an H2 blocker.
rationales.C: Pantoprazole is a PPI, not an H2 blocker.
rationales.D: Esomeprazole is a PPI, not an H2 blocker.
rationales.E: Sucralfate is a mucosal protectant; it is not an H2 blocker and is less commonly used for simple intermittent heartburn.

takeaway: H2 blockers (famotidine) can be useful for intermittent heartburn; PPIs are stronger but slower and usually scheduled.
module_tags: Module 8a Acid Suppression (PPIs, H2 Blockers, Mucosal Protection)

title: Ulcer coating agent
stem:
A 60-year-old woman has a duodenal ulcer and asks for a medication that works by coating the ulcer and protecting it from acid.
She takes several morning medications and wants to know which one can bind other drugs and should be spaced out.
question: Which medication best fits this description?

rationales.A: Correct. Sucralfate forms a protective barrier over ulcers and can bind other medications, so it should be separated from other drugs.
rationales.B: Pantoprazole is a PPI that suppresses acid production; it does not coat the ulcer or bind other meds in the same way.
rationales.C: Famotidine is an H2 blocker and does not coat ulcers.
rationales.D: Omeprazole is a PPI, not a coating agent.
rationales.E: Esomeprazole is a PPI, not a coating agent.

takeaway: Sucralfate is a mucosal protectant—think 'coats ulcers' and 'separate from other meds'.
module_tags: Module 8a Acid Suppression (PPIs, H2 Blockers, Mucosal Protection)

title: Chemo-induced nausea prophylaxis
stem:
A 52-year-old woman is starting highly emetogenic chemotherapy.
The oncology team wants to add an NK1 receptor antagonist to prevent delayed chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting.
question: Which medication is an NK1 receptor antagonist option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Aprepitant is an NK1 receptor antagonist used for prevention of chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting, particularly delayed emesis.
rationales.B: Ondansetron is a 5-HT3 antagonist used for nausea/vomiting but is not an NK1 antagonist.
rationales.C: Promethazine is an antihistamine/antiemetic and is not an NK1 antagonist.
rationales.D: Metoclopramide is a dopamine antagonist/prokinetic and is not an NK1 antagonist.
rationales.E: Scopolamine is an anticholinergic used for motion sickness and is not used as an NK1 antagonist for chemo prophylaxis.

takeaway: Chemo prophylaxis: NK1 antagonist (aprepitant) targets delayed nausea—distinct from ondansetron (5-HT3).

title: Diabetic gastroparesis
stem:
A 59-year-old man with long-standing diabetes has early satiety, postprandial fullness, and nausea.
Gastric emptying study confirms gastroparesis.
question: Which medication can improve symptoms by increasing gastric motility?

rationales.A: Correct. Metoclopramide is a prokinetic agent used for diabetic gastroparesis by increasing gastric motility (monitor for EPS/tardive dyskinesia).
rationales.B: Ondansetron can reduce nausea but does not address delayed gastric emptying.
rationales.C: Promethazine can reduce nausea but does not improve gastric motility.
rationales.D: Loperamide slows GI motility and would worsen gastroparesis symptoms.
rationales.E: Sucralfate coats ulcers; it does not treat gastroparesis.

takeaway: Gastroparesis needs a prokinetic—metoclopramide is the high-yield choice (but watch for neurologic adverse effects).
module_tags: Module 3b Metformin & Oral Agents Overview (diabetes context)

title: Chronic constipation
stem:
A 41-year-old woman has chronic functional constipation.
No red flags (no weight loss, anemia, or blood in stool).
She wants a gentle daily medication that increases stool water content.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Polyethylene glycol is an osmotic laxative that draws water into the stool and is commonly used for chronic constipation.
rationales.B: Loperamide treats diarrhea by slowing motility and would worsen constipation.
rationales.C: Diphenoxylate/atropine is an antidiarrheal and would worsen constipation.
rationales.D: Dicyclomine is an antispasmodic and can worsen constipation due to anticholinergic effects.
rationales.E: Ondansetron can cause constipation and is not a laxative.

takeaway: Chronic constipation: polyethylene glycol is a high-yield first-line osmotic option.

title: IBS-C
stem:
A 35-year-old woman meets criteria for irritable bowel syndrome with constipation (IBS-C).
She has abdominal pain that improves after bowel movements and constipation despite fiber and polyethylene glycol.
question: Which medication is an appropriate next option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Linaclotide is used for IBS-C and chronic idiopathic constipation; it can improve stool frequency and abdominal symptoms in IBS-C.
rationales.B: Loperamide is for diarrhea and can worsen constipation.
rationales.C: Diphenoxylate/atropine is for diarrhea and can worsen constipation.
rationales.D: Dicyclomine may help cramping but does not treat constipation and may worsen it due to anticholinergic effects.
rationales.E: Ondansetron is an antiemetic and can cause constipation.

takeaway: IBS-C that persists despite basics: linaclotide is a high-yield next-line medication.

title: Hepatic encephalopathy
stem:
A 58-year-old man with cirrhosis is brought in for confusion and asterixis.
Ammonia level is elevated. He is diagnosed with hepatic encephalopathy.
question: Which medication is a standard first-line therapy to reduce ammonia levels?

rationales.A: Correct. Lactulose acidifies the colon and traps ammonia, promoting its excretion and improving hepatic encephalopathy.
rationales.B: Loperamide slows motility and does not reduce ammonia; it may worsen constipation.
rationales.C: Docusate is a stool softener and does not specifically treat encephalopathy.
rationales.D: Senna/docusate treats constipation but does not specifically reduce ammonia for encephalopathy.
rationales.E: PEG with electrolytes is mainly for bowel prep; it is not the standard first-line encephalopathy therapy.

takeaway: Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose is a high-yield first-line to reduce ammonia and improve mentation.

title: Acute non-bloody diarrhea
stem:
A 27-year-old man returns from travel with watery diarrhea for 1 day.
He has no fever, no blood in stool, and is tolerating oral fluids.
He wants symptomatic treatment to decrease stool frequency.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Loperamide is an antidiarrheal that decreases intestinal motility and can reduce stool frequency in uncomplicated, non-bloody diarrhea.
rationales.B: PEG is a laxative and would worsen diarrhea.
rationales.C: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative and would worsen diarrhea.
rationales.D: Senna/docusate is for constipation and would worsen diarrhea.
rationales.E: Lactulose is a laxative and would worsen diarrhea.

takeaway: Uncomplicated watery diarrhea (no fever/blood): loperamide is a high-yield symptomatic option.

title: Antidiarrheal with anticholinergic component
stem:
A 44-year-old woman has chronic noninfectious diarrhea.
Her clinician considers an antidiarrheal that is an opioid analog and is combined with atropine to discourage misuse.
question: Which medication fits this description?

rationales.A: Correct. Diphenoxylate is an opioid analog that slows GI motility; atropine is added to deter abuse.
rationales.B: Loperamide is an opioid receptor agonist antidiarrheal but is not the combination product with atropine.
rationales.C: Docusate is a stool softener and would not treat diarrhea.
rationales.D: PEG is a laxative and would worsen diarrhea.
rationales.E: Linaclotide is for IBS-C and would worsen diarrhea.

takeaway: Diphenoxylate/atropine = antidiarrheal opioid analog + atropine deterrent—classic pharmacology detail.

title: Ulcerative colitis maintenance
stem:
A 25-year-old woman has mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis with intermittent bloody diarrhea and urgency.
She is stable as an outpatient and needs a medication to reduce colonic inflammation and maintain remission.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Mesalamine (5-ASA) is commonly used for mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis to reduce inflammation and maintain remission.
rationales.B: Infliximab is a biologic reserved for moderate-to-severe disease or refractory cases; it is not typically first-line for mild UC.
rationales.C: Loperamide provides symptomatic diarrhea control but does not treat underlying inflammation and may be risky in active severe colitis.
rationales.D: Diphenoxylate/atropine is symptomatic only and does not treat inflammation.
rationales.E: Dicyclomine is an antispasmodic and does not treat UC inflammation.

takeaway: Mild–moderate UC: start with anti-inflammatory therapy (mesalamine), not just antidiarrheals.

title: Essential tremor with asthma
stem:
A 58-year-old woman has essential tremor that interferes with writing.
PMH: asthma with intermittent wheezing.
She wants medication therapy and would like to avoid a drug that could worsen bronchospasm.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Primidone is a first-line option for essential tremor and avoids the bronchospasm risk associated with nonselective beta-blockers.
rationales.B: Propranolol is effective for essential tremor but is nonselective and can trigger bronchospasm in asthma.
rationales.C: Metoprolol is β1-selective and may be safer than propranolol in asthma, but primidone is a classic first-line alternative when beta-blockers are undesirable.
rationales.D: Gabapentin may help some neuropathic pain conditions; it is not a classic first-line essential tremor medication compared with primidone/propranolol.
rationales.E: Topiramate can be used in select cases but is not as classic a first-line answer as primidone here, especially when the vignette is designed around avoiding beta-blocker bronchospasm.

takeaway: Essential tremor: propranolol or primidone—choose primidone when asthma makes nonselective beta-blockers risky.

title: Moderate Alzheimer disease
stem:
A 74-year-old man with Alzheimer disease is taking donepezil.
His symptoms have progressed to moderate disease with worsening function.
The clinician wants to add an NMDA receptor antagonist to slow symptom progression.
question: Which medication is most appropriate to add?

rationales.A: Correct. Memantine is an NMDA receptor antagonist used in moderate-to-severe Alzheimer disease, often added to a cholinesterase inhibitor like donepezil.
rationales.B: Donepezil is already being used; the question asks for an add-on NMDA antagonist.
rationales.C: Risperidone is an antipsychotic sometimes used for severe agitation/psychosis but is not the requested cognitive add-on agent and carries risks in dementia.
rationales.D: Sertraline may treat depression/anxiety but does not target Alzheimer cognitive symptoms.
rationales.E: Meclizine treats vertigo/motion sickness and can worsen confusion in older adults due to anticholinergic effects.

takeaway: Alzheimer pharmacology: donepezil (AChE inhibitor) + memantine (NMDA antagonist) for moderate–severe disease.

title: Antiepileptic hyponatremia risk
stem:
A 38-year-old man with focal seizures needs an antiseizure medication.
The neurologist suggests a 'keto-analog' of carbamazepine that generally has fewer drug interactions,
but warns about hyponatremia.
question: Which medication best matches this description?

rationales.A: Correct. Oxcarbazepine is a carbamazepine derivative (keto-analog) used for focal seizures and is notable for hyponatremia risk.
rationales.B: Carbamazepine is related and used for focal seizures, but the vignette specifically describes the keto-analog (oxcarbazepine).
rationales.C: Phenytoin is a classic antiepileptic but is not the carbamazepine keto-analog described.
rationales.D: Valproic acid is a broad-spectrum agent but is not the carbamazepine keto-analog and has different key toxicities.
rationales.E: Levetiracetam is a newer antiepileptic with fewer interactions, but it is not the carbamazepine keto-analog described and is not classically linked to hyponatremia like oxcarbazepine.

takeaway: Oxcarbazepine is the carbamazepine cousin—remember hyponatremia as a high-yield adverse effect.

title: Migraine prevention
stem:
A 29-year-old woman has 6 migraine days per month despite appropriate acute therapy.
She wants a daily preventive medication.
PMH: obesity; she would prefer a medication that is not associated with weight gain.
question: Which medication is an appropriate migraine preventive option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Topiramate is used for migraine prevention and is often associated with weight loss, aligning with her preference.
rationales.B: Rizatriptan is an acute abortive medication, not a daily preventive.
rationales.C: Sumatriptan is an acute abortive medication, not a preventive.
rationales.D: Acetaminophen/butalbital/caffeine is used for acute headache and can contribute to medication-overuse headache; it is not a preventive therapy.
rationales.E: Ondansetron can treat nausea but does not prevent migraines.

takeaway: Migraine prevention ≠ triptans. Topiramate is a high-yield preventive option (often weight loss).

title: Breastfeeding contraception
stem:
A 28-year-old woman is 4 weeks postpartum and breastfeeding.
She wants an oral contraceptive that will not decrease milk production and avoids estrogen-related clot risk early postpartum.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Norethindrone is a progestin-only pill (“mini-pill”) and is commonly used during breastfeeding because it avoids estrogen effects on milk supply and postpartum thrombosis risk.
rationales.B: Combined estrogen/progestin pills (ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel) can decrease milk supply and carry increased clot risk early postpartum.
rationales.C: Drospirenone/ethinyl estradiol is also a combined estrogen/progestin pill and is not preferred this early postpartum while breastfeeding.
rationales.D: Depot medroxyprogesterone is a progestin-only option but it is an injection—not the oral option asked for in this question.
rationales.E: A levonorgestrel IUD is an excellent progestin-only option, but it is not an oral medication (the question asks for an oral contraceptive).

takeaway: Breastfeeding + early postpartum: progestin-only contraception is preferred; the progestin-only *pill* in the Top 300 is norethindrone.

title: MRA-induced gynecomastia
stem:
A 57-year-old man with HFrEF (EF 30%) is on guideline-directed therapy including:
- Sacubitril/valsartan
- Metoprolol succinate
- Spironolactone
He reports new breast tenderness and enlargement over the last 2 months.
K 4.6 mEq/L, SCr 1.0 mg/dL.
question: Which medication change best addresses his adverse effect while maintaining MRA benefit?

rationales.A: Correct. Spironolactone can cause gynecomastia due to antiandrogen effects. Eplerenone is a more selective MRA with less gynecomastia risk while preserving HF benefits.
rationales.B: Hydrochlorothiazide does not replace the mortality benefit of an MRA in HFrEF.
rationales.C: Digoxin can help symptoms/rate control but does not substitute for an MRA’s outcome benefits and does not address the gynecomastia mechanism.
rationales.D: Propranolol is not an evidence-based beta-blocker for HFrEF mortality benefit; metoprolol succinate is preferred.
rationales.E: Nifedipine is not a substitute for ARNI therapy in HFrEF and does not address the adverse effect.

takeaway: Spironolactone → gynecomastia. If MRA therapy is indicated, eplerenone is the common switch.

title: Pregnancy + ACE inhibitor
stem:
A 30-year-old woman is 8 weeks pregnant.
She has chronic hypertension and has been taking lisinopril 20 mg daily for several years.
BP today is 142/88 mmHg.
question: Which medication should be discontinued due to fetal toxicity risk?

rationales.A: Correct. ACE inhibitors (like lisinopril) are contraindicated in pregnancy due to risk of fetal renal toxicity and other malformations.
rationales.B: Nifedipine is commonly used for hypertension in pregnancy in many settings.
rationales.C: Thiazides are sometimes continued in select cases; they are not the classic pregnancy-contraindicated agent here.
rationales.D: Metoprolol is not the classic 'must stop' teratogenic drug like ACE inhibitors (though pregnancy medication choices are individualized).
rationales.E: Amlodipine is not the classic pregnancy-contraindicated drug in this list.

takeaway: ACE inhibitors/ARBs are high-yield contraindications in pregnancy—stop lisinopril.

title: Hypertension in pregnancy
stem:
A 27-year-old woman is 24 weeks pregnant and has persistent blood pressures around 150/95 mmHg.
PMH: asthma with frequent albuterol use.
She needs an oral antihypertensive option.
question: Which medication is an appropriate option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Nifedipine is a calcium channel blocker that is commonly used as an oral option for hypertension in pregnancy.
rationales.B: Lisinopril (ACE inhibitor) is contraindicated in pregnancy.
rationales.C: Losartan (ARB) is contraindicated in pregnancy.
rationales.D: Spironolactone is generally avoided in pregnancy due to antiandrogenic effects and potential fetal risk.
rationales.E: Warfarin is teratogenic and not used for routine hypertension management.

takeaway: Pregnancy HTN: avoid ACEi/ARB and warfarin; nifedipine is a classic safe option.

title: Chronic angina with low BP
stem:
A 63-year-old man has chronic stable angina despite:
- Metoprolol succinate 100 mg daily
- Nitroglycerin (sublingual) as needed
He cannot tolerate higher doses of beta-blocker because of fatigue and has borderline blood pressure (100/62 mmHg).
He wants an additional medication to reduce angina frequency without significantly lowering BP or HR.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ranolazine is used for chronic angina and has minimal effects on heart rate and blood pressure, making it useful when hypotension/bradycardia limit other options.
rationales.B: Long-acting nitrates can reduce angina but commonly lower blood pressure—undesirable with his borderline BP.
rationales.C: Amlodipine can help angina but may lower blood pressure.
rationales.D: Diltiazem lowers heart rate and blood pressure; it may worsen fatigue/bradycardia in someone already limited by beta-blocker effects.
rationales.E: Propranolol is not preferred for chronic stable angina in this setting and would further lower heart rate/blood pressure.

takeaway: If hypotension/bradycardia limit antianginals, ranolazine is a high-yield add-on because it doesn’t significantly lower BP/HR.

title: Severe CKD + AF anticoagulation
stem:
A 79-year-old man has nonvalvular atrial fibrillation and stage 5 CKD (eGFR 12 mL/min/1.73m²), not yet on dialysis.
He needs long-term stroke prevention anticoagulation.
question: Which anticoagulant is the best choice from the options below based on the most established use in severe CKD?

rationales.A: Correct. Warfarin has the longest history of use across advanced CKD stages and can be titrated by INR; many DOACs have limited data/labeling constraints in very low eGFR.
rationales.B: Apixaban may be used in some advanced CKD settings, but warfarin is the most established option in severe CKD with careful INR monitoring.
rationales.C: Rivaroxaban has more renal clearance concerns and is generally less favored as eGFR becomes very low.
rationales.D: Enoxaparin accumulates in severe renal impairment and is not a preferred long-term outpatient AF anticoagulant in eGFR 12.
rationales.E: Aspirin alone is not adequate stroke prevention therapy for atrial fibrillation.

takeaway: In very advanced CKD, warfarin remains a high-yield default anticoagulant choice (with INR monitoring) when DOAC use is uncertain.

title: Once-daily DOAC maintenance option
stem:
A 54-year-old woman is started on anticoagulation for acute DVT and will continue long-term.
She struggles with twice-daily medication adherence and prefers a once-daily oral anticoagulant regimen (after initial therapy as appropriate).
question: Which DOAC offers a once-daily maintenance dosing option for VTE treatment (after the initial dosing phase), potentially helping adherence?

rationales.A: Correct. Rivaroxaban has once-daily dosing as part of its maintenance regimen for VTE treatment (after initial dosing period), which can support adherence for some patients.
rationales.B: Apixaban is dosed twice daily for VTE/AF—less aligned with her preference.
rationales.C: Warfarin is once daily but is not a DOAC and requires frequent INR monitoring and variable dosing—this question is specifically targeting once-daily DOAC maintenance simplicity.
rationales.D: Enoxaparin requires injections and is not an oral once-daily maintenance anticoagulant in typical outpatient VTE treatment.
rationales.E: Aspirin is not a DOAC and does not treat acute DVT.

takeaway: When adherence is an issue, dosing frequency matters—rivaroxaban offers a once-daily maintenance option for VTE therapy.

title: Contact lens conjunctivitis
stem:
A 19-year-old contact lens wearer presents with acute unilateral eye redness, pain, and purulent discharge.
Vision is intact but he has significant irritation.
Bacterial conjunctivitis is suspected and Pseudomonas coverage is desired due to contact lens use.
question: Which medication provides a fluoroquinolone ophthalmic option appropriate for a contact lens wearer (Pseudomonas risk)?

rationales.A: Correct. Fluoroquinolone eye drops (like ofloxacin) are commonly used for contact lens–associated bacterial conjunctivitis because they provide Pseudomonas coverage.
rationales.B: Polymyxin B/trimethoprim can treat uncomplicated conjunctivitis but is less preferred when Pseudomonas coverage is a priority in contact lens wearers.
rationales.C: Tobramycin is an antibiotic eye drop, but it is not a fluoroquinolone; the question is specifically asking for a fluoroquinolone option for contact lens wearers.
rationales.D: Ketotifen treats allergic conjunctivitis, not bacterial infection.
rationales.E: Olopatadine also treats allergic conjunctivitis, not bacterial infection.

takeaway: Contact lens conjunctivitis raises Pseudomonas concern—think fluoroquinolone drops like ofloxacin.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Allergic conjunctivitis
stem:
A 24-year-old woman has bilateral itchy, watery eyes every spring.
She has no purulent discharge and no vision changes.
Exam shows conjunctival injection and clear tearing—consistent with allergic conjunctivitis.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Olopatadine is a topical antihistamine/mast cell stabilizer eye drop used for allergic conjunctivitis (itching, watery eyes).
rationales.B: Ofloxacin is an antibiotic eye drop used for bacterial infections, not allergic conjunctivitis.
rationales.C: Tobramycin is an antibiotic and does not treat allergic conjunctivitis.
rationales.D: Polymyxin B/trimethoprim is an antibiotic eye drop and does not treat allergic conjunctivitis.
rationales.E: Erythromycin is an antibiotic and does not treat allergic conjunctivitis.

takeaway: Itchy/watery eyes without purulence → allergic conjunctivitis → antihistamine drops like olopatadine.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Allergic conjunctivitis OTC option
stem:
A 30-year-old man has seasonal allergic conjunctivitis and wants an over-the-counter eye drop
that can relieve itching and redness.
question: Which medication best fits this request?

rationales.A: Correct. Ketotifen ophthalmic is a topical antihistamine/mast cell stabilizer used for allergic conjunctivitis and is commonly available OTC.
rationales.B: Ofloxacin is an antibiotic for bacterial conjunctivitis/keratitis, not allergy symptoms.
rationales.C: Dorzolamide is a glaucoma medication, not an allergy eye drop.
rationales.D: Timolol ophthalmic is for glaucoma and can have systemic beta-blocker effects; it does not treat allergic conjunctivitis.
rationales.E: Atropine is not used to treat allergic conjunctivitis and can cause pupil dilation and anticholinergic effects.

takeaway: For OTC allergy eye drops, ketotifen is a high-yield antihistamine/mast cell stabilizer.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Otitis externa
stem:
A 17-year-old swimmer has ear pain and tenderness when the tragus is pressed.
Otoscopy shows an edematous, erythematous external ear canal with debris.
Diagnosis: acute otitis externa.
question: Which medication is an appropriate treatment option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ciprofloxacin/dexamethasone otic drops provide topical antibiotic plus anti-inflammatory steroid therapy and are commonly used for otitis externa.
rationales.B: Cefdinir is an oral antibiotic used for some respiratory infections; otitis externa is treated topically in most cases.
rationales.C: Azithromycin is not standard for otitis externa and would be unnecessary systemic therapy.
rationales.D: Amoxicillin is used for otitis media, not otitis externa.
rationales.E: Oseltamivir treats influenza, not otitis externa.

takeaway: Otitis externa is usually treated topically—ciprofloxacin/dexamethasone otic is a classic option.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Otitis externa with concern for perforation
stem:
A 10-year-old boy has otitis externa after swimming.
His parents report he had a tympanic membrane perforation as a toddler, and they are worried about ear drops.
The clinician chooses an otic antibiotic with good safety for middle ear exposure.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ofloxacin otic is a non-ototoxic fluoroquinolone ear drop commonly used when tympanic membrane status is uncertain or perforation/tubes are present.
rationales.B: Neomycin-containing otic drops are generally avoided when TM perforation is possible due to ototoxicity risk.
rationales.C: Cefdinir is an oral antibiotic and is not standard first-line therapy for uncomplicated otitis externa, which is typically treated topically.
rationales.D: Amoxicillin treats otitis media and does not treat otitis externa effectively.
rationales.E: Cefuroxime is an oral antibiotic and is not standard first-line therapy for otitis externa.

takeaway: Possible TM perforation/tubes: choose a non-ototoxic fluoroquinolone ear drop (ofloxacin) and avoid neomycin-containing products.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Glaucoma add-on
stem:
A 67-year-old woman has primary open-angle glaucoma.
Despite latanoprost at night, her intraocular pressure remains above goal.
The ophthalmologist wants to add a topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitor.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Dorzolamide is a topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that lowers intraocular pressure by reducing aqueous humor production.
rationales.B: Bimatoprost is a prostaglandin analog like latanoprost; it’s not the carbonic anhydrase inhibitor class requested.
rationales.C: Brimonidine is an alpha-2 agonist used in glaucoma, but the question specifically targets carbonic anhydrase inhibitor therapy.
rationales.D: Timolol is a beta-blocker drop; effective but not a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor.
rationales.E: Atropine is not used to lower IOP and can worsen angle closure risk.

takeaway: Glaucoma add-ons include CAIs (dorzolamide), alpha-2 agonists (brimonidine), and beta-blocker drops (timolol).
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Glaucoma + asthma
stem:
A 62-year-old man has open-angle glaucoma and moderate asthma requiring albuterol most days.
The ophthalmologist is considering add-on eye drops to lower intraocular pressure.
question: Which medication should be avoided due to risk of bronchospasm from systemic beta-blockade?

rationales.A: Correct. Timolol ophthalmic can be systemically absorbed and act like a nonselective beta-blocker, potentially worsening asthma/bronchospasm.
rationales.B: Dorzolamide does not cause beta-blockade and is generally safe regarding bronchospasm.
rationales.C: Brimonidine is an alpha-2 agonist; it does not cause bronchospasm via beta-blockade.
rationales.D: Bimatoprost is a prostaglandin analog and is not associated with bronchospasm.
rationales.E: Latanoprost is a prostaglandin analog and is not associated with bronchospasm.

takeaway: Even eye drops can have systemic effects—timolol can worsen asthma via beta-blockade.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Pre-op skin antisepsis
stem:
A 45-year-old woman is preparing for elective surgery.
She is asked to use a topical antiseptic wash the night before and the morning of surgery to reduce skin bacteria.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Chlorhexidine is a broad antiseptic used for surgical skin preparation to reduce bacterial burden.
rationales.B: Hydroquinone is used for hyperpigmentation (e.g., melasma), not antisepsis.
rationales.C: Mupirocin treats localized skin infections/impetigo and is sometimes used for nasal decolonization, but it is not the typical full-body pre-op antiseptic wash.
rationales.D: Tretinoin is for acne/photoaging and does not provide antisepsis.
rationales.E: Clobetasol is a high-potency topical steroid and would not be used for antisepsis.

takeaway: Chlorhexidine is a high-yield antiseptic for surgical skin prep.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Actinic keratoses field therapy
stem:
A 71-year-old man has multiple rough, scaly lesions on his forehead diagnosed as actinic keratoses.
Dermatology recommends 'field therapy' to treat visible and subclinical lesions across the sun-damaged area.
question: Which medication is an appropriate topical field therapy option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Topical fluorouracil is commonly used as field therapy for actinic keratoses to target abnormal keratinocytes.
rationales.B: Hydroquinone is for hyperpigmentation and does not treat actinic keratoses.
rationales.C: Tretinoin can improve acne/photoaging but is not the classic primary field therapy for actinic keratoses.
rationales.D: Topical steroids reduce inflammation but do not treat actinic keratoses.
rationales.E: Mupirocin is an antibiotic for localized bacterial infection.

takeaway: Actinic keratoses across a sun-damaged field → topical fluorouracil is a classic field therapy.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Melasma/post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation
stem:
A 34-year-old woman has symmetric hyperpigmented patches on her cheeks that worsened after pregnancy.
Diagnosis: melasma.
She requests a topical depigmenting agent.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Hydroquinone is a topical skin-lightening agent used for hyperpigmentation such as melasma.
rationales.B: Topical fluorouracil is used for actinic keratoses, not melasma.
rationales.C: Clobetasol is a high-potency topical steroid and is not a depigmenting agent.
rationales.D: Chlorhexidine is an antiseptic wash, not a hyperpigmentation treatment.
rationales.E: Ketoconazole treats fungal infections, not melasma.

takeaway: Melasma/hyperpigmentation → hydroquinone is a high-yield topical depigmenting option (plus sun protection).
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Comedonal acne
stem:
A 17-year-old with comedonal acne is prescribed a topical retinoid.
After 1 week, he complains of dryness and mild peeling and asks if he should stop the medication.
question: Which medication is most appropriate for comedonal acne and commonly causes early dryness/peeling that improves with continued use and proper counseling?

rationales.A: Correct. Tretinoin is a topical retinoid used for comedonal acne; early irritation/dryness is common and patients should be counseled that benefits take weeks.
rationales.B: Clobetasol is a high-potency topical steroid and is not used for acne; it can worsen acne and cause skin atrophy if misused.
rationales.C: Mupirocin is a topical antibiotic for localized bacterial skin infections (e.g., impetigo), not comedonal acne.
rationales.D: Hydroquinone is a depigmenting agent for hyperpigmentation, not acne.
rationales.E: Chlorhexidine is an antiseptic wash and is not a primary treatment for comedonal acne.
