takeaway: Tretinoin is high-yield for comedonal acne; set expectations that early irritation is normal and improvement takes weeks.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Atopic dermatitis on body
stem:
A 9-year-old has atopic dermatitis with itchy, erythematous plaques on the arms and legs.
Symptoms persist despite emollients. Lesions are not on the face or groin.
question: Which topical corticosteroid is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Triamcinolone topical is a common medium-potency corticosteroid used for eczema flares on trunk/extremities.
rationales.B: Clobetasol is ultra–high potency and is generally avoided in children and in sensitive areas; it’s not the best first step here.
rationales.C: Betamethasone can be higher potency depending on formulation; triamcinolone is a classic medium-potency choice for this scenario.
rationales.D: Hydroquinone treats hyperpigmentation, not eczema inflammation.
rationales.E: Topical fluorouracil is used for actinic keratoses, not eczema.

takeaway: Eczema flares on body: a medium potency steroid like triamcinolone is a high-yield choice; reserve clobetasol for tougher plaques.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Plaque psoriasis
stem:
A 46-year-old man has chronic plaque psoriasis with thick, well-demarcated scaly plaques on the elbows and knees.
He needs a short course of a very high-potency topical corticosteroid for thick plaques.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Clobetasol is an ultra–high potency topical corticosteroid often used short-term for thick plaque psoriasis on elbows/knees (avoid face/groin).
rationales.B: Triamcinolone is medium potency and may be insufficient for thick plaques needing very high potency therapy.
rationales.C: Hydroquinone treats hyperpigmentation, not psoriasis.
rationales.D: Mupirocin treats bacterial skin infections, not psoriasis inflammation.
rationales.E: Chlorhexidine is an antiseptic and does not treat psoriasis.

takeaway: Thick plaque psoriasis on elbows/knees → short course clobetasol is a classic high-potency choice.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Mild nail fungus
stem:
A 40-year-old woman has mild distal toenail onychomycosis involving one nail.
She prefers to avoid oral therapy and wants a topical option.
question: Which medication is the best topical option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ciclopirox is a topical antifungal lacquer used for mild onychomycosis in select cases.
rationales.B: Terbinafine is the most effective oral option for dermatophyte onychomycosis, but she wants to avoid oral therapy.
rationales.C: Fluconazole is more commonly used for Candida; it is not the classic topical nail lacquer option.
rationales.D: Mupirocin is an antibiotic and does not treat fungal nail infection.
rationales.E: Clobetasol is a topical steroid and does not treat fungus.

takeaway: Mild onychomycosis and avoiding systemic therapy → ciclopirox topical lacquer is a high-yield option (though less effective than oral terbinafine).
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Iron deficiency anemia
stem:
A 27-year-old woman has fatigue and pica.
Labs: Hgb 9.8 g/dL, MCV 72 fL, ferritin 7 ng/mL.
She is started on oral iron therapy.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ferrous sulfate is a standard oral iron replacement for iron deficiency anemia (counsel on constipation, dark stools, and spacing from certain meds).
rationales.B: Potassium chloride treats hypokalemia, not iron deficiency anemia.
rationales.C: Vitamin D treats deficiency/osteoporosis support, not iron deficiency anemia.
rationales.D: PPIs can reduce iron absorption and do not treat iron deficiency.
rationales.E: Lactulose treats constipation/hepatic encephalopathy; it does not treat iron deficiency.

takeaway: Microcytic anemia + low ferritin → iron deficiency → treat with oral ferrous sulfate and counsel on GI effects and absorption.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300
Module 8a Acid Suppression (PPIs, H2 Blockers, Mucosal Protection)

title: Bone health deficiency in elderly
stem:
A 69-year-old woman has osteoporosis risk factors and reports limited sun exposure.
25-hydroxyvitamin D is 12 ng/mL (low).
question: Which medication is most appropriate to treat vitamin D deficiency from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) is used to treat vitamin D deficiency and support bone health.
rationales.B: Ferrous sulfate treats iron deficiency, not vitamin D deficiency.
rationales.C: Potassium chloride treats hypokalemia.
rationales.D: Denosumab treats osteoporosis but vitamin D deficiency should be corrected/supplemented; denosumab does not replace vitamin D repletion.
rationales.E: Alendronate treats osteoporosis but does not correct vitamin D deficiency.

takeaway: Low 25-OH vitamin D → treat with cholecalciferol; it’s foundational for bone health and osteoporosis therapy safety.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Uncomplicated bacterial conjunctivitis
stem:
A 6-year-old child has red eyes with purulent discharge and eyelids stuck shut in the morning.
No contact lens use. No vision changes.
Diagnosis: uncomplicated bacterial conjunctivitis.
question: Which medication is an appropriate first-line option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Polymyxin B/trimethoprim ophthalmic drops are commonly used for uncomplicated bacterial conjunctivitis in children.
rationales.B: Ketotifen treats allergic conjunctivitis (itching/watery), not bacterial purulent infection.
rationales.C: Olopatadine treats allergic conjunctivitis, not bacterial infection.
rationales.D: Azelastine nasal treats allergic rhinitis, not conjunctivitis.
rationales.E: Fluticasone nasal treats allergic rhinitis, not conjunctivitis.

takeaway: Purulent discharge without contact lenses → uncomplicated bacterial conjunctivitis → polymyxin/trimethoprim drops are a common first-line option.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Bacterial conjunctivitis alternative
stem:
A 45-year-old man has bacterial conjunctivitis with purulent discharge.
He needs an antibiotic eye drop.
question: Which medication is an ophthalmic antibiotic option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Tobramycin ophthalmic is an antibiotic eye drop used for bacterial conjunctivitis.
rationales.B: Timolol ophthalmic is for glaucoma, not infection.
rationales.C: Brimonidine is for glaucoma, not infection.
rationales.D: Dorzolamide/timolol is a glaucoma combo drop, not an antibiotic.
rationales.E: Bimatoprost is a glaucoma medication, not an antibiotic.

takeaway: When you need an antibiotic eye drop, tobramycin is a high-yield option (distinct from glaucoma drops).
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Cataract surgery risk
stem:
A 72-year-old man with BPH is scheduled for cataract surgery next month.
His urologist recently started a medication for urinary hesitancy and weak stream.
The ophthalmologist warns that one BPH medication is associated with intraoperative floppy iris syndrome.
question: Which medication is most associated with this complication?

rationales.A: Correct. Tamsulosin (an alpha-1 blocker) is strongly associated with intraoperative floppy iris syndrome; surgeons want to know about current or prior use.
rationales.B: Finasteride (5-alpha reductase inhibitor) is not the classic floppy iris culprit.
rationales.C: Dutasteride is also a 5-alpha reductase inhibitor and is not the classic floppy iris culprit.
rationales.D: Tadalafil is used for ED and sometimes BPH symptoms, but it is not linked to floppy iris syndrome like tamsulosin.
rationales.E: Mirabegron is used for overactive bladder and is not linked to floppy iris syndrome.

takeaway: Cataract surgery + BPH meds: tamsulosin is the classic floppy iris syndrome association—always disclose it.
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: BPH with enlarged prostate
stem:
A 66-year-old man has BPH symptoms and a markedly enlarged prostate on exam.
He prefers a medication that can shrink the prostate over time and reduce risk of urinary retention.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Dutasteride is a 5-alpha reductase inhibitor that reduces DHT and can shrink the prostate over months, improving symptoms and reducing progression risk.
rationales.B: Tamsulosin improves symptoms quickly by relaxing smooth muscle but does not shrink the prostate.
rationales.C: Mirabegron treats overactive bladder symptoms (urgency/frequency), not BPH obstruction.
rationales.D: Oxybutynin treats overactive bladder and can worsen urinary retention in BPH.
rationales.E: Phenazopyridine provides short-term dysuria relief and does not treat BPH.

takeaway: Big prostate + goal to shrink gland: 5-alpha reductase inhibitor (dutasteride/finasteride) is high-yield.
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: OAB with cognitive concerns
stem:
A 78-year-old woman has overactive bladder with urgency and urge incontinence.
PMH: mild cognitive impairment.
She wants medication but wants to avoid worsening memory or confusion.
question: Which medication is the best option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Mirabegron (a beta-3 agonist) treats overactive bladder without anticholinergic cognitive burden, making it preferred when cognition is a concern.
rationales.B: Oxybutynin is strongly anticholinergic and can worsen confusion and cognitive symptoms in older adults.
rationales.C: Solifenacin is also anticholinergic (though sometimes better tolerated than oxybutynin); it can still worsen cognition.
rationales.D: Diphenhydramine is anticholinergic and can worsen confusion; it does not treat OAB.
rationales.E: Dicyclomine is anticholinergic and can worsen cognition; it is used for GI spasm, not OAB.

takeaway: Older adults + OAB + cognitive concerns: mirabegron is a high-yield alternative to anticholinergic bladder meds.
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: Dysuria symptom relief
stem:
A 23-year-old woman is diagnosed with uncomplicated cystitis and is started on nitrofurantoin.
She asks if there is a medication she can take for the next 1–2 days to relieve burning with urination while the antibiotic starts working.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Phenazopyridine is a urinary analgesic used short-term for dysuria symptom relief; it can discolor urine orange/red.
rationales.B: Oxybutynin treats overactive bladder and does not treat acute infectious dysuria.
rationales.C: Tamsulosin treats BPH obstruction, not UTI dysuria.
rationales.D: Mirabegron treats OAB urgency, not UTI dysuria.
rationales.E: Dicyclomine treats GI spasm, not urinary pain.

takeaway: UTI discomfort bridge: phenazopyridine provides short-term urinary analgesia while antibiotics take effect.
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: PDE5 inhibitor + nitrates
stem:
A 60-year-old man with erectile dysfunction asks for a medication to improve erections.
PMH: chronic stable angina treated with isosorbide mononitrate daily.
question: Which medication is contraindicated due to risk of severe hypotension?

rationales.A: Correct. Sildenafil (a PDE5 inhibitor) is contraindicated with nitrates (like isosorbide mononitrate) due to potentially life-threatening hypotension.
rationales.B: Tamsulosin can cause orthostatic hypotension but is not the classic absolute contraindication with nitrates like PDE5 inhibitors are.
rationales.C: Finasteride does not cause the nitrate interaction.
rationales.D: Mirabegron does not interact dangerously with nitrates in this way.
rationales.E: Phenazopyridine does not interact with nitrates.

takeaway: Never mix nitrates with PDE5 inhibitors—sildenafil (and tadalafil) + nitrates can cause dangerous hypotension.
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: Estrogen therapy with uterus
stem:
A 52-year-old woman with bothersome hot flashes wants hormone therapy.
She has an intact uterus and no history of breast cancer or VTE.
The clinician plans to prescribe estradiol.
question: Which additional medication should be added to reduce the risk of endometrial hyperplasia/cancer?

rationales.A: Correct. In women with an intact uterus, adding a progestogen (progesterone) protects the endometrium when systemic estrogen (estradiol) is used.
rationales.B: Conjugated estrogens are another form of estrogen and do not provide endometrial protection; unopposed estrogen increases endometrial hyperplasia/cancer risk.
rationales.C: Testosterone is not used to protect the endometrium during estrogen therapy.
rationales.D: Raloxifene is a SERM used for osteoporosis/breast cancer risk reduction; it is not used as endometrial protection for systemic estrogen therapy.
rationales.E: Estradiol is the estrogen being prescribed; adding more estrogen does not protect the endometrium.

takeaway: Systemic estrogen + uterus → add progesterone for endometrial protection.

title: Postmenopausal osteoporosis + breast cancer risk
stem:
A 59-year-old postmenopausal woman has low bone density (T-score -2.6).
She has a strong family history of estrogen-receptor–positive breast cancer.
She does not have hot flashes and wants an osteoporosis medication that can also reduce breast cancer risk.
No history of DVT/PE.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Raloxifene is a SERM that helps prevent/treat osteoporosis and reduces risk of ER-positive breast cancer in certain patients.
rationales.B: Estradiol can help bone density but is systemic estrogen therapy and is not chosen primarily for breast cancer risk reduction; it can also have other risks.
rationales.C: Progesterone is not an osteoporosis treatment and does not reduce breast cancer risk.
rationales.D: Alendronate is a strong osteoporosis therapy but does not reduce breast cancer risk.
rationales.E: Denosumab treats osteoporosis but does not reduce breast cancer risk.

takeaway: Raloxifene is a high-yield SERM choice when osteoporosis treatment and breast cancer risk reduction align (but avoid with VTE history).

title: Cannot tolerate oral bisphosphonate
stem:
A 72-year-old woman with osteoporosis is prescribed alendronate.
After 2 doses, she develops severe esophageal irritation despite proper administration technique.
She wants a non-oral option with infrequent dosing.
Renal function: eGFR 60.
question: Which medication is the best alternative from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Zoledronic acid is an IV bisphosphonate (often yearly) and avoids esophageal irritation from oral bisphosphonates.
rationales.B: Risedronate is also an oral bisphosphonate and can still cause esophageal irritation.
rationales.C: Alendronate caused intolerance and remains oral.
rationales.D: Raloxifene is an osteoporosis option but is not a bisphosphonate and is generally less potent for fracture reduction than bisphosphonates; it also carries VTE risk.
rationales.E: Vitamin D is supportive but does not treat osteoporosis alone.

takeaway: Oral bisphosphonate intolerance → consider IV zoledronic acid for infrequent dosing and no esophageal exposure.

title: Osteoporosis with severe CKD
stem:
A 76-year-old woman has osteoporosis with a prior fragility fracture.
Renal function: eGFR 18 mL/min/1.73m².
The clinician wants an osteoporosis therapy that can be used in advanced CKD (with careful monitoring for hypocalcemia).
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Denosumab is not renally cleared and can be used in advanced CKD, though hypocalcemia risk is higher and monitoring/supplementation is important.
rationales.B: Zoledronic acid is a bisphosphonate that can be problematic in severe CKD and is often avoided or contraindicated at low eGFR.
rationales.C: Oral bisphosphonates like risedronate are generally not preferred in severe CKD due to renal concerns and limited data.
rationales.D: Alendronate is also generally avoided/not preferred in severe CKD.
rationales.E: Teriparatide can be used in some situations, but the classic CKD-friendly non-bisphosphonate highlighted for advanced CKD is denosumab (with calcium/vitamin D considerations).

takeaway: Severe CKD limits bisphosphonates—denosumab is a high-yield alternative (watch hypocalcemia).

title: Before denosumab
stem:
A 68-year-old woman is starting denosumab for osteoporosis.
Baseline labs show low 25-hydroxyvitamin D at 14 ng/mL.
The clinician wants to reduce her risk of denosumab-associated hypocalcemia.
question: Which medication should be started to correct her deficiency?

rationales.A: Correct. Vitamin D repletion (cholecalciferol) helps normalize calcium homeostasis and reduces hypocalcemia risk when starting antiresorptive therapy like denosumab.
rationales.B: Iron treats iron deficiency anemia and does not correct vitamin D deficiency.
rationales.C: Potassium chloride treats hypokalemia.
rationales.D: Alendronate is another osteoporosis therapy but does not correct vitamin D deficiency.
rationales.E: Famotidine is an H2 blocker and does not correct vitamin D deficiency.

takeaway: Before starting denosumab, check and replete vitamin D (and ensure adequate calcium intake) to reduce hypocalcemia risk.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Parkinson disease
stem:
A 61-year-old man has early Parkinson disease with resting tremor and bradykinesia.
He has mild symptoms and wants a medication option that directly stimulates dopamine receptors.
question: Which medication best fits this description from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Pramipexole is a dopamine agonist used in Parkinson disease (and restless legs).
rationales.B: Donepezil is an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor used for Alzheimer disease.
rationales.C: Memantine is an NMDA receptor antagonist used for Alzheimer disease.
rationales.D: Topiramate is used for migraine prevention and seizures, not Parkinson.
rationales.E: Meclizine is used for vertigo/motion sickness, not Parkinson disease.

takeaway: Parkinson dopamine agonists include pramipexole and ropinirole—high-yield class recognition.

title: Restless legs syndrome
stem:
A 52-year-old woman has an uncomfortable urge to move her legs at night that improves with movement.
Symptoms disrupt sleep and meet criteria for restless legs syndrome.
She wants a medication option that acts as a dopamine agonist.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ropinirole is a dopamine agonist used for restless legs syndrome.
rationales.B: Meclizine treats vertigo/motion sickness and does not treat RLS.
rationales.C: Melatonin can help sleep onset but does not treat the underlying RLS symptoms.
rationales.D: Gabapentin can sometimes be used for RLS, but the question specifically highlights a dopamine agonist choice; ropinirole is the classic answer.
rationales.E: Sertraline is an SSRI and can sometimes worsen/restless symptoms in some patients; it does not treat RLS.

takeaway: Restless legs syndrome: dopamine agonists (ropinirole/pramipexole) are high-yield options.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Enzyme induction
stem:
A 26-year-old woman with focal seizures is stable on carbamazepine.
She wants to start a combined oral contraceptive pill for birth control.
The clinician warns that one seizure medication can reduce oral contraceptive effectiveness by inducing hepatic enzymes.
question: Which medication is the interacting seizure medication?

rationales.A: Correct. Carbamazepine is a strong enzyme inducer and can reduce serum levels and effectiveness of hormonal contraceptives.
rationales.B: Levetiracetam has minimal drug interactions and is not a classic enzyme-inducing anticonvulsant.
rationales.C: Lamotrigine is not a strong enzyme inducer; rather, estrogen-containing OCPs can lower lamotrigine levels (the interaction direction is different).
rationales.D: Valproic acid is not a strong enzyme inducer like carbamazepine and does not typically reduce OCP effectiveness via enzyme induction.
rationales.E: Gabapentin has minimal pharmacokinetic interactions and is not a classic enzyme-inducing anticonvulsant.

takeaway: Carbamazepine is a high-yield enzyme inducer—watch interactions, including reduced effectiveness of combined OCPs.

title: Antiepileptic gingival hyperplasia
stem:
A 19-year-old man with epilepsy has been taking phenytoin for 8 months.
He reports swollen, overgrown gums and bleeding when brushing his teeth.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible?

rationales.A: Correct. Phenytoin is classically associated with gingival hyperplasia, along with other effects like hirsutism and ataxia.
rationales.B: Carbamazepine can cause leukopenia, hyponatremia, and rash, but gingival hyperplasia is more classic for phenytoin.
rationales.C: Valproic acid is associated with weight gain, tremor, hepatotoxicity, and teratogenicity—not gingival hyperplasia.
rationales.D: Levetiracetam is associated with behavioral changes/irritability, not gingival hyperplasia.
rationales.E: Lamotrigine is associated with rash including SJS risk, not gingival hyperplasia.

takeaway: Phenytoin = gingival hyperplasia is a classic board-style adverse effect.

title: Antiepileptic teratogenicity in pregnancy
stem:
A 28-year-old woman with epilepsy is 6 weeks pregnant.
Her seizures have been well controlled on valproic acid.
She asks which antiseizure medication carries the highest teratogenic risk and is generally avoided in pregnancy if possible.
question: Which medication is the best answer from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Valproic acid is strongly associated with major congenital malformations (including neural tube defects) and neurodevelopmental risk, making it a classic 'avoid if possible' in pregnancy.
rationales.B: Lamotrigine is often considered a safer option in pregnancy compared with valproate (though dosing/levels may change).
rationales.C: Levetiracetam is also commonly used as a relatively safer option in pregnancy compared with valproate.
rationales.D: Oxcarbazepine has fewer data than some agents and can cause hyponatremia; it is not the classic 'highest teratogenic risk' drug compared with valproate.
rationales.E: Topiramate carries teratogenic risk (e.g., cleft lip/palate), but valproate is the classic highest-risk antiseizure medication in this list.

takeaway: Pregnancy + epilepsy: valproic acid is a high-yield teratogen—avoid if possible; consider alternatives like lamotrigine/levetiracetam.

title: Antiepileptic rash risk with rapid titration
stem:
A 23-year-old woman recently started lamotrigine for bipolar disorder.
After 2 weeks, she develops a widespread rash with fever and mucosal involvement.
question: Which medication is most likely causing this high-risk reaction?

rationales.A: Correct. Lamotrigine can cause serious rash including Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis, especially early in therapy or with rapid titration.
rationales.B: Lithium has a different adverse effect profile (tremor, thyroid/kidney issues) and is not classically associated with SJS/TEN.
rationales.C: Bupropion is associated with seizure risk and insomnia; severe mucosal rash is not classic.
rationales.D: Sertraline can cause GI upset and sexual dysfunction; severe mucosal rash is not classic.
rationales.E: Quetiapine can cause sedation and metabolic effects; not classically SJS/TEN.

takeaway: Lamotrigine has a high-yield rare but severe rash risk (SJS/TEN)—patients need early rash counseling.

title: Antiepileptic behavioral adverse effect
stem:
A 16-year-old boy with seizures starts levetiracetam.
Over the next month, his parents report new irritability, mood swings, and aggression.
No fever or rash. Labs are normal.
question: Which medication is most likely causing these behavioral symptoms?

rationales.A: Correct. Levetiracetam is well-known for behavioral adverse effects such as irritability, agitation, and mood changes.
rationales.B: Phenytoin is associated with gingival hyperplasia, ataxia, and other neurologic effects rather than new-onset aggression as a classic hallmark.
rationales.C: Carbamazepine can cause hyponatremia, leukopenia, and rash; behavioral aggression is less classic than levetiracetam.
rationales.D: Valproic acid is associated with weight gain, tremor, hepatotoxicity, and teratogenicity; behavioral aggression is less classic.
rationales.E: Oxcarbazepine is associated with hyponatremia; behavioral aggression is less classic than levetiracetam.

takeaway: Levetiracetam’s high-yield side effect: behavioral changes/irritability.

title: TNF inhibitor safety
stem:
A 49-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis is starting a TNF inhibitor for better disease control.
She has no prior biologic therapy.
The clinician orders screening tests before initiating treatment because one class can reactivate latent tuberculosis.
question: Which medication is the TNF inhibitor in the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Etanercept is a TNF inhibitor and requires screening for latent TB and certain infections before starting.
rationales.B: Hydroxychloroquine is a DMARD but not a TNF inhibitor; its key monitoring is different (e.g., eye exams).
rationales.C: Methotrexate is a conventional DMARD, not a TNF inhibitor.
rationales.D: Prednisone is a corticosteroid, not a TNF inhibitor.
rationales.E: Allopurinol is used for chronic gout management, not RA biologic therapy.

takeaway: Biologic DMARDs like TNF inhibitors (etanercept, adalimumab) require latent TB screening—high-yield safety step.

title: Calcineurin inhibitor adverse effects
stem:
A 55-year-old kidney transplant recipient is taking immunosuppression.
He develops rising serum creatinine and worsening hypertension over several weeks.
The team suspects a calcineurin inhibitor adverse effect.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Cyclosporine (a calcineurin inhibitor) is classically associated with nephrotoxicity and hypertension (among other effects).
rationales.B: Mycophenolate is associated with GI effects and cytopenias/teratogenicity, not the classic nephrotoxicity + hypertension pattern.
rationales.C: Azathioprine can cause myelosuppression and hepatotoxicity; nephrotoxicity + hypertension is less classic than with calcineurin inhibitors.
rationales.D: Methotrexate is not used as a standard transplant immunosuppressant and has hepatic/pulmonary toxicity risks.
rationales.E: Hydroxychloroquine is a DMARD with retinal toxicity risk, not transplant nephrotoxicity/hypertension.

takeaway: Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) are high-yield for nephrotoxicity and hypertension—monitor renal function and BP.

title: Teratogenic immunosuppressant
stem:
A 29-year-old woman with lupus nephritis is taking immunosuppression and wants to become pregnant.
The rheumatologist reviews her medications and identifies one with strong teratogenicity that should be avoided in pregnancy.
question: Which medication is most concerning from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Mycophenolate is strongly teratogenic and is generally avoided in pregnancy; it requires careful reproductive counseling.
rationales.B: Azathioprine may be used in pregnancy in select autoimmune/transplant settings when benefits outweigh risks; it is not the classic 'strongly teratogenic' drug here.
rationales.C: Hydroxychloroquine is often continued in pregnancy for lupus because it can reduce flares.
rationales.D: Prednisone may be used in pregnancy when needed; it is not the classic severe teratogen in this list.
rationales.E: Tacrolimus may be used in certain pregnancy situations (e.g., transplant) under specialist care; it is not the classic 'must avoid due to strong teratogenicity' like mycophenolate.

takeaway: Mycophenolate is a high-yield teratogen—avoid in pregnancy and counsel on contraception/planning.

title: Spasticity
stem:
A 48-year-old man with multiple sclerosis has painful muscle spasticity affecting gait.
He wants an oral muscle relaxant commonly used for spasticity (not just acute back strain).
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Baclofen is a GABA-B agonist used for spasticity (e.g., MS, spinal cord injury).
rationales.B: Cyclobenzaprine is used for acute musculoskeletal spasm but is less targeted for chronic neurologic spasticity and can be very sedating/anticholinergic.
rationales.C: Methocarbamol is used for acute muscle spasm and is not the classic MS spasticity medication.
rationales.D: Naproxen treats pain/inflammation but does not directly reduce spasticity.
rationales.E: Tramadol treats pain but does not treat spasticity and carries seizure/serotonin risks.

takeaway: Neurologic spasticity (MS) → baclofen is a high-yield oral option.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Anaphylaxis
stem:
A 19-year-old woman with a known peanut allergy accidentally eats a cookie containing peanuts.
Within minutes she develops diffuse hives, lip swelling, wheezing, and dizziness.
Vitals: BP 86/54 mmHg, HR 128 bpm, RR 26/min, SpO2 92% on room air.
She is speaking in short sentences.
question: Which medication should be administered immediately as first-line treatment?

rationales.A: Correct. Intramuscular epinephrine is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis (hypotension + respiratory symptoms). It rapidly treats bronchospasm and supports blood pressure.
rationales.B: Diphenhydramine can help itching/hives but does not reverse airway edema or shock and should never delay epinephrine.
rationales.C: Albuterol can help bronchospasm but does not treat hypotension/airway edema; it is an adjunct after epinephrine.
rationales.D: Systemic steroids have delayed onset and are not first-line for immediate stabilization in anaphylaxis.
rationales.E: Famotidine (H2 blocker) may be used as an adjunct for skin symptoms but does not treat airway compromise or shock.

takeaway: Anaphylaxis = epinephrine first. Antihistamines, albuterol, and steroids are adjuncts—not substitutes.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Benzodiazepine oversedation
stem:
A 44-year-old man receives midazolam for a short endoscopy procedure.
After the procedure, he is difficult to arouse and has shallow respirations.
Vitals: BP 128/76 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, RR 8/min, SpO2 90% on room air.
There is no suspicion of opioid use and he has no seizure history.
question: Which medication can reverse benzodiazepine effects?

rationales.A: Correct. Flumazenil is a benzodiazepine receptor antagonist used to reverse benzodiazepine sedation (with important seizure risk caveats).
rationales.B: Naloxone reverses opioids, not benzodiazepines.
rationales.C: Epinephrine treats anaphylaxis/cardiac arrest physiology—not benzodiazepine oversedation.
rationales.D: Atropine treats symptomatic bradycardia—not benzodiazepine oversedation.
rationales.E: Adenosine treats certain SVTs—not benzodiazepine oversedation.

takeaway: Benzodiazepine oversedation → flumazenil (but use caution: can precipitate seizures in chronic benzo users or mixed overdoses).
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300
Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Narrow-complex SVT
stem:
A 26-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset palpitations and lightheadedness.
ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 190 bpm.
She is stable: BP 122/74 mmHg, no chest pain, no heart failure signs.
Vagal maneuvers are attempted without success.
question: Which medication is the best next step to acutely terminate this rhythm?

rationales.A: Correct. Adenosine is the classic rapid-acting medication used to terminate stable, regular narrow-complex SVT (AVNRT/AVRT) when vagal maneuvers fail.
rationales.B: Amiodarone is used for certain atrial/ventricular arrhythmias but is not first-line to terminate stable narrow-complex SVT.
rationales.C: Digoxin can help rate control in AF but does not acutely terminate SVT.
rationales.D: Beta-blockers can slow AV nodal conduction, but adenosine is the classic first-choice for acute termination in stable narrow-complex SVT.
rationales.E: Warfarin is for anticoagulation and does not treat acute SVT.

takeaway: Stable regular narrow-complex SVT + failed vagal maneuvers → adenosine is the classic acute termination drug.

title: Ventricular arrhythmia/rhythm control in structural heart disease
stem:
A 68-year-old man with ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF 25%) presents with recurrent episodes of symptomatic ventricular tachycardia.
He has an ICD but continues to receive shocks.
The electrophysiologist wants to start an antiarrhythmic that is effective in structural heart disease.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Amiodarone is commonly used for ventricular arrhythmias and rhythm control in patients with structural heart disease/HFrEF.
rationales.B: Flecainide is generally avoided in structural heart disease due to proarrhythmic risk.
rationales.C: Dronedarone is used for atrial fibrillation in select patients but is not used for VT and is contraindicated in certain HF settings.
rationales.D: Verapamil is not used to treat VT in this context and can worsen HFrEF via negative inotropy.
rationales.E: Digoxin is used for rate control in AF and does not treat VT.

takeaway: Structural heart disease + serious ventricular arrhythmia → amiodarone is a high-yield antiarrhythmic choice; class IC agents like flecainide are avoided.

title: AF with HFrEF and borderline BP
stem:
A 76-year-old man with HFrEF (EF 28%) presents with atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.
Vitals: BP 92/60 mmHg, HR 148 bpm, SpO2 96%.
He is not in cardiogenic shock but is borderline hypotensive.
The team wants rate control without significantly lowering blood pressure.
question: Which medication is the best option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Digoxin can provide ventricular rate control in AF and has minimal blood pressure lowering; it is often considered when hypotension limits beta-blockers/CCBs, especially in HFrEF.
rationales.B: Diltiazem can lower blood pressure and is generally avoided in decompensated HFrEF due to negative inotropy.
rationales.C: Verapamil is also negatively inotropic and can worsen HFrEF and hypotension.
rationales.D: Nifedipine is a dihydropyridine CCB used for hypertension/angina; it does not control AF rate and can cause hypotension.
rationales.E: Amlodipine does not control AF rate and may lower blood pressure.

takeaway: AF with RVR + HFrEF + borderline hypotension: digoxin is a classic rate-control option when CCBs are undesirable.

title: ACS with PCI
stem:
A 58-year-old man presents with NSTEMI and undergoes coronary stent placement.
He is being discharged on dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT).
PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia. No history of intracranial hemorrhage.
question: Which P2Y12 inhibitor is an appropriate component of DAPT after ACS with PCI from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ticagrelor is a P2Y12 inhibitor commonly used with aspirin as part of DAPT after ACS with stent placement.
rationales.B: Warfarin is an anticoagulant used for AF/VTE/valvular indications; it is not a substitute for a P2Y12 inhibitor in DAPT.
rationales.C: Apixaban is an anticoagulant and does not replace a P2Y12 inhibitor for stent-related platelet inhibition.
rationales.D: Enoxaparin is an anticoagulant used acutely for ACS/VTE, not the standard long-term oral P2Y12 component of DAPT.
rationales.E: Digoxin is for rate control/symptomatic HF and has no role in DAPT.

takeaway: After ACS + PCI, DAPT includes aspirin + a P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor is a high-yield option).

title: Secondary stroke prevention with aspirin intolerance
stem:
A 67-year-old woman had an ischemic stroke 3 months ago.
She was started on aspirin but developed true hypersensitivity (urticaria and angioedema).
She needs an alternative antiplatelet for secondary stroke prevention.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet agent used for secondary prevention of ischemic stroke and is a common alternative when aspirin cannot be used.
rationales.B: Rivaroxaban is an anticoagulant and is not the standard replacement for antiplatelet therapy in non-cardioembolic stroke prevention.
rationales.C: Warfarin is used for cardioembolic stroke prevention (e.g., AF), not routine replacement for aspirin in non-cardioembolic stroke.
rationales.D: Enoxaparin is an injectable anticoagulant and is not used for long-term secondary stroke prevention in this scenario.
rationales.E: Aspirin is contraindicated due to her true hypersensitivity.

takeaway: If aspirin can’t be used for secondary stroke prevention, clopidogrel is a high-yield alternative antiplatelet.

title: Acute DVT in pregnancy
stem:
A 32-year-old woman who is 18 weeks pregnant presents with unilateral leg swelling and pain.
Ultrasound confirms proximal DVT.
She has no history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
question: Which anticoagulant is most appropriate for treatment during pregnancy from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin) is commonly used for DVT treatment in pregnancy because it does not cross the placenta.
rationales.B: Warfarin is teratogenic and is generally avoided during pregnancy.
rationales.C: DOACs like rivaroxaban are generally avoided in pregnancy due to limited safety data and potential fetal risk.
rationales.D: Apixaban is also generally avoided in pregnancy for the same reasons.
rationales.E: Aspirin does not treat acute DVT.

takeaway: Pregnancy + acute DVT: enoxaparin is a high-yield treatment choice; avoid warfarin and most DOACs.

title: HF volume overload with poor oral absorption
stem:
A 71-year-old man with chronic HFrEF reports worsening edema and shortness of breath.
He is taking furosemide 80 mg twice daily, but has minimal diuresis.
He also has gut edema and inconsistent oral absorption is suspected.
Creatinine is stable.
question: Which loop diuretic is a reasonable switch for more reliable oral bioavailability from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Torsemide is a loop diuretic with more consistent oral bioavailability than furosemide and is often used when absorption is unreliable.
rationales.B: Hydrochlorothiazide is a thiazide diuretic and is less effective than a loop for significant HF-related volume overload.
rationales.C: Chlorthalidone is also a thiazide-like diuretic and is not the primary switch for diuretic resistance due to poor furosemide absorption.
rationales.D: Spironolactone is an MRA with mortality benefit in HFrEF but is not a potent diuretic for rapid decongestion.
rationales.E: Amlodipine does not treat volume overload.

takeaway: If furosemide isn’t working due to absorption issues, torsemide is a high-yield loop diuretic switch.

title: Severe edema needing potent loop diuresis
stem:
A 64-year-old woman with decompensated heart failure has marked lower-extremity edema and pulmonary congestion.
She has chronic kidney disease and requires a potent loop diuretic.
The clinician selects a loop diuretic with high potency per mg.
question: Which medication best fits this role from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Bumetanide is a loop diuretic with high potency per mg and is used for significant volume overload, including in patients with reduced kidney function.
rationales.B: Hydrochlorothiazide is a thiazide diuretic and is not potent enough alone for severe HF congestion.
rationales.C: Chlorthalidone is a thiazide-like diuretic and is not used as the primary agent for severe decompensated HF congestion.
rationales.D: Eplerenone provides neurohormonal benefit but is not a potent decongestive diuretic.
rationales.E: Atenolol is a beta-blocker and does not treat edema.

takeaway: For significant HF congestion, loop diuretics are key; bumetanide is a high-potency loop option.

title: HFrEF beta-blocker selection
stem:
A 59-year-old man has chronic HFrEF (EF 32%) and hypertension.
He is clinically stable and euvolemic on sacubitril/valsartan and a loop diuretic.
The clinician wants to add an evidence-based beta-blocker that also provides alpha-1 blockade to lower blood pressure.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Carvedilol is an evidence-based beta-blocker for HFrEF and also blocks alpha-1 receptors, which can help lower blood pressure.
rationales.B: Atenolol is not one of the classic evidence-based beta-blockers shown to improve mortality in HFrEF.
rationales.C: Propranolol is nonselective and not an evidence-based HFrEF beta-blocker.
rationales.D: Metoprolol tartrate is not the HFrEF mortality-benefit formulation; metoprolol succinate is preferred for HFrEF.
rationales.E: Verapamil is a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker and can worsen HFrEF due to negative inotropy.

takeaway: Evidence-based HFrEF beta-blockers include carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, and bisoprolol—carvedilol also lowers BP via alpha-1 blockade.

title: HFrEF + hypertension
stem:
A 63-year-old woman with HFrEF (EF 30%) and chronic hypertension asks about adding a calcium channel blocker for blood pressure control.
She is already on sacubitril/valsartan and a beta-blocker.
question: Which calcium channel blocker should be avoided because it can worsen systolic heart failure?

rationales.A: Correct. Verapamil is a non-dihydropyridine CCB that is negatively inotropic and can worsen HFrEF.
rationales.B: Amlodipine is a dihydropyridine CCB and is generally considered safer in HFrEF if a CCB is needed for hypertension.
rationales.C: Nifedipine is also a dihydropyridine CCB and does not have the same negative inotropy as verapamil.
rationales.D: Chlorthalidone is a thiazide-like diuretic, not a CCB.
rationales.E: Losartan is an ARB, not a CCB.

takeaway: Non-DHP CCBs (verapamil, diltiazem) are generally avoided in HFrEF due to negative inotropy; DHP CCBs like amlodipine are safer if needed.

title: ACE inhibitor cough
stem:
A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and hypertension is started on lisinopril for renal protection.
After 3 weeks, he develops a persistent dry cough.
He has no angioedema and wants to continue kidney-protective therapy.
question: Which medication is the best alternative from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. ARBs (like irbesartan) provide similar renal and BP benefits as ACE inhibitors but are less likely to cause cough.
rationales.B: Continuing lisinopril would likely perpetuate ACE-inhibitor–associated cough.
rationales.C: Thiazides lower BP but do not provide the same RAAS-mediated renal protective effect as ACEi/ARB in diabetic kidney disease.
rationales.D: Amlodipine lowers BP but does not replace RAAS blockade renal benefits.
rationales.E: Atenolol lowers BP/HR but does not provide the same renal protective benefit.

takeaway: ACE inhibitor cough → switch to an ARB (e.g., irbesartan) to maintain RAAS benefit.

title: Post-MI with reduced EF
stem:
A 62-year-old man is 1 week post-myocardial infarction.
Echo shows EF 38%.
He is on aspirin, a high-intensity statin, and a beta-blocker.
BP is 128/76 mmHg, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, potassium 4.3 mEq/L.
question: Which medication should be added to improve long-term outcomes from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. ACE inhibitors like ramipril improve outcomes after MI, especially with reduced EF, by reducing remodeling and lowering cardiovascular risk.
rationales.B: Nifedipine can treat hypertension/angina but does not provide the same post-MI remodeling benefit as ACE inhibitors.
rationales.C: Diltiazem is not a standard post-MI mortality-reducing therapy and can be problematic in reduced EF due to negative inotropy.
rationales.D: Ibuprofen is an NSAID used for pain and does not improve post-MI outcomes; NSAIDs can increase cardiovascular risk.
rationales.E: Hydroxyzine treats anxiety/itching and is not cardiovascular secondary prevention.

takeaway: After MI—especially with reduced EF—ACE inhibitors (e.g., ramipril) are high-yield outcome-improving therapy when tolerated.

title: Statin myopathy trigger
stem:
A 63-year-old man takes simvastatin 40 mg nightly for hyperlipidemia.
He is prescribed an antibiotic for community-acquired pneumonia.
Three days later, he develops diffuse muscle pain and weakness.
Labs: CK 2,800 U/L (elevated), creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (baseline 0.9).
question: Which newly started medication most likely precipitated this interaction?

rationales.A: Correct. Clarithromycin is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor and can markedly increase simvastatin levels, raising risk of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis.
rationales.B: Azithromycin has much less CYP3A4 inhibition and is less likely to cause severe statin level increases compared with clarithromycin.
rationales.C: Doxycycline is not a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor and is not a classic cause of simvastatin toxicity.
rationales.D: Amoxicillin does not cause this statin interaction.
rationales.E: Nitrofurantoin is used for cystitis and is not a CYP3A4 inhibitor that increases simvastatin levels.

takeaway: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin) + simvastatin/lovastatin → high risk myopathy/rhabdo; hold/switch statin or antibiotic.

title: Statin choice with many drug interactions
stem:
A 70-year-old man with ASCVD needs statin therapy but takes multiple interacting medications.
His clinician wants a statin with fewer CYP3A4 interactions to reduce risk of myopathy when other medications change.
question: Which statin is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Pravastatin has fewer CYP3A4 interactions than simvastatin or lovastatin and is commonly chosen when drug–drug interactions are a concern.
rationales.B: Simvastatin is a common culprit in CYP3A4-mediated interactions and myopathy risk.
rationales.C: Lovastatin is also heavily affected by CYP3A4 inhibitors.
rationales.D: Atorvastatin has some CYP3A4 metabolism; it generally has fewer interaction issues than simvastatin/lovastatin but more than pravastatin.
rationales.E: Clarithromycin is an antibiotic, not a statin.

takeaway: If drug interactions are a major concern, pravastatin is a high-yield statin with fewer CYP3A4 issues than simvastatin/lovastatin.

title: Statin administration counseling
stem:
A 52-year-old man is started on a statin for hyperlipidemia.
At follow-up, LDL reduction is less than expected.
He admits he takes the medication every morning on an empty stomach.
The clinician suspects he is taking a statin whose absorption is improved with food and that is typically taken with the evening meal.
question: Which statin best fits this description?

rationales.A: Correct. Lovastatin is typically taken with the evening meal to improve absorption (a classic counseling point).
rationales.B: Rosuvastatin can generally be taken any time of day with or without food.
rationales.C: Atorvastatin can generally be taken any time of day with or without food.
rationales.D: Pravastatin can generally be taken with or without food; it does not have the classic 'must take with evening meal' counseling point like lovastatin.
rationales.E: Simvastatin is typically taken in the evening, but the classic 'take with food' absorption counseling point is most associated with lovastatin.

takeaway: Not all statins have the same administration counseling—lovastatin is the classic 'take with the evening meal' statin.

title: Very high LDL despite maximal therapy
stem:
A 48-year-old man with familial hypercholesterolemia has LDL 190 mg/dL despite:
- Maximally tolerated high-intensity statin
- Ezetimibe
He has established ASCVD.
The clinician wants to add an injectable PCSK9 inhibitor to further lower LDL and CV risk.
question: Which medication is an appropriate choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Alirocumab is a PCSK9 inhibitor used for very high LDL levels and can significantly lower LDL when added to statin ± ezetimibe.
rationales.B: Gemfibrozil primarily lowers triglycerides and can increase myopathy risk when combined with some statins; it does not provide the large LDL reduction expected from PCSK9 inhibitors.
rationales.C: Fenofibrate primarily lowers triglycerides; it is not the next-step therapy for severe LDL elevation on statin + ezetimibe.
rationales.D: Icosapent ethyl reduces triglycerides and CV events in select patients but is not a primary LDL-lowering agent like PCSK9 inhibitors.
rationales.E: Digoxin is not a lipid medication.

takeaway: ASCVD + very high LDL despite statin + ezetimibe → consider a PCSK9 inhibitor (alirocumab/evolocumab).
module_tags: Module 2b Non-Statin Lipids (Ezetimibe, PCSK9, Fibrates, Omega-3)

title: Diabetes medication in advanced CKD
stem:
A 72-year-old man with type 2 diabetes is taking metformin 1,000 mg twice daily.
His kidney function has declined.
Labs: eGFR 24 mL/min/1.73m².
He asks which diabetes medication needs to be stopped because of increased risk of lactic acidosis in advanced CKD.
question: Which medication should be discontinued?

rationales.A: Correct. Metformin should generally be avoided or discontinued in advanced CKD (very low eGFR) due to increased risk of lactic acidosis.
rationales.B: Sitagliptin can be used with renal dose adjustment; it is not the classic 'must stop' due to lactic acidosis risk.
rationales.C: Basal insulin can be used in CKD (with careful dose adjustment) and does not carry metformin’s lactic acidosis concern.
rationales.D: SGLT2 inhibitor use depends on indication and eGFR thresholds; the classic medication that must be stopped for lactic acidosis concern is metformin.
rationales.E: Glipizide can be used cautiously (hypoglycemia risk increases in CKD), but it is not the metformin-specific lactic acidosis issue.

takeaway: Advanced CKD is a high-yield reason to stop metformin due to lactic acidosis risk; other agents may be adjusted instead.

title: T2DM + HFrEF
stem:
A 60-year-old man has type 2 diabetes (A1c 8.1%) and HFrEF (EF 35%).
He is on metformin and guideline-directed HF therapy.
Renal function: eGFR 58.
The clinician wants a diabetes medication that also provides heart failure benefit.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Dapagliflozin (an SGLT2 inhibitor) improves heart failure outcomes and provides glucose lowering in appropriate patients.
rationales.B: Pioglitazone can cause fluid retention and may worsen heart failure.
rationales.C: Glipizide lowers glucose but does not provide the same HF outcome benefits and carries hypoglycemia risk.
rationales.D: Sitagliptin is weight neutral and lowers glucose modestly but does not provide the same HF outcome benefits as SGLT2 inhibitors.
rationales.E: Regular insulin can lower glucose but does not provide HF outcome benefits and increases hypoglycemia/weight gain risk.

takeaway: In T2DM with HFrEF, SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin) are high-yield choices for both glucose and HF benefit.

title: T2DM with ASCVD and obesity
stem:
A 54-year-old woman has type 2 diabetes (A1c 8.4%) and obesity (BMI 36).
She also has established ASCVD (prior MI).
She wants a medication that can improve glycemic control and support weight loss while reducing cardiovascular risk.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Liraglutide is a GLP-1 receptor agonist that improves glycemic control, promotes weight loss, and has cardiovascular benefit in appropriate patients with ASCVD.
rationales.B: Glimepiride can lower glucose but increases hypoglycemia risk and tends to cause weight gain.
rationales.C: Pioglitazone can cause weight gain and edema and does not provide the same weight-loss benefit as GLP-1 agents.
rationales.D: Basal insulin can lower glucose but often causes weight gain and does not provide the same CV/weight-loss profile as GLP-1 agents.
rationales.E: Doxepin is not a diabetes medication.

takeaway: T2DM + obesity + ASCVD: GLP-1 agonists like liraglutide are high-yield for weight loss and CV risk reduction.

title: Clopidogrel + GERD
stem:
A 65-year-old man had a coronary stent placed 2 months ago and is taking clopidogrel.
He has symptomatic GERD and needs a proton pump inhibitor.
The clinician wants to minimize reduction of clopidogrel activation by avoiding strong CYP2C19 inhibition.
question: Which acid-suppressing medication is preferred from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Pantoprazole is often preferred when a PPI is needed with clopidogrel because it has less CYP2C19 inhibition than omeprazole/esomeprazole.
rationales.B: Omeprazole can inhibit CYP2C19 and may reduce conversion of clopidogrel to its active metabolite.
rationales.C: Esomeprazole is similar to omeprazole and can also inhibit CYP2C19, potentially reducing clopidogrel effect.
rationales.D: Lansoprazole may have less interaction than omeprazole, but pantoprazole is a classic preferred choice when minimizing interaction is the key goal.
rationales.E: Famotidine is an H2 blocker and may help GERD, but the question specifically asks for a PPI choice while minimizing the clopidogrel interaction.

takeaway: If a patient on clopidogrel needs a PPI, pantoprazole is a high-yield preferred option to reduce CYP2C19 interaction concerns.
module_tags: Module 8a Acid Suppression (PPIs, H2 Blockers, Mucosal Protection)

title: Hyperthyroidism in 1st trimester
stem:
A 29-year-old woman is 9 weeks pregnant and is diagnosed with Graves disease.
Symptoms: weight loss, tremor, palpitations.
Labs: suppressed TSH, elevated free T4.
The clinician wants an antithyroid drug preferred in the first trimester.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. PTU is typically preferred in the first trimester due to methimazole-associated embryopathy risk early in pregnancy (therapy decisions are individualized).
rationales.B: Methimazole is generally preferred outside the first trimester but is often avoided early in pregnancy due to embryopathy risk.
rationales.C: Levothyroxine treats hypothyroidism, not hyperthyroidism.
rationales.D: Liothyronine is thyroid hormone replacement and would worsen hyperthyroidism.
rationales.E: Atenolol can help symptoms (palpitations/tremor) but does not treat thyroid hormone overproduction.

takeaway: Graves disease in early pregnancy: PTU is the classic first-trimester antithyroid choice; methimazole is commonly used after the first trimester.

title: Primary hypothyroidism
stem:
A 46-year-old woman has fatigue, weight gain, constipation, and cold intolerance.
Labs: TSH 18 mIU/L (high), free T4 low.
Diagnosis: primary hypothyroidism.
question: Which medication is the most appropriate long-term therapy from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Levothyroxine (T4) is the standard first-line replacement therapy for primary hypothyroidism.
rationales.B: Liothyronine (T3) is not routine monotherapy for hypothyroidism and can cause more fluctuations and cardiac effects.
rationales.C: Methimazole treats hyperthyroidism, not hypothyroidism.
rationales.D: PTU treats hyperthyroidism, not hypothyroidism.
rationales.E: Propranolol can reduce hyperthyroid symptoms but does not treat hypothyroidism.

takeaway: Primary hypothyroidism → replace with levothyroxine (T4) and titrate to target TSH.

title: Graves disease (not pregnant)
stem:
A 33-year-old woman is diagnosed with Graves disease.
She is not pregnant and has no plans for pregnancy in the next year.
Labs: TSH suppressed, free T4 elevated.
The clinician plans antithyroid therapy as first-line pharmacologic treatment.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Methimazole is generally preferred over PTU for most nonpregnant adults due to a more favorable safety profile and once-daily dosing in many cases.
rationales.B: PTU is often reserved for first-trimester pregnancy or thyroid storm (due to additional inhibition of peripheral conversion), because of hepatotoxicity risk.
rationales.C: Levothyroxine treats hypothyroidism, not Graves hyperthyroidism.
rationales.D: Liothyronine is thyroid hormone and would worsen hyperthyroidism.
rationales.E: Dexamethasone can reduce peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 and is used in thyroid storm settings, not as long-term standalone Graves therapy.

takeaway: Most nonpregnant Graves patients: methimazole is the high-yield first-line antithyroid drug; PTU is for specific situations.

title: Acute MS relapse
stem:
A 37-year-old woman with multiple sclerosis presents with new vision changes and weakness for 3 days.
MRI suggests an acute inflammatory relapse.
The neurologist wants short-term high-dose corticosteroids to hasten recovery.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. High-dose IV methylprednisolone is commonly used for acute MS relapses to reduce inflammation and speed symptom recovery.
rationales.B: Hydrocortisone is a corticosteroid but is not the classic high-dose IV regimen used for MS relapses.
rationales.C: Prednisone is often used orally for many conditions, but acute MS relapses are classically treated with high-dose IV methylprednisolone.
rationales.D: Dexamethasone is a corticosteroid with different typical uses; methylprednisolone is the high-yield MS relapse choice.
rationales.E: Ibuprofen can treat pain but does not treat an MS inflammatory relapse.

takeaway: Acute MS relapse → high-dose IV methylprednisolone is the classic steroid regimen.

title: Smoking cessation + depression
stem:
A 41-year-old man smokes 1 pack per day and wants to quit.
He also has depressive symptoms and prefers a non-nicotine oral medication that can help with cravings.
He has no seizure history and does not have an eating disorder.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Bupropion can aid smoking cessation and treats depression; it is a common non-nicotine oral option for quitting smoking (avoid in seizure/eating disorder).
rationales.B: Sertraline treats depression/anxiety but is not a primary smoking cessation medication.
rationales.C: Clonazepam is a benzodiazepine and is not used for smoking cessation; it carries dependence risk.
rationales.D: Hydroxyzine can help anxiety but does not treat nicotine dependence.
rationales.E: Zolpidem treats insomnia and does not help smoking cessation.

takeaway: Bupropion is a high-yield dual-purpose option: depression treatment + smoking cessation support (screen for seizure/eating disorder).
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: SSRI discontinuation symptoms
stem:
A 29-year-old woman has been taking paroxetine for generalized anxiety disorder.
She stops it abruptly because she feels better.
Two days later she develops dizziness, nausea, “electric shock” sensations, and irritability.
question: Which medication is most associated with prominent discontinuation syndrome due to a short half-life?

rationales.A: Correct. Paroxetine has a relatively short half-life and is classically associated with SSRI discontinuation syndrome if stopped abruptly.
rationales.B: Fluoxetine has a long half-life, so discontinuation symptoms are less common/severe.
rationales.C: Sertraline can cause discontinuation symptoms, but paroxetine is more classically associated with severe discontinuation due to short half-life.
rationales.D: Escitalopram can cause discontinuation symptoms, but paroxetine is a classic high-yield culprit.
rationales.E: Bupropion is not an SSRI and does not cause SSRI discontinuation syndrome.

takeaway: Short half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) → higher discontinuation syndrome risk; taper instead of abrupt stopping.

title: Depression with insomnia and low appetite
stem:
A 38-year-old woman has major depressive disorder with prominent insomnia and poor appetite with weight loss.
She prefers a medication that can help sleep and appetite.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Mirtazapine is sedating and can increase appetite/weight—useful when depression is accompanied by insomnia and low appetite.
rationales.B: Bupropion is more activating and can worsen insomnia; it is less likely to increase appetite.
rationales.C: Fluoxetine can be activating and may worsen insomnia early in treatment.
rationales.D: Venlafaxine can be activating and does not specifically help appetite; it may worsen insomnia.
rationales.E: Atomoxetine is used for ADHD and does not treat depression.

takeaway: Match antidepressant choice to symptoms: mirtazapine is a high-yield option for depression + insomnia + weight loss.
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: Sleep maintenance insomnia
stem:
A 52-year-old man has chronic insomnia characterized by frequent nighttime awakenings.
He wants a non-benzodiazepine option and prefers to avoid medications with high dependence risk.
question: Which medication is an appropriate option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Low-dose doxepin is used for sleep maintenance insomnia and is not a benzodiazepine (counsel about sedation).
rationales.B: Alprazolam is a benzodiazepine with high dependence risk and is not preferred for chronic insomnia.
rationales.C: Midazolam is used for procedural sedation, not chronic insomnia management.
rationales.D: Diazepam is a benzodiazepine and is not preferred for chronic insomnia due to dependence and long half-life effects.
rationales.E: Clonazepam is a benzodiazepine and is not preferred as a primary chronic insomnia medication.

takeaway: For sleep maintenance insomnia, low-dose doxepin is a high-yield non-benzodiazepine option.
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: Active seizure in the field
stem:
EMS arrives to find a 35-year-old man actively seizing for 6 minutes.
IV access is not yet established.
The paramedic needs a fast-acting benzodiazepine that can be given intramuscularly to stop the seizure.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Midazolam is a benzodiazepine that can be administered IM/intranasal and is commonly used for acute seizure termination when IV access is not available.
rationales.B: Zolpidem is a hypnotic for insomnia and does not treat acute seizures.
rationales.C: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines and would worsen ongoing seizure control.
rationales.D: Phenytoin is a maintenance/second-line antiseizure medication and is not the immediate first-line abortive agent in the field without IV access.
rationales.E: Melatonin is a sleep aid and does not treat seizures.

takeaway: Active seizure/status epilepticus: benzodiazepines first. If no IV access, midazolam IM is a high-yield choice.

title: Panic disorder short-term adjunct
stem:
A 28-year-old woman has panic disorder and is starting an SSRI.
She has frequent panic attacks and needs a short-term adjunct medication while the SSRI takes effect.
Her clinician chooses a longer-acting benzodiazepine.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Clonazepam is a longer-acting benzodiazepine sometimes used short-term as a bridge for panic symptoms while an SSRI begins working (with dependence counseling).
rationales.B: Fluoxetine is an SSRI and can treat panic disorder, but it takes weeks to work and is not the immediate short-term bridge medication requested.
rationales.C: Buspirone can help generalized anxiety but is not typically used for immediate panic attack control and also takes time to work.
rationales.D: Hydroxyzine can reduce anxiety but is not the classic bridge medication for panic disorder and can be sedating.
rationales.E: Zolpidem treats insomnia, not panic attacks.

takeaway: SSRIs take weeks; short-term benzodiazepine bridge therapy (e.g., clonazepam) may be used cautiously for panic disorder.
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: Migraine with CAD
stem:
A 57-year-old man has episodic migraines with severe unilateral throbbing headache and photophobia.
PMH: coronary artery disease with prior stent.
He needs an acute migraine treatment option that avoids vasoconstriction.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Rimegepant is a CGRP receptor antagonist used for acute migraine treatment and does not cause the vasoconstriction associated with triptans.
rationales.B: Sumatriptan is a triptan and can cause vasoconstriction; it is generally avoided in significant CAD.
rationales.C: Rizatriptan is also a triptan with vasoconstrictive effects and is generally avoided in significant CAD.
rationales.D: Isosorbide mononitrate is an antianginal nitrate and does not treat migraines; it can even cause headaches.
rationales.E: Aspirin may help mild pain but is not a targeted acute migraine therapy like rimegepant, and the key point here is avoiding vasoconstriction.

takeaway: If triptans are contraindicated (CAD), CGRP antagonists like rimegepant are high-yield acute migraine options.

title: Opioid overdose
stem:
A 33-year-old man is found unresponsive with shallow breathing.
Vitals: RR 6/min, SpO2 86% on room air, pinpoint pupils.
Friends report he took oxycodone for back pain and may have taken extra doses.
question: Which medication should be administered immediately?

rationales.A: Correct. Naloxone is an opioid antagonist used to rapidly reverse opioid-induced respiratory depression.
rationales.B: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, not opioids.
rationales.C: Epinephrine is for anaphylaxis and does not reverse opioid respiratory depression.
rationales.D: Atropine treats bradycardia and does not reverse opioid effects.
rationales.E: Adenosine treats SVT and does not reverse opioid effects.

takeaway: Opioid overdose (respiratory depression + miosis) → naloxone.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts
Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Acute moderate-to-severe pain
stem:
A 45-year-old man is discharged after an outpatient orthopedic procedure.
He has acute moderate-to-severe pain despite scheduled ibuprofen and acetaminophen.
He needs a short course of an opioid combination product for breakthrough pain.
question: Which medication is the best option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Acetaminophen/oxycodone is a common short-term option for acute moderate-to-severe pain when nonopioids are insufficient.
rationales.B: Acetaminophen/butalbital/caffeine is used for certain headache disorders and can cause dependence/medication-overuse headache; it is not standard post-op analgesia.
rationales.C: Melatonin is a sleep aid and does not treat acute post-op pain.
rationales.D: Allopurinol is for chronic gout urate lowering, not acute pain management.
rationales.E: Dicyclomine is an antispasmodic used for IBS symptoms, not post-op pain.

takeaway: For short-term severe pain, opioid combination products like acetaminophen/oxycodone may be used after optimizing nonopioids.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Allergic rhinitis with congestion
stem:
A 34-year-old man has seasonal allergic rhinitis with prominent nasal congestion, sneezing, and itchy eyes.
He wants a non-sedating option and asks about a combination product that includes a decongestant.
PMH: no hypertension or arrhythmia.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Loratadine/pseudoephedrine combines a second-generation antihistamine with a decongestant and can help congestion plus allergic symptoms (counsel on stimulant effects).
rationales.B: Diphenhydramine is sedating and anticholinergic; not preferred when avoiding sedation.
rationales.C: Cetirizine is effective but can still cause some sedation and does not include a decongestant for prominent congestion.
rationales.D: Intranasal fluticasone is excellent for allergic rhinitis (including congestion) but is not the requested combination antihistamine + decongestant product.
rationales.E: Azelastine nasal can help allergic rhinitis but is not the antihistamine + decongestant combo requested.

takeaway: For allergy symptoms with prominent congestion, a combo like loratadine/pseudoephedrine may help (avoid in uncontrolled HTN/arrhythmias).

title: Acute gout flare
stem:
A 56-year-old man presents with sudden onset of severe pain, redness, and swelling in the first MTP joint.
Diagnosis: acute gout flare.
He has a history of peptic ulcer disease and wants to avoid NSAIDs.
question: Which medication is an appropriate treatment option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Colchicine is a standard option for treating acute gout flares, especially when NSAIDs are not desired.
rationales.B: Allopurinol is a urate-lowering therapy for long-term management; it does not treat acute flare pain immediately.
rationales.C: Febuxostat is also for chronic urate lowering, not acute flare treatment.
rationales.D: Aspirin is not used for acute gout flare treatment and can affect uric acid handling.
rationales.E: Hydrochlorothiazide can increase uric acid and may worsen gout risk; it is not a gout flare treatment.

takeaway: Acute gout flare: treat inflammation/pain (colchicine/NSAID/steroid). Urate-lowering drugs (allopurinol/febuxostat) are for long-term control.

title: Chronic gout with high uric acid
stem:
A 60-year-old man has recurrent gout attacks (4 flares in the last year) and uric acid 9.8 mg/dL.
He is currently not in an acute flare.
The clinician plans long-term urate-lowering therapy to reduce future attacks and prevent tophi.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Allopurinol is a xanthine oxidase inhibitor used for long-term urate lowering in gout to prevent recurrent flares and tophi.
rationales.B: Colchicine treats acute flares and is sometimes used as flare prophylaxis during initiation, but it is not the primary urate-lowering medication.
rationales.C: Ibuprofen can treat acute pain/inflammation but does not lower uric acid long-term.
rationales.D: Acetaminophen treats pain but does not reduce inflammation or lower uric acid.
rationales.E: Hydroxyzine is unrelated.

takeaway: Frequent gout flares → start long-term urate lowering (allopurinol). Treat flares separately and consider flare prophylaxis when starting ULT.

title: Allopurinol intolerance
stem:
A 58-year-old man with chronic gout was started on allopurinol.
Within 1 week he developed a diffuse pruritic rash and facial swelling, and the medication was stopped.
He needs an alternative long-term urate-lowering medication.
question: Which medication is the best alternative from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Febuxostat is a xanthine oxidase inhibitor alternative used for long-term urate lowering when allopurinol is not tolerated (consider CV risk).
rationales.B: Colchicine helps treat acute flares and may be used short-term prophylactically but does not lower uric acid long-term.
rationales.C: Ibuprofen treats pain/inflammation but does not lower uric acid.
rationales.D: Acetaminophen treats pain but does not lower uric acid or treat inflammation well in gout.
rationales.E: Hydrochlorothiazide can raise uric acid and can worsen gout risk.

takeaway: If allopurinol isn’t tolerated, febuxostat is a high-yield alternative urate-lowering therapy (but weigh cardiovascular risk).

title: Rheumatoid arthritis first-line DMARD
stem:
A 44-year-old woman has newly diagnosed rheumatoid arthritis with symmetric MCP and wrist swelling,
morning stiffness >1 hour, and elevated inflammatory markers.
She needs a first-line disease-modifying therapy to slow joint damage.
She is not pregnant and uses reliable contraception.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Methotrexate is a cornerstone first-line DMARD for rheumatoid arthritis and helps prevent progressive joint damage.
rationales.B: NSAIDs improve pain but do not prevent joint damage—RA requires DMARD therapy.
rationales.C: Prednisone can control inflammation short-term but is not an ideal long-term disease-modifying strategy due to significant adverse effects.
rationales.D: Hydroxychloroquine can be used for milder disease or in combination, but methotrexate is the classic first-line DMARD for many patients with active RA.
rationales.E: Etanercept is a biologic DMARD often used if conventional DMARDs are inadequate or for certain disease severity; methotrexate is typically tried first.

takeaway: RA requires DMARDs to prevent joint damage—methotrexate is the high-yield first-line anchor drug.

title: Retinal toxicity risk
stem:
A 36-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus is starting long-term disease-modifying therapy.
Her clinician chooses a medication that is often continued long-term and requires baseline and periodic ophthalmologic monitoring due to retinal toxicity risk.
question: Which medication is associated with this retinal toxicity risk?

rationales.A: Correct. Hydroxychloroquine can cause retinal toxicity with long-term use; baseline and periodic eye exams help detect early changes before vision loss.
rationales.B: Methotrexate has important monitoring (e.g., liver, blood counts) but retinal toxicity is not its classic adverse effect.
rationales.C: Etanercept requires infection screening/monitoring; retinal toxicity is not its hallmark adverse effect.
rationales.D: Allopurinol can cause hypersensitivity reactions; it is not associated with retinal toxicity monitoring.
rationales.E: Amiodarone has multiple toxicities (thyroid, lung, liver, eye deposits), but the classic drug requiring retinal toxicity screening via regular ophthalmologic exams in autoimmune therapy is hydroxychloroquine.

takeaway: Hydroxychloroquine is high-yield for lupus/RA but carries retinal toxicity risk—counsel baseline and periodic eye exams.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: C. difficile colitis
stem:
A 66-year-old woman recently completed clindamycin for a dental infection.
Now she has 8 watery stools/day, abdominal cramping, and a low-grade fever.
Stool toxin test is positive for Clostridioides difficile.
question: Which medication is most appropriate from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Oral vancomycin is a standard therapy for C. difficile infection because it delivers drug directly to the gut lumen.
rationales.B: IV vancomycin does not effectively treat C. difficile colitis because it does not achieve adequate concentrations in the gut lumen when given IV.
rationales.C: Antidiarrheals like loperamide can worsen/toxin retention and are generally avoided in suspected/confirmed C. difficile.
rationales.D: Diphenoxylate/atropine is also an antidiarrheal and is not appropriate for C. difficile treatment.
rationales.E: Azithromycin does not treat C. difficile colitis and could worsen antibiotic-associated diarrhea.

takeaway: C. diff colitis: treat with oral vancomycin (not IV), and avoid antimotility agents.

title: MRSA pneumonia option
stem:
A 58-year-old man is hospitalized with pneumonia.
Sputum culture grows MRSA.
He is taking sertraline for depression.
The team wants an MRSA-active antibiotic option that has excellent lung penetration but requires awareness of serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs.
question: Which medication best fits this description from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Linezolid is active against MRSA and is associated with serotonin syndrome risk when combined with serotonergic drugs like SSRIs (due to MAOI activity).
rationales.B: Oral vancomycin is for C. difficile colitis and is not used to treat pneumonia.
rationales.C: TMP-SMX can treat some MRSA skin infections and PCP, but it is not the classic first-line inpatient MRSA pneumonia agent in this vignette emphasizing serotonin risk.
rationales.D: Ceftriaxone does not cover MRSA.
rationales.E: Azithromycin does not cover MRSA.

takeaway: Linezolid is a high-yield MRSA option—especially for pneumonia—but remember the serotonin syndrome interaction with SSRIs.

title: PCP prophylaxis in HIV
stem:
A 39-year-old man with HIV is newly established in care.
Labs: CD4 count 140 cells/mm³.
He is started on antiretroviral therapy and needs prophylaxis against Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP).
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. TMP-SMX is the standard prophylaxis for PCP in patients with low CD4 counts.
rationales.B: Azithromycin is used for certain atypical infections and (in some protocols) MAC prophylaxis at very low CD4 counts; it is not first-line PCP prophylaxis.
rationales.C: Ciprofloxacin does not provide PCP prophylaxis.
rationales.D: Oral vancomycin is for C. difficile colitis, not PCP prophylaxis.
rationales.E: Fluconazole treats fungal infections (e.g., Candida, cryptococcal suppression in some contexts) but is not PCP prophylaxis.

takeaway: Low CD4 count + PCP prevention: TMP-SMX is the high-yield first-line prophylaxis.

title: COPD maintenance
stem:
A 66-year-old man with COPD has persistent dyspnea on exertion despite as-needed albuterol.
He has no history of asthma.
He needs a once-daily maintenance inhaler that combines a LAMA and a LABA.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Umeclidinium/vilanterol is a LAMA/LABA combination used for COPD maintenance therapy.
rationales.B: Albuterol is a short-acting rescue inhaler and is not an appropriate once-daily maintenance LAMA/LABA therapy.
rationales.C: Inhaled corticosteroid monotherapy is not first-line maintenance for COPD without asthma features and does not provide the dual bronchodilation asked for.
rationales.D: Salmeterol is a LABA but not a LAMA/LABA combination; in COPD, dual bronchodilation with LAMA/LABA can be more effective for symptoms.
rationales.E: Montelukast is used for asthma/allergic rhinitis, not as primary COPD maintenance.

takeaway: COPD maintenance with persistent symptoms: step up to long-acting bronchodilators—LAMA/LABA combo (umeclidinium/vilanterol) is a high-yield option.

title: AF rhythm control warning
stem:
A 70-year-old man has atrial fibrillation and NYHA class III heart failure (EF 25%).
He asks about medications to maintain normal rhythm so he “doesn’t feel the irregular heartbeat.”
question: Which antiarrhythmic should be avoided because it increases risk of harm in symptomatic heart failure and is contraindicated in certain AF populations?

rationales.A: Correct. Dronedarone can increase risk of adverse outcomes in patients with symptomatic heart failure and is contraindicated in certain AF settings (e.g., permanent AF).
