rationales.B: Amiodarone is often used for rhythm control in patients with structural heart disease/HFrEF when rhythm control is needed (with toxicity monitoring).
rationales.C: Digoxin is used for rate control and symptom relief in select patients; it is not the key contraindicated rhythm-control agent highlighted here.
rationales.D: Metoprolol succinate is a beta-blocker used for HFrEF and rate control in AF; it does not have the dronedarone contraindication profile.
rationales.E: Adenosine is used to terminate certain SVTs and is not a chronic AF rhythm-control medication.

takeaway: Dronedarone can be harmful in symptomatic HF/permanent AF—know when to avoid it.

title: Antiarrhythmic with beta-blockade + QT prolongation
stem:
A 62-year-old woman with recurrent symptomatic atrial arrhythmias is being evaluated for rhythm-control therapy.
The cardiologist mentions a medication that is both a nonselective beta-blocker and a class III antiarrhythmic,
so it can prolong the QT interval and cause torsades de pointes.
question: Which medication best matches this description?

rationales.A: Correct. Sotalol has both beta-blocking properties and class III antiarrhythmic effects and is associated with QT prolongation and torsades risk.
rationales.B: Metoprolol is a beta-blocker but does not have class III potassium-channel blockade and is not a typical torsades-provoking QT-prolonger like sotalol.
rationales.C: Digoxin is used for rate control and has a different toxicity profile (e.g., GI symptoms, arrhythmias) rather than QT-prolongation torsades risk.
rationales.D: Adenosine is an acute SVT termination drug and is not used as chronic rhythm-control therapy.
rationales.E: Amlodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker used for hypertension/angina, not as an antiarrhythmic.

takeaway: Sotalol = beta-blocker + class III effects → QT prolongation/torsades risk (think ECG monitoring).

title: Paroxysmal AF without structural heart disease
stem:
A 41-year-old man has symptomatic paroxysmal atrial fibrillation about once per month.
Echocardiogram shows normal structure and EF 60%.
He is interested in a “pill-in-the-pocket” approach to terminate episodes when they occur.
question: Which medication is an appropriate option for this patient from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Flecainide (class IC) can be used for rhythm control in paroxysmal AF in patients without structural heart disease and is used in pill-in-the-pocket strategies in selected patients.
rationales.B: Dronedarone is used for rhythm control in some patients, but the classic pill-in-the-pocket class IC option highlighted for structurally normal hearts is flecainide.
rationales.C: Amiodarone is effective but is generally reserved due to long-term toxicity; it is not the classic pill-in-the-pocket choice in a healthy heart.
rationales.D: Digoxin is a rate-control drug and does not terminate AF episodes in a pill-in-the-pocket strategy.
rationales.E: Sotalol can be used for rhythm control but is not the classic pill-in-the-pocket medication and requires QT monitoring.

takeaway: Pill-in-the-pocket AF termination is a classic use case for flecainide in patients without structural heart disease.

title: Hypertensive acute pulmonary edema
stem:
A 68-year-old man presents with acute severe shortness of breath and frothy sputum.
Vitals: BP 210/110 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, SpO2 86% on room air.
Exam: diffuse crackles; he is in distress.
The team diagnoses hypertensive acute pulmonary edema and wants rapid preload/afterload reduction.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below for rapid IV vasodilation?

rationales.A: Correct. IV nitroglycerin can be titrated rapidly for acute hypertensive pulmonary edema to reduce preload/afterload and improve symptoms.
rationales.B: Sublingual nitroglycerin can help angina and may be used emergently, but this vignette specifically asks for rapid titratable IV therapy in severe pulmonary edema.
rationales.C: Isosorbide mononitrate is a long-acting oral nitrate used for chronic angina prophylaxis, not acute titratable management.
rationales.D: Ranolazine is for chronic angina symptom reduction and is not used for acute hypertensive pulmonary edema.
rationales.E: Sildenafil is contraindicated with nitrates and is not used for acute pulmonary edema.

takeaway: Acute hypertensive pulmonary edema: think titratable IV nitroglycerin for rapid hemodynamic improvement.
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: Chronic stable angina prophylaxis
stem:
A 61-year-old woman has chronic stable angina.
She uses sublingual nitroglycerin several times per week despite beta-blocker therapy.
Blood pressure is 118/70 mmHg.
She wants a long-acting nitrate taken once daily to reduce angina frequency.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Isosorbide mononitrate is a long-acting oral nitrate used for chronic angina prophylaxis (with nitrate-free interval considerations).
rationales.B: Sublingual nitroglycerin is used for acute angina episodes, not once-daily prevention.
rationales.C: IV nitroglycerin is for acute inpatient situations (e.g., hypertensive pulmonary edema), not outpatient daily prophylaxis.
rationales.D: Ranolazine is another chronic angina option, but the question specifically asks for a long-acting nitrate once-daily preventive therapy.
rationales.E: Sildenafil treats erectile dysfunction and is contraindicated with nitrates.

takeaway: Stable angina prevention: long-acting nitrates like isosorbide mononitrate reduce episode frequency; keep SL nitro for rescue.

title: Add-on LDL lowering
stem:
A 57-year-old woman with ASCVD is taking high-intensity atorvastatin.
Despite adherence, LDL remains 92 mg/dL.
She wants an oral add-on medication that lowers LDL by reducing intestinal cholesterol absorption.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ezetimibe lowers LDL by inhibiting intestinal cholesterol absorption and is a common oral add-on to statins when additional LDL lowering is needed.
rationales.B: Fenofibrate primarily lowers triglycerides and has a smaller effect on LDL compared with ezetimibe.
rationales.C: Gemfibrozil also primarily lowers triglycerides and can increase myopathy risk with certain statins.
rationales.D: Icosapent ethyl targets triglycerides and CV risk in select patients; it is not primarily an LDL-lowering add-on like ezetimibe.
rationales.E: Alirocumab is a PCSK9 inhibitor and can greatly lower LDL, but it is injectable; the question asks for an oral add-on that blocks intestinal absorption.

takeaway: When LDL remains above goal on statin therapy, ezetimibe is a high-yield oral add-on option.
module_tags: Module 2b Non-Statin Lipids (Ezetimibe, PCSK9, Fibrates, Omega-3)

title: PCSK9 inhibitor option
stem:
A 52-year-old man with familial hypercholesterolemia and prior MI has LDL 130 mg/dL despite:
- High-intensity statin
- Ezetimibe
He is willing to use an injectable medication for additional LDL lowering.
question: Which medication is an appropriate PCSK9 inhibitor choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Evolocumab is a PCSK9 inhibitor given by injection and can markedly lower LDL when added to statin ± ezetimibe.
rationales.B: Fenofibrate mainly lowers triglycerides and does not provide the large LDL reduction expected from PCSK9 inhibition.
rationales.C: Gemfibrozil mainly lowers triglycerides and can increase myopathy risk with some statins; it is not a PCSK9 inhibitor.
rationales.D: Icosapent ethyl targets triglycerides and CV risk in select patients; it is not a primary LDL-lowering PCSK9 inhibitor.
rationales.E: Lovastatin is a statin, but he is already on high-intensity statin therapy; the question asks for an injectable PCSK9 option.

takeaway: For very high-risk patients with LDL still elevated on statin + ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors like evolocumab are high-yield next steps.
module_tags: Module 2b Non-Statin Lipids (Ezetimibe, PCSK9, Fibrates, Omega-3)

title: Severe hypertriglyceridemia on statin
stem:
A 60-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and ASCVD is taking rosuvastatin.
Fasting triglycerides are 780 mg/dL.
The clinician wants to reduce pancreatitis risk and also wants to minimize statin–fibrate myopathy risk.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Fenofibrate is a fibrate used to lower very high triglycerides and is generally preferred over gemfibrozil when combined with a statin due to lower myopathy interaction risk.
rationales.B: Gemfibrozil also lowers triglycerides but has a higher risk of significant drug interaction–related myopathy when used with many statins.
rationales.C: Ezetimibe primarily lowers LDL, not severe triglycerides.
rationales.D: Icosapent ethyl can lower triglycerides and reduce CV risk, but in very high triglycerides where pancreatitis risk is a priority, a fibrate is a classic first choice.
rationales.E: Alirocumab is a PCSK9 inhibitor focused on LDL reduction, not severe hypertriglyceridemia.

takeaway: Very high triglycerides: fibrates are high-yield. If a statin is on board, fenofibrate is often preferred over gemfibrozil.
module_tags: Module 2b Non-Statin Lipids (Ezetimibe, PCSK9, Fibrates, Omega-3)

title: ASCVD + triglycerides 150–499 on statin
stem:
A 64-year-old woman with established ASCVD is taking high-intensity statin therapy.
Fasting triglycerides are 220 mg/dL, LDL is at goal.
She asks if there is a medication that can further reduce cardiovascular risk in patients like her with elevated triglycerides despite statin therapy.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Icosapent ethyl is used in select high-risk patients with elevated triglycerides on statins to reduce cardiovascular events.
rationales.B: Gemfibrozil lowers triglycerides but is not the classic add-on for CV event reduction in the statin-treated 150–499 mg/dL TG range and has interaction issues with statins.
rationales.C: Fenofibrate lowers triglycerides and is used for very high TG or certain dyslipidemias; it is not the classic answer for CV risk reduction in this vignette.
rationales.D: Ezetimibe lowers LDL by blocking intestinal absorption; her LDL is already at goal and the vignette emphasizes triglyceride-associated residual risk.
rationales.E: Lovastatin is not needed; she is already on high-intensity statin therapy.

takeaway: In high-risk patients with TG 150–499 despite statin therapy, icosapent ethyl is a high-yield add-on for further CV risk reduction.
module_tags: Module 2b Non-Statin Lipids (Ezetimibe, PCSK9, Fibrates, Omega-3)

title: Diabetic ketoacidosis
stem:
A 23-year-old man with type 1 diabetes presents with abdominal pain and vomiting.
Labs: glucose 520 mg/dL, anion gap 26, bicarbonate 12 mEq/L, positive serum ketones.
Diagnosis: diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).
After initial fluids and potassium assessment, the team starts an insulin infusion.
question: Which insulin is most commonly used for continuous IV infusion in DKA from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Regular insulin is the standard insulin used for IV infusion in DKA due to predictable pharmacokinetics when given intravenously.
rationales.B: Insulin glargine is a long-acting basal insulin and is not used as the primary IV infusion agent in DKA.
rationales.C: Insulin lispro is a rapid-acting mealtime insulin; IV protocols classically use regular insulin.
rationales.D: Insulin aspart is also rapid-acting; the classic IV DKA infusion insulin is regular insulin.
rationales.E: Metformin is an oral agent and is not used to treat DKA.

takeaway: DKA management includes fluids, electrolytes, and an IV regular insulin infusion after potassium is addressed.

title: Basal glucose control in T2DM
stem:
A 49-year-old man with type 2 diabetes has elevated fasting glucose readings (180–220 mg/dL)
despite metformin and lifestyle changes.
His clinician plans to add a once-daily long-acting basal insulin to target fasting hyperglycemia.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Insulin glargine is a long-acting basal insulin commonly dosed once daily to target fasting glucose.
rationales.B: Regular insulin is shorter acting and is used as prandial coverage or IV infusion in DKA, not as a once-daily basal insulin.
rationales.C: Insulin aspart is rapid-acting and is used for mealtime coverage, not basal fasting control.
rationales.D: Sitagliptin is an oral DPP-4 inhibitor; it can help A1c but does not specifically provide basal insulin replacement for high fasting glucose.
rationales.E: Glipizide can lower glucose but carries hypoglycemia risk and is not the basal insulin therapy requested.

takeaway: Fasting hyperglycemia often needs basal insulin—glargine is a high-yield once-daily basal option.

title: Rapid-acting mealtime glucose coverage
stem:
A 17-year-old with type 1 diabetes uses basal insulin and needs prandial coverage.
He wants a rapid-acting insulin he can inject right before meals to control postprandial glucose spikes.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Insulin aspart is a rapid-acting insulin analog used for mealtime (bolus) coverage to blunt postprandial glucose rises.
rationales.B: Insulin glargine is long-acting basal insulin, not mealtime coverage.
rationales.C: Insulin detemir is also a basal insulin, not a rapid-acting mealtime insulin.
rationales.D: Metformin is an oral agent mainly used in type 2 diabetes and is not a replacement for mealtime insulin in type 1 diabetes.
rationales.E: Pioglitazone is a thiazolidinedione used in type 2 diabetes; it is not a mealtime insulin.

takeaway: Type 1 diabetes requires both basal + bolus coverage; rapid-acting analogs like insulin aspart are classic mealtime bolus agents.

title: Alternative basal glucose control
stem:
A 36-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes uses rapid-acting insulin for meals and needs a basal insulin.
Her clinician prescribes a long-acting basal insulin option that can be dosed once or twice daily depending on patient needs.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Insulin detemir is a long-acting basal insulin used to provide background insulin coverage.
rationales.B: Insulin lispro is rapid-acting and used for mealtime coverage, not basal needs.
rationales.C: Regular insulin is shorter acting and is not a long-acting basal insulin option.
rationales.D: Metformin is an oral agent for type 2 diabetes and is not basal insulin.
rationales.E: Sitagliptin is an oral DPP-4 inhibitor and is not basal insulin.

takeaway: Basal insulin options include detemir and glargine—use basal to cover fasting/background needs and rapid insulin for meals.

title: Weekly GLP-1 option for T2DM + obesity
stem:
A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes (A1c 8.7%) and obesity (BMI 38) wants to lose weight.
She prefers a once-weekly injectable medication that improves glycemic control and supports weight loss.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Semaglutide is a GLP-1 receptor agonist with once-weekly dosing that improves glycemic control and promotes meaningful weight loss.
rationales.B: Liraglutide is also a GLP-1 receptor agonist, but it is typically dosed daily; the vignette specifically requests once-weekly therapy.
rationales.C: Sitagliptin is a DPP-4 inhibitor; it is weight neutral and generally provides less weight loss than GLP-1 agents.
rationales.D: Glipizide can lower glucose but is associated with hypoglycemia and weight gain.
rationales.E: Pioglitazone can cause weight gain and edema and does not match the weight-loss goal.

takeaway: For T2DM + obesity, GLP-1 receptor agonists are high-yield. Once-weekly option: semaglutide.

title: Sulfonylurea choice in CKD
stem:
A 74-year-old man with type 2 diabetes needs additional oral therapy.
Renal function: eGFR 28 mL/min/1.73m².
The clinician wants a sulfonylurea option with relatively lower risk of prolonged hypoglycemia in CKD compared with other sulfonylureas.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Glipizide is often preferred among sulfonylureas in CKD because it has fewer active metabolites and a lower risk of prolonged hypoglycemia compared with some alternatives.
rationales.B: Glimepiride can cause hypoglycemia and may have higher risk of prolonged effects in CKD than glipizide.
rationales.C: Pioglitazone is not a sulfonylurea and can cause edema/weight gain.
rationales.D: Metformin is typically avoided in advanced CKD due to lactic acidosis risk.
rationales.E: Empagliflozin is an SGLT2 inhibitor; its glycemic efficacy depends on kidney function and it is not a sulfonylurea.

takeaway: When a sulfonylurea is needed in CKD, glipizide is a common high-yield preferred option (still monitor for hypoglycemia).

title: Diabetes medication that can worsen heart failure
stem:
A 66-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and chronic heart failure (EF 30%) needs additional glycemic control.
She recently started a new diabetes medication.
Two weeks later, she develops weight gain, ankle edema, and worsening shortness of breath.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Pioglitazone (a thiazolidinedione) can cause fluid retention and weight gain and may worsen heart failure.
rationales.B: SGLT2 inhibitors like empagliflozin generally improve heart failure outcomes and are not associated with TZD-like fluid retention.
rationales.C: Metformin does not typically cause edema; key concerns are GI effects and lactic acidosis risk in advanced CKD.
rationales.D: Sitagliptin is weight neutral and is not a classic cause of edema/weight gain.
rationales.E: Semaglutide typically supports weight loss rather than edema/weight gain.

takeaway: Thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone) can cause edema and worsen HF—high-yield safety point.

title: Depression + diabetic neuropathic pain
stem:
A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes has burning, tingling pain in both feet consistent with diabetic peripheral neuropathy.
He also has major depressive disorder.
He wants one medication that can help both mood and neuropathic pain.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Duloxetine is an SNRI used for depression and is also a high-yield option for diabetic neuropathic pain.
rationales.B: Sertraline treats depression but is not a primary neuropathic pain agent.
rationales.C: Bupropion treats depression but does not treat neuropathic pain.
rationales.D: Gabapentin treats neuropathic pain but is not an antidepressant for major depressive disorder.
rationales.E: Pregabalin treats neuropathic pain but is not an antidepressant for major depressive disorder.

takeaway: When depression and neuropathic pain coexist, duloxetine is a high-yield dual-purpose choice.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: SNRI with dose-dependent hypertension
stem:
A 39-year-old woman is started on an SNRI for major depressive disorder.
At follow-up, her blood pressure has risen from 118/74 to 146/92 mmHg without other changes.
The clinician notes this effect is dose-related and more common with one SNRI.
question: Which medication is the most likely cause from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Venlafaxine is classically associated with dose-dependent increases in blood pressure and requires BP monitoring, especially at higher doses.
rationales.B: Fluoxetine (SSRI) is not classically associated with dose-dependent hypertension like venlafaxine.
rationales.C: Sertraline (SSRI) is not classically associated with venlafaxine-like dose-dependent hypertension.
rationales.D: Bupropion can be activating and may affect BP in some patients, but the classic dose-dependent hypertension association is venlafaxine.
rationales.E: Mirtazapine is more associated with sedation and weight gain than hypertension.

takeaway: Venlafaxine is a high-yield SNRI adverse effect: dose-dependent blood pressure elevation.

title: Active metabolite of venlafaxine
stem:
A 42-year-old man is being treated for major depressive disorder.
His clinician considers switching him to an SNRI that is the active metabolite of venlafaxine.
question: Which medication best fits this description from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Desvenlafaxine is the active metabolite of venlafaxine and is an SNRI used for major depressive disorder.
rationales.B: Venlafaxine is the parent medication, not the metabolite being asked for.
rationales.C: Duloxetine is an SNRI but is not the active metabolite of venlafaxine.
rationales.D: Citalopram is an SSRI, not an SNRI metabolite of venlafaxine.
rationales.E: Bupropion is an atypical antidepressant, not an SNRI metabolite.

takeaway: Desvenlafaxine = active metabolite of venlafaxine (SNRI).
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: SSRI with QT prolongation risk
stem:
A 68-year-old man with depression is started on an SSRI.
Two weeks later, ECG shows a prolonged QTc.
He also takes no other QT-prolonging medications and electrolytes are normal.
The clinician recalls that one SSRI has a higher dose-dependent QT prolongation concern and has stricter max-dose guidance in older adults.
question: Which medication is the most likely cause from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Citalopram is a classic SSRI associated with dose-dependent QT prolongation risk and max-dose limitations, especially in older adults.
rationales.B: Sertraline is generally not the classic SSRI most associated with QT prolongation compared with citalopram.
rationales.C: Fluoxetine has many interactions and a long half-life, but citalopram is the classic SSRI linked to QT prolongation concerns.
rationales.D: Paroxetine is more associated with anticholinergic effects and discontinuation syndrome than QT prolongation.
rationales.E: Bupropion is not an SSRI and is not classically associated with QT prolongation as the key teaching point.

takeaway: Citalopram has a high-yield dose-dependent QT prolongation risk—consider ECG risk factors and dosing limits.
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: Neuropathic pain/migraine prevention
stem:
A 33-year-old woman has frequent migraines (6/month) and also has chronic neuropathic pain symptoms.
She prefers a single medication option used off-label for migraine prevention that can also help neuropathic pain.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Amitriptyline (a tricyclic antidepressant) is a high-yield option used for migraine prevention and neuropathic pain (counsel sedation/anticholinergic effects).
rationales.B: Fluoxetine treats depression but is not a classic migraine-prevention/neuropathic-pain choice like TCAs.
rationales.C: Bupropion is more activating and is not a classic migraine prevention agent.
rationales.D: Gabapentin treats neuropathic pain but is not a classic first-choice migraine prevention medication in this list.
rationales.E: Rimegepant can be used for acute migraine treatment (and prevention in some regimens), but the vignette is pointing to the classic TCA used for prevention and neuropathic pain together.

takeaway: Amitriptyline is a high-yield TCA used for neuropathic pain and migraine prevention (watch anticholinergic/sedation).
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)
Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: TCA with less anticholinergic burden
stem:
A 72-year-old man has chronic neuropathic pain.
He previously tried amitriptyline but stopped due to severe dry mouth, constipation, and daytime sedation.
The clinician wants a tricyclic antidepressant option that is generally less anticholinergic/sedating than amitriptyline.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Nortriptyline (a secondary amine TCA) is generally less anticholinergic and less sedating than amitriptyline (a tertiary amine), and is used for neuropathic pain in some patients.
rationales.B: Amitriptyline is more anticholinergic/sedating and caused his intolerable side effects.
rationales.C: Diphenhydramine is strongly anticholinergic and sedating and is not a neuropathic pain antidepressant option.
rationales.D: Cyclobenzaprine is a muscle relaxant with anticholinergic effects; it is not a preferred long-term neuropathic pain antidepressant strategy.
rationales.E: Trazodone is often used for insomnia; it is not a classic neuropathic pain medication choice.

takeaway: If a TCA is needed but amitriptyline is too anticholinergic, nortriptyline is a high-yield alternative.
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)
Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Insomnia adjunct with rare priapism risk
stem:
A 40-year-old man with depression is taking sertraline but still has significant insomnia.
His clinician adds a low-dose medication at bedtime for sleep.
Two weeks later, he presents to the ED with a painful erection lasting 5 hours.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Trazodone is commonly used for insomnia in depressed patients and is classically associated (rarely) with priapism.
rationales.B: Bupropion is activating and is not commonly used as a sleep aid; priapism is not its classic adverse effect.
rationales.C: Hydroxyzine can cause sedation but is not classically associated with priapism.
rationales.D: Doxepin can be used for sleep maintenance insomnia, but priapism is more classically associated with trazodone.
rationales.E: Melatonin is a sleep aid and is not associated with priapism.

takeaway: Trazodone is a common sleep adjunct—but counsel rare priapism risk (seek urgent care for prolonged painful erection).
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: Adjunct for treatment-resistant depression
stem:
A 35-year-old woman has major depressive disorder.
She has partial response to sertraline after 10 weeks at an adequate dose.
Her psychiatrist plans augmentation with an atypical antipsychotic that is a partial dopamine agonist.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Aripiprazole is a partial dopamine agonist atypical antipsychotic commonly used as augmentation for major depressive disorder.
rationales.B: Haloperidol is a typical antipsychotic used for acute agitation/psychosis and is not a common depression augmentation choice.
rationales.C: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and requires intensive monitoring; it is not a typical MDD augmentation choice.
rationales.D: Olanzapine is an atypical antipsychotic but is not the one known for partial dopamine agonism emphasized in this question.
rationales.E: Lithium can be used as augmentation in depression, but the question specifically targets the partial dopamine agonist atypical antipsychotic choice.

takeaway: Aripiprazole is a high-yield augmentation option for MDD and is a partial dopamine agonist.
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia
stem:
A 29-year-old man with schizophrenia has persistent hallucinations and delusions despite adequate trials of two antipsychotics.
His psychiatrist recommends a medication reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia that requires regular absolute neutrophil count (ANC) monitoring due to risk of agranulocytosis.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and requires ANC monitoring due to agranulocytosis risk.
rationales.B: Risperidone is a commonly used antipsychotic but does not have the unique mandatory ANC monitoring requirement like clozapine.
rationales.C: Aripiprazole is an atypical antipsychotic but is not reserved specifically for treatment resistance with ANC monitoring.
rationales.D: Haloperidol is a typical antipsychotic used for psychosis/agitation and does not require ANC monitoring like clozapine.
rationales.E: Olanzapine can cause metabolic effects but does not require ANC monitoring like clozapine.

takeaway: Clozapine is the high-yield answer for treatment-resistant schizophrenia—remember ANC monitoring for agranulocytosis.

title: Acute agitation in delirium
stem:
A 73-year-old man is hospitalized with pneumonia and develops acute delirium with severe agitation.
He is attempting to climb out of bed and pull out IV lines.
Nonpharmacologic interventions are attempted, but he remains a danger to himself.
The team selects a high-potency typical antipsychotic commonly used for acute agitation in the hospital.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Haloperidol is a high-potency typical antipsychotic frequently used for acute agitation in delirium (watch EPS and QT risk).
rationales.B: Clozapine is not used for acute delirium due to monitoring needs and adverse effects.
rationales.C: Aripiprazole is not the classic rapid-acting hospital delirium agitation medication compared with haloperidol.
rationales.D: Fluoxetine is an antidepressant and will not acutely treat agitation in delirium.
rationales.E: Zolpidem is a hypnotic and can worsen delirium; it is not used to treat delirium-related agitation.

takeaway: Severe agitation in delirium: haloperidol is a classic choice (monitor for EPS and QT prolongation).

title: Atypical antipsychotic with high metabolic risk
stem:
A 27-year-old man with schizophrenia is started on an atypical antipsychotic.
Over 3 months, he gains 25 pounds and develops increased fasting glucose and triglycerides.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Olanzapine is strongly associated with weight gain and metabolic adverse effects (hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia).
rationales.B: Haloperidol is more associated with EPS; it is not as strongly linked to metabolic syndrome as olanzapine.
rationales.C: Aripiprazole generally has a lower metabolic risk profile than olanzapine.
rationales.D: Risperidone can cause metabolic effects, but olanzapine is a classic high-risk agent for significant weight gain/metabolic syndrome.
rationales.E: Clozapine also has high metabolic risk, but this vignette is testing the classic metabolic syndrome association; olanzapine is a clear answer (and clozapine has a unique ANC monitoring hallmark tested separately).

takeaway: Olanzapine is a high-yield antipsychotic for major weight gain/metabolic syndrome—monitor weight, A1c, and lipids.

title: Bipolar depression with insomnia
stem:
A 31-year-old woman with bipolar disorder presents with a depressive episode and severe insomnia.
She wants a medication that can treat bipolar depression and is known to be sedating at bedtime.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Quetiapine is an atypical antipsychotic used for bipolar disorder (including bipolar depression) and is often sedating.
rationales.B: Bupropion can be activating and may worsen insomnia; it is not a primary bipolar depression monotherapy choice.
rationales.C: Aripiprazole is less sedating and is not the classic sedating bedtime option for bipolar depression in this list.
rationales.D: Haloperidol is used for acute psychosis/agitation and is not a typical bipolar depression treatment choice.
rationales.E: Sertraline can help unipolar depression but antidepressant monotherapy can precipitate mania in bipolar disorder without mood stabilizer coverage.

takeaway: Quetiapine is a high-yield option for bipolar depression and is commonly sedating (useful when insomnia is prominent).
module_tags: Module 4b Antidepressants II + Mood Stabilizers (TCAs, Atypicals, Lithium)

title: Galactorrhea/amenorrhea from antipsychotic
stem:
A 24-year-old woman is treated for psychosis with an antipsychotic.
Two months later, she reports missed periods and milky nipple discharge.
Pregnancy test is negative.
Prolactin level is elevated.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Risperidone is classically associated with hyperprolactinemia, which can cause galactorrhea, amenorrhea, and sexual dysfunction.
rationales.B: Aripiprazole (partial dopamine agonist) is less likely to raise prolactin and may even lower it in some settings.
rationales.C: Quetiapine is less likely to cause significant prolactin elevation than risperidone.
rationales.D: Olanzapine is more notable for metabolic effects than prolactin elevation as the key hallmark.
rationales.E: Clozapine has unique risks (agranulocytosis, seizures, metabolic effects) but is not a classic hyperprolactinemia culprit like risperidone.

takeaway: Risperidone → hyperprolactinemia is a classic adverse effect (galactorrhea/amenorrhea).

title: First-line stimulant for ADHD
stem:
A 9-year-old boy has ADHD with significant inattention and hyperactivity at school and home.
He has no cardiac history, normal BP/HR, and no substance use risk.
His parents want a first-line stimulant option commonly used in children.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Methylphenidate is a first-line stimulant for ADHD and is widely used in pediatric patients (monitor appetite, sleep, BP/HR).
rationales.B: Atomoxetine is a nonstimulant option and may be chosen when stimulants are not appropriate; it is not the classic first-line stimulant requested.
rationales.C: Guanfacine ER is an alpha-2 agonist used as nonstimulant ADHD therapy/adjunct (often for tics/impulsivity).
rationales.D: Bupropion can help ADHD symptoms in some cases but is not the classic first-line pediatric stimulant.
rationales.E: Sertraline treats anxiety/depression and does not treat core ADHD symptoms.

takeaway: First-line ADHD pharmacotherapy often includes stimulants—methylphenidate is a high-yield option.

title: ADHD + binge eating disorder
stem:
A 28-year-old woman has ADHD and also meets criteria for binge eating disorder.
She wants a stimulant option that is a prodrug of dextroamphetamine and has an FDA indication for binge eating disorder.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Lisdexamfetamine is a prodrug of dextroamphetamine and is used for ADHD; it also has an indication for binge eating disorder.
rationales.B: Methylphenidate is a stimulant for ADHD but is not the prodrug/ binge-eating–indicated medication described.
rationales.C: Dexmethylphenidate is the d-enantiomer of methylphenidate and is not the prodrug/ binge-eating–indicated medication described.
rationales.D: Atomoxetine is a nonstimulant for ADHD and is not indicated for binge eating disorder.
rationales.E: Guanfacine ER is a nonstimulant option/adjunct for ADHD and is not indicated for binge eating disorder.

takeaway: Lisdexamfetamine is a high-yield ADHD stimulant (prodrug) and is also used for binge eating disorder.

title: d-isomer of methylphenidate
stem:
A 13-year-old with ADHD is switching medications due to side effects.
The clinician selects an agent that is the d-enantiomer (more active isomer) of methylphenidate.
question: Which medication matches this description from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Dexmethylphenidate is the d-enantiomer of methylphenidate and is used for ADHD.
rationales.B: Methylphenidate is the racemic parent compound, not the isolated d-isomer.
rationales.C: Lisdexamfetamine is a prodrug of dextroamphetamine, not a methylphenidate isomer.
rationales.D: Atomoxetine is a nonstimulant norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor, not a stimulant isomer.
rationales.E: Guanfacine ER is an alpha-2 agonist and not related to methylphenidate isomer chemistry.

takeaway: Dexmethylphenidate = d-enantiomer of methylphenidate (ADHD stimulant).

title: Nonstimulant ADHD medication
stem:
A 22-year-old college student has ADHD but has a history of stimulant misuse.
He wants a nonstimulant option that is not a controlled substance.
The clinician chooses a selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor used for ADHD.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Atomoxetine is a nonstimulant selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor used for ADHD and is not a controlled substance.
rationales.B: Methylphenidate is a stimulant and has misuse potential.
rationales.C: Mixed amphetamine salts are stimulants with misuse potential.
rationales.D: Lisdexamfetamine is a stimulant (prodrug) and still carries misuse potential.
rationales.E: Guanfacine ER is a nonstimulant ADHD option, but it is an alpha-2 agonist (not a norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor) and is not the medication described.

takeaway: Atomoxetine is a high-yield nonstimulant ADHD option (NE reuptake inhibitor), useful when misuse risk is a concern.

title: ADHD with tics/impulsivity
stem:
A 10-year-old girl has ADHD and a comorbid tic disorder.
Her parents want to avoid stimulant worsening of tics.
The clinician selects a nonstimulant alpha-2A agonist that can cause sedation and low blood pressure.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Guanfacine ER is an alpha-2A agonist used for ADHD (often helpful for hyperactivity/impulsivity and tics); it can cause sedation, bradycardia, and hypotension.
rationales.B: Methylphenidate is a stimulant and may worsen tics in some patients.
rationales.C: Amphetamine salts are stimulants and may worsen tics in some patients.
rationales.D: Atomoxetine is a nonstimulant NE reuptake inhibitor and is an option, but the vignette specifically describes the alpha-2 agonist mechanism and sedation/hypotension.
rationales.E: Sertraline treats anxiety/depression and does not treat core ADHD symptoms.

takeaway: Guanfacine ER is a high-yield nonstimulant ADHD option—alpha-2 agonist effects mean sedation and low BP/HR can occur.

title: Mixed salt stimulant for ADHD
stem:
A 30-year-old man with ADHD wants a stimulant medication.
The clinician prescribes a product containing mixed amphetamine salts.
question: Which medication is the best match from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Amphetamine/dextroamphetamine salts are mixed amphetamine salts used to treat ADHD.
rationales.B: Atomoxetine is a nonstimulant NE reuptake inhibitor, not mixed amphetamine salts.
rationales.C: Guanfacine ER is an alpha-2 agonist and is not mixed amphetamine salts.
rationales.D: Methylphenidate is a stimulant but is not a mixed amphetamine salt product.
rationales.E: Lisdexamfetamine is a prodrug of dextroamphetamine, not mixed amphetamine salts.

takeaway: Know the major ADHD med buckets: stimulants (amphetamine salts, methylphenidate) vs nonstimulants (atomoxetine, guanfacine ER).

title: OA pain with GI ulcer history
stem:
A 67-year-old woman has knee osteoarthritis with daily pain.
PMH: prior NSAID-associated peptic ulcer bleed 2 years ago.
She needs an anti-inflammatory option and wants to minimize GI ulcer risk compared with nonselective NSAIDs.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Celecoxib is a COX-2 selective NSAID and is generally associated with lower GI ulcer risk than nonselective NSAIDs (though risk is not zero).
rationales.B: Ibuprofen is a nonselective NSAID and carries GI ulcer/bleed risk.
rationales.C: Naproxen is a nonselective NSAID and carries GI ulcer/bleed risk.
rationales.D: Indomethacin is a nonselective NSAID with higher adverse effect burden; it is not preferred in someone with prior GI bleed.
rationales.E: Ketorolac has high GI/renal toxicity risk and is for short-term use only; it is not appropriate for chronic OA management.

takeaway: Celecoxib (COX-2 selective) is a high-yield option when NSAID therapy is needed but GI risk is a major concern.

title: Short-term severe acute pain
stem:
A 38-year-old man presents to the ED with severe renal colic pain from a kidney stone.
He needs a potent NSAID for short-term pain control.
The clinician chooses an NSAID that should not be used for more than about 5 days due to high GI and renal toxicity risk.
question: Which medication best fits this description from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ketorolac is a potent NSAID used for short-term acute pain; duration is limited due to GI bleeding and renal toxicity risk.
rationales.B: Meloxicam is used for chronic OA/RA; it is not the classic short-term high-toxicity ED analgesic NSAID described.
rationales.C: Celecoxib is COX-2 selective and used more for chronic inflammatory pain, not the classic short-term ketorolac scenario.
rationales.D: Acetaminophen can help pain but does not provide the same anti-inflammatory potency as ketorolac for renal colic.
rationales.E: Nabumetone is a chronic-use NSAID option and is not the classic high-toxicity short-term ketorolac.

takeaway: Ketorolac is a high-yield short-term NSAID for severe acute pain—limit duration (GI/renal risk).

title: Acute gout flare when colchicine not tolerated
stem:
A 59-year-old man has an acute gout flare in the first MTP joint.
He tried colchicine in the past but developed severe diarrhea and refuses it today.
He has no history of GI bleed or advanced kidney disease.
question: Which medication is the best treatment choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Indomethacin is a classic NSAID option for treating acute gout flares when colchicine is not tolerated and no NSAID contraindications exist.
rationales.B: Allopurinol is for long-term urate lowering and is not started as acute flare monotherapy for immediate symptom relief.
rationales.C: Febuxostat is also for chronic urate lowering, not acute flare treatment.
rationales.D: Colchicine is effective for acute flares, but he cannot tolerate it and refuses it today.
rationales.E: Acetaminophen can treat pain but lacks anti-inflammatory effect and is not a preferred acute gout flare therapy.

takeaway: Acute gout flares are treated with anti-inflammatory therapy (NSAID/colchicine/steroid). Indomethacin is a classic NSAID choice.

title: Chronic osteoarthritis pain
stem:
A 62-year-old man has chronic osteoarthritis pain.
He wants an NSAID option that is commonly taken once daily for chronic inflammatory pain control.
He has no history of GI bleed and normal renal function.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Meloxicam is a commonly used once-daily NSAID for chronic osteoarthritis and other inflammatory pain conditions.
rationales.B: Ketorolac is for short-term acute pain only due to toxicity and is not used chronically.
rationales.C: Acetaminophen can help pain but is not an NSAID and may be less effective for inflammatory pain in some patients.
rationales.D: Indomethacin is not typically the preferred chronic OA NSAID due to side effects; it is more classically used for acute gout flares.
rationales.E: Tramadol is an opioid-like analgesic and is not the first-line NSAID requested.

takeaway: For chronic OA, longer-acting NSAIDs like meloxicam are common; avoid ketorolac for chronic use.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Topical NSAID for localized OA pain
stem:
A 70-year-old woman has localized knee osteoarthritis pain.
She has chronic kidney disease stage 3 and wants to avoid systemic NSAIDs if possible.
Her clinician recommends a topical NSAID gel for localized pain relief with lower systemic exposure.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Diclofenac is available as a topical NSAID and is commonly used for localized osteoarthritis pain to reduce systemic NSAID exposure.
rationales.B: Indomethacin is an oral NSAID with higher systemic adverse effect risk; it is not the topical gel option being asked for.
rationales.C: Ketorolac is used short-term systemically; it is not the common topical OA gel option.
rationales.D: Celecoxib is an oral COX-2 selective NSAID, not a topical gel option in this list.
rationales.E: Naproxen is an oral NSAID and would increase systemic exposure.

takeaway: For localized OA pain—especially when systemic NSAID risk is a concern—topical diclofenac is a high-yield option.

title: NSAID prodrug option
stem:
A 64-year-old man with chronic osteoarthritis needs an NSAID.
He reports dyspepsia with several NSAIDs.
His clinician considers an NSAID that is a nonacidic prodrug and may be somewhat better tolerated in the stomach for some patients.
question: Which medication best fits this description from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Nabumetone is a nonacidic prodrug NSAID that may cause somewhat less GI irritation in some patients compared with certain other NSAIDs (GI risk is not eliminated).
rationales.B: Ibuprofen is a nonselective NSAID and commonly causes GI irritation/dyspepsia.
rationales.C: Indomethacin is associated with higher adverse effect burden and is not typically chosen to improve dyspepsia.
rationales.D: Ketorolac has high GI toxicity risk and is used only short-term.
rationales.E: Acetaminophen is not an NSAID and does not provide anti-inflammatory effect.

takeaway: Nabumetone is a high-yield ‘prodrug NSAID’ option sometimes chosen when GI tolerability is a concern (still counsel GI/renal risks).

title: Localized neuropathic pain
stem:
A 71-year-old woman had shingles 4 months ago.
She now has persistent burning pain and allodynia in the same dermatomal distribution (postherpetic neuralgia).
The pain is localized to a small area on her torso.
question: Which medication is an appropriate localized treatment option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Lidocaine patches can provide topical analgesia for localized neuropathic pain such as postherpetic neuralgia.
rationales.B: Acetaminophen may help mild pain but is usually insufficient for neuropathic pain and does not provide localized anesthetic effect.
rationales.C: NSAIDs are not particularly effective for neuropathic pain like postherpetic neuralgia.
rationales.D: Allopurinol is for chronic gout urate lowering, not neuropathic pain.
rationales.E: Azithromycin is an antibiotic and does not treat neuropathic pain.

takeaway: Postherpetic neuralgia can be treated with neuropathic pain agents and topical options—lidocaine patches are high-yield for localized pain.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Fibromyalgia
stem:
A 46-year-old woman has widespread musculoskeletal pain for >6 months, fatigue, and nonrestorative sleep.
Workup is negative for inflammatory arthritis.
Diagnosis: fibromyalgia.
The clinician recommends a gabapentinoid that can improve pain and sleep.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Pregabalin is a gabapentinoid used for neuropathic pain and is a high-yield option for fibromyalgia symptom management.
rationales.B: Sertraline can treat depression/anxiety but is not a primary fibromyalgia pain medication.
rationales.C: Acetaminophen may help mild pain but is often inadequate for fibromyalgia and does not address neuropathic pain mechanisms.
rationales.D: NSAIDs are often not very effective for fibromyalgia because it is not primarily an inflammatory condition.
rationales.E: Risperidone is an antipsychotic and does not treat fibromyalgia.

takeaway: Fibromyalgia is not primarily inflammatory—think neuromodulators like pregabalin for pain/sleep symptoms.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Opioid-tolerant chronic cancer pain
stem:
A 63-year-old man with metastatic cancer is opioid-tolerant and has stable chronic pain.
He has difficulty swallowing pills due to esophageal compression and wants a long-acting option that provides continuous analgesia.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Transdermal fentanyl patches provide continuous long-acting analgesia and are used in opioid-tolerant patients with chronic pain.
rationales.B: Hydrocodone/acetaminophen is a short-acting oral combination and is not suitable when swallowing is difficult and long-acting coverage is desired.
rationales.C: Tramadol is weaker and short acting; it is not a long-acting transdermal option for opioid-tolerant severe cancer pain.
rationales.D: Naloxone reverses opioids and would worsen pain/precipitate withdrawal in an opioid-tolerant patient.
rationales.E: Ketorolac is for short-term acute pain only and is not appropriate for chronic cancer pain management.

takeaway: Fentanyl patches are for opioid-tolerant chronic pain when continuous long-acting analgesia is needed (not for opioid-naïve patients).
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Severe cancer pain
stem:
A 58-year-old woman with metastatic cancer has severe ongoing pain requiring a strong opioid.
She can swallow tablets and has no history of opioid use disorder.
The clinician wants a classic strong opioid option for severe pain control.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Morphine is a strong opioid used for severe pain, including cancer-related pain; extended-release formulations provide long-acting analgesia when appropriate.
rationales.B: Acetaminophen is useful for mild-to-moderate pain but is often insufficient alone for severe cancer pain.
rationales.C: NSAIDs can help inflammatory pain but are typically insufficient alone for severe cancer pain and carry GI/renal risk.
rationales.D: Naloxone reverses opioids and is used for overdose; it would worsen pain/precipitate withdrawal in someone receiving opioids.
rationales.E: Diphenhydramine is an antihistamine and does not treat severe cancer pain.

takeaway: Severe cancer pain often requires strong opioids—morphine is a high-yield foundational option; pair with safety counseling and monitoring.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Knee osteoarthritis
stem:
A 66-year-old woman has chronic knee osteoarthritis pain that limits walking.
She has tried scheduled acetaminophen with only partial relief.
Her clinician prescribes **Lodine** to reduce inflammation and pain.
question: Which medication was prescribed?

rationales.A: Correct. Lodine is the brand name for **etodolac**, an NSAID used for inflammatory pain such as osteoarthritis.
rationales.B: Celecoxib is a COX-2 selective NSAID (Celebrex), not Lodine.
rationales.C: Meloxicam is an NSAID often dosed once daily, but it is not Lodine.
rationales.D: Nabumetone is a prodrug NSAID, but it is not Lodine.
rationales.E: Diclofenac is an NSAID available in oral/topical forms, but it is not Lodine.

takeaway: Know high-yield brand ↔ generic pairs: **Lodine = etodolac**.

title: COPD rescue bronchodilator option
stem:
A 72-year-old man with COPD presents with wheezing and increased shortness of breath.
He reports that albuterol makes him feel “shaky” with palpitations.
The clinician wants a **short-acting inhaled anticholinergic** bronchodilator for symptom relief.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ipratropium is a **SAMA** (short-acting muscarinic antagonist) used for bronchospasm relief in COPD and sometimes as an adjunct in asthma.
rationales.B: Tiotropium is a **LAMA** (long-acting muscarinic antagonist) used for maintenance, not quick relief.
rationales.C: Budesonide inhaled is an inhaled corticosteroid controller, not a rescue bronchodilator.
rationales.D: Montelukast is an oral leukotriene receptor antagonist controller, not a quick-acting bronchodilator.
rationales.E: Fluticasone nasal treats allergic rhinitis; it does not relieve bronchospasm in COPD.

takeaway: COPD bronchodilator buckets: **SAMA = ipratropium** (rescue/adjunct) vs **LAMA = tiotropium** (maintenance).

title: SABA with less tremor/palpitations
stem:
A 46-year-old woman with intermittent asthma uses albuterol as needed.
She reports bothersome tremor and palpitations after each dose but still needs a quick-relief inhaler.
Her clinician chooses the **R-enantiomer** of albuterol, which may cause fewer beta-agonist side effects in some patients.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Levalbuterol is the R-enantiomer of albuterol and is a SABA used for quick relief; some patients report fewer tremor/palpitations.
rationales.B: Tiotropium is a long-acting anticholinergic (LAMA) for maintenance, not quick relief.
rationales.C: Ipratropium is a SAMA and can help bronchospasm, but it is not a beta-agonist alternative equivalent to a SABA for asthma rescue.
rationales.D: Budesonide inhaled is a controller (inhaled corticosteroid), not a rescue inhaler.
rationales.E: Montelukast is an oral controller, not an immediate rescue medication.

takeaway: If albuterol causes excessive tremor/palpitations, **levalbuterol** is a high-yield alternative SABA option.

title: COPD maintenance
stem:
A 69-year-old man with COPD has persistent dyspnea despite using albuterol several times per day.
He has no history of asthma.
The clinician wants a **once-daily long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA)** for maintenance bronchodilation.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Tiotropium is a LAMA used for **maintenance** therapy in COPD (and sometimes asthma add-on).
rationales.B: Ipratropium is a SAMA (short-acting) and is not a once-daily maintenance LAMA.
rationales.C: Albuterol is a SABA rescue inhaler and should not be used as sole maintenance therapy.
rationales.D: Budesonide is an inhaled corticosteroid; it is not a bronchodilator and is not first-line monotherapy for COPD maintenance.
rationales.E: Fluticasone nasal treats allergic rhinitis, not COPD.

takeaway: COPD symptom control often improves with long-acting bronchodilators—**tiotropium (LAMA)** is a high-yield maintenance option.

title: Persistent asthma in a young child
stem:
A 4-year-old boy has persistent asthma symptoms (wheezing >2 days/week and nighttime symptoms twice/month).
His parents have trouble using a metered-dose inhaler even with a spacer.
The clinician wants an **inhaled corticosteroid available as a nebulized solution** for maintenance control.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Budesonide is an inhaled corticosteroid and is available in nebulized forms used in younger children who cannot use inhalers reliably.
rationales.B: Advair is an ICS/LABA combination inhaler; it is not the classic nebulized inhaled steroid option for young children.
rationales.C: Tiotropium is a bronchodilator (LAMA) and is not first-line controller therapy for pediatric persistent asthma.
rationales.D: Albuterol is a rescue inhaler and does not control airway inflammation long-term.
rationales.E: Montelukast is an oral controller option but is not the inhaled corticosteroid requested.

takeaway: Persistent asthma requires a controller—**inhaled corticosteroids** are first-line; **budesonide** has a high-yield nebulized option for young kids.

title: Identify the budesonide/formoterol combination inhaler
stem:
A 38-year-old woman with moderate persistent asthma remains symptomatic on an inhaled corticosteroid alone.
Her clinician prescribes **Symbicort** as step-up therapy to add a long-acting beta-agonist.
question: Which medication combination is Symbicort?

rationales.A: Correct. Symbicort contains **budesonide (ICS) + formoterol (LABA)**.
rationales.B: Advair contains fluticasone + salmeterol, not budesonide + formoterol.
rationales.C: Dulera contains mometasone + formoterol, not budesonide + formoterol.
rationales.D: Fluticasone (Flovent) is an inhaled corticosteroid alone (no LABA).
rationales.E: Tiotropium is a LAMA bronchodilator and does not contain an inhaled corticosteroid.

takeaway: Know high-yield combo inhalers: **Symbicort = budesonide/formoterol**.

title: Identify the fluticasone/salmeterol combination inhaler
stem:
A 63-year-old man with asthma/COPD overlap has persistent symptoms despite inhaled corticosteroid therapy.
His pulmonologist prescribes **Advair** to provide combination ICS/LABA therapy.
question: Which medication combination is Advair?

rationales.A: Correct. Advair contains **fluticasone (ICS) + salmeterol (LABA)**.
rationales.B: Symbicort contains budesonide + formoterol, not fluticasone + salmeterol.
rationales.C: Dulera contains mometasone + formoterol, not fluticasone + salmeterol.
rationales.D: Albuterol is a SABA rescue inhaler and is not an ICS/LABA combo.
rationales.E: Tiotropium is a LAMA maintenance bronchodilator and is not an ICS/LABA combo.

takeaway: Advair is a high-yield **ICS/LABA** combo: **fluticasone + salmeterol**.

title: Identify the mometasone/formoterol combination inhaler
stem:
A 29-year-old man with moderate persistent asthma needs step-up therapy from inhaled corticosteroid monotherapy.
His clinician prescribes **Dulera**.
question: Which medication combination is Dulera?

rationales.A: Correct. Dulera contains **mometasone (ICS) + formoterol (LABA)**.
rationales.B: Advair contains fluticasone + salmeterol.
rationales.C: Symbicort contains budesonide + formoterol.
rationales.D: Flovent is fluticasone alone (ICS only).
rationales.E: Anoro Ellipta is a LAMA/LABA combo for COPD (umeclidinium/vilanterol), not an ICS/LABA.

takeaway: Know your combo inhalers: **Dulera = mometasone/formoterol**.

title: Allergic rhinitis
stem:
A 25-year-old woman has seasonal allergic rhinitis with daily congestion and sneezing.
Her clinician recommends a daily intranasal corticosteroid and prescribes **Nasonex**.
question: Which medication was prescribed?

rationales.A: Correct. **Nasonex** is mometasone nasal, an intranasal corticosteroid effective for allergic rhinitis symptoms, including congestion.
rationales.B: Fluticasone nasal is also an intranasal steroid, but it is not Nasonex (it is commonly Flonase).
rationales.C: Azelastine is an intranasal antihistamine, not an intranasal corticosteroid brand Nasonex.
rationales.D: Cetirizine is an oral antihistamine, not Nasonex.
rationales.E: Pseudoephedrine is an oral decongestant, not Nasonex.

takeaway: High-yield brand ↔ generic: **Nasonex = mometasone nasal**.

title: Allergic rhinitis med reduced by fruit juice
stem:
A 19-year-old woman takes an OTC allergy medication every morning.
She usually takes it with orange juice.
She reports it “stopped working” after she started drinking a large glass of orange juice with breakfast daily.
Her pharmacist explains that certain fruit juices can reduce absorption of one second-generation antihistamine.
question: Which medication is most likely affected?

rationales.A: Correct. **Fexofenadine** absorption can be reduced by certain fruit juices (e.g., orange/apple/grapefruit) and by some antacids, decreasing effectiveness.
rationales.B: Cetirizine is not the classic second-generation antihistamine with a fruit juice absorption interaction teaching point.
rationales.C: Loratadine is not the classic agent associated with clinically notable fruit juice absorption reduction.
rationales.D: Diphenhydramine is a first-generation sedating antihistamine; the fruit juice absorption teaching point targets fexofenadine.
rationales.E: Pseudoephedrine is a decongestant; fruit juice absorption interaction is not the classic issue here.

takeaway: Counseling pearl: **fexofenadine** effectiveness can drop if taken with certain fruit juices or some antacids.

title: Active enantiomer of cetirizine
stem:
A 32-year-old man with allergic rhinitis is switching medications.
His clinician recommends a second-generation antihistamine that is the **active levo-enantiomer** of cetirizine.
question: Which medication best matches this description from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. **Levocetirizine** is the levo-enantiomer of cetirizine and is used for allergic rhinitis and urticaria.
rationales.B: Fexofenadine is a second-generation antihistamine but is not the enantiomer of cetirizine.
rationales.C: Loratadine is a second-generation antihistamine but is not related as an enantiomer of cetirizine.
rationales.D: Diphenhydramine is first-generation and sedating, not an enantiomer of cetirizine.
rationales.E: Pseudoephedrine is a decongestant, not an antihistamine.

takeaway: Drug family recognition: **levocetirizine = active enantiomer of cetirizine** (2nd-gen antihistamine).

title: Nasal congestion with uncontrolled HTN/BPH
stem:
A 68-year-old man has a bad cold with nasal congestion.
PMH: uncontrolled hypertension (BP 172/98) and benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) with urinary hesitancy.
Meds: amlodipine, tamsulosin.
He asks which OTC medication could worsen his blood pressure and urinary retention.
question: Which medication should he avoid?

rationales.A: Correct. **Pseudoephedrine** is a sympathomimetic decongestant that can raise blood pressure and worsen urinary retention in BPH.
rationales.B: Intranasal steroids like fluticasone can help congestion without the same systemic sympathetic effects.
rationales.C: Cetirizine is an antihistamine; it does not usually raise BP like pseudoephedrine (though it can cause some sedation).
rationales.D: Azelastine nasal is an antihistamine spray; it does not have the same BP/urinary retention risk as pseudoephedrine.
rationales.E: Mometasone nasal is an intranasal steroid and does not raise BP like pseudoephedrine.

takeaway: High-yield counseling: avoid **pseudoephedrine** in uncontrolled HTN and in patients prone to urinary retention (BPH).
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: Intranasal antihistamine with bitter taste
stem:
A 27-year-old woman has allergic rhinitis with sneezing and itchy, runny nose.
She wants a medication that works quickly and can be used as a nasal spray.
After starting therapy, she complains of a strong **bitter taste** in her mouth after each dose.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Azelastine is an **intranasal antihistamine**; bitter taste is a classic counseling point.
rationales.B: Fluticasone nasal is an intranasal corticosteroid; bitter taste is less characteristic.
rationales.C: Mometasone nasal is also an intranasal corticosteroid; bitter taste is not the hallmark adverse effect.
rationales.D: Fexofenadine is an oral antihistamine and does not cause a bitter taste from nasal administration.
rationales.E: Pseudoephedrine is an oral decongestant and does not cause a bitter taste from a nasal spray.

takeaway: Azelastine is a high-yield **intranasal antihistamine**; warn patients about **bitter taste** and proper spray technique.

title: Nightmares and mood changes after asthma med
stem:
A 15-year-old boy with asthma and allergic rhinitis is started on a nightly oral controller medication.
Within 2 weeks, his parents report new irritability, anxiety, and vivid nightmares.
No other medications were changed.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible?

rationales.A: Correct. Montelukast (a leukotriene receptor antagonist) has a high-yield warning for **neuropsychiatric events** (e.g., mood changes, nightmares).
rationales.B: Inhaled budesonide can cause thrush/hoarseness but is not the classic cause of neuropsychiatric symptoms.
rationales.C: Tiotropium is an inhaled anticholinergic and is not known for causing nightmares/mood changes as a key adverse effect.
rationales.D: Fexofenadine is a second-generation antihistamine and is not a classic cause of neuropsychiatric effects.
rationales.E: Fluticasone nasal is locally acting and is not associated with this hallmark adverse effect.

takeaway: Montelukast can cause **neuropsychiatric effects**—screen and counsel patients, especially when used for mild symptoms.

title: COPD med with narrow therapeutic index
stem:
A 60-year-old man with COPD is taking a chronic oral medication for bronchodilation.
He is started on ciprofloxacin for a urinary tract infection.
Three days later he develops nausea, vomiting, tremor, and a new tachyarrhythmia.
A drug level is found to be markedly elevated.
question: Which medication level is most likely elevated?

rationales.A: Correct. Theophylline has a **narrow therapeutic index** and significant drug–drug interactions; CYP inhibition (e.g., with certain antibiotics) can raise levels → nausea, tremor, arrhythmias, seizures.
rationales.B: Roflumilast does not have the classic narrow therapeutic index/toxicity level monitoring profile like theophylline.
rationales.C: Montelukast is not monitored with drug levels and is not known for this acute toxicity pattern.
rationales.D: Tiotropium is inhaled and not monitored with serum levels; it does not cause this classic toxicity pattern.
rationales.E: Symbicort is inhaled; toxicity from elevated serum levels due to ciprofloxacin interaction is not the classic issue.

takeaway: Theophylline is high-yield for **drug interactions and toxicity**—think GI upset + tremor + arrhythmias/seizures when levels rise.

title: COPD chronic bronchitis with frequent exacerbations
stem:
A 67-year-old man with severe COPD and **chronic bronchitis** has 3 exacerbations in the last year despite optimized inhaler therapy (LAMA/LABA).
He is not a current smoker.
BMI is 20 and he reports decreased appetite.
The pulmonologist considers adding an oral medication that can reduce COPD exacerbations but may cause weight loss and psychiatric side effects.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Roflumilast is an oral **PDE-4 inhibitor** used in severe COPD with chronic bronchitis and frequent exacerbations; weight loss and psychiatric effects are notable adverse effects.
rationales.B: Montelukast is used for asthma/allergic rhinitis, not COPD chronic bronchitis exacerbation reduction as described.
rationales.C: Theophylline is an older bronchodilator with narrow therapeutic index; it is not the targeted exacerbation-reduction PDE-4 therapy described.
rationales.D: Omalizumab is an anti-IgE biologic for allergic asthma, not COPD chronic bronchitis exacerbation reduction.
rationales.E: Tiotropium is a maintenance LAMA inhaler and is already part of optimized inhaler therapy in this scenario.

takeaway: Severe COPD + chronic bronchitis + frequent exacerbations: consider **roflumilast** (watch weight loss and mood effects).

title: Severe allergic asthma with high IgE
stem:
A 24-year-old woman has severe persistent asthma despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroid/LABA therapy.
She has a history of allergic rhinitis and multiple positive skin tests to perennial allergens.
Labs show elevated IgE.
The clinician plans to start a subcutaneous biologic that targets IgE.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Omalizumab is an **anti-IgE** monoclonal antibody used for allergic asthma in appropriately selected patients; monitor for anaphylaxis risk.
rationales.B: Dupilumab targets IL-4/IL-13 pathways and is used for eosinophilic asthma and atopic disease; the vignette specifically points to high IgE allergic asthma → omalizumab.
rationales.C: Roflumilast is for COPD chronic bronchitis exacerbation reduction, not allergic asthma biologic therapy.
rationales.D: Montelukast is an oral leukotriene receptor antagonist; it is not a biologic anti-IgE therapy for severe asthma.
rationales.E: Theophylline is an older bronchodilator and is not a targeted biologic for allergic asthma.

takeaway: Phenotype matters: **allergic asthma + elevated IgE** → think **omalizumab**.

title: Eosinophilic asthma + atopic dermatitis
stem:
A 30-year-old man has severe asthma and long-standing atopic dermatitis (eczema).
He has frequent exacerbations despite inhaled corticosteroid/LABA therapy.
CBC shows eosinophils 720 cells/µL.
The clinician plans a subcutaneous biologic that targets IL-4/IL-13 signaling and can also improve atopic dermatitis.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Dupilumab targets IL-4/IL-13 pathways and is used for eosinophilic asthma and atopic dermatitis.
rationales.B: Omalizumab is anti-IgE and is selected for allergic asthma phenotypes; this vignette emphasizes eosinophilia and atopic dermatitis benefit → dupilumab.
rationales.C: Roflumilast is for COPD chronic bronchitis exacerbation reduction, not eosinophilic asthma biologic therapy.
rationales.D: Montelukast is an oral leukotriene receptor antagonist and is not the targeted biologic described.
rationales.E: Theophylline is an older bronchodilator and does not address eosinophilic inflammation or atopic dermatitis.

takeaway: Eosinophilic/Type 2 asthma with atopic disease: **dupilumab** is a high-yield biologic option.

title: Duodenal ulcer barrier therapy
stem:
A 54-year-old man has epigastric pain relieved by food and is diagnosed with a duodenal ulcer.
He is prescribed a medication that **coats the ulcer base** and forms a protective barrier.
The pharmacist counsels him to take it on an **empty stomach** and separate it from other oral medications.
question: Which medication is the best match from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Sucralfate forms a protective barrier over ulcers and should be taken on an empty stomach; it can bind other medications and reduce absorption.
rationales.B: Pantoprazole is a proton pump inhibitor that reduces acid secretion; it does not primarily work by coating the ulcer.
rationales.C: Famotidine is an H2 blocker that reduces acid; it does not form a protective coating.
rationales.D: Ondansetron is an antiemetic and does not treat ulcers by coating them.
rationales.E: Metoclopramide is a prokinetic/antiemetic and does not coat ulcers.

takeaway: Sucralfate = **mucosal protectant** that coats ulcers; take on empty stomach and separate from other meds due to binding.
module_tags: Module 8a Acid Suppression (PPIs, H2 Blockers, Mucosal Protection)

title: Post-op nausea/vomiting
stem:
A 45-year-old woman develops significant nausea after surgery.
She needs an antiemetic that blocks 5-HT3 receptors and is commonly used for postoperative and chemotherapy-related nausea.
Her clinician also reviews her ECG risk factors because this medication can prolong QT.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ondansetron is a **5-HT3 antagonist** widely used for postoperative and chemotherapy-related nausea; QT prolongation is a known risk.
rationales.B: Promethazine is an antihistamine/antiemetic but is more sedating and is not the 5-HT3 antagonist described.
rationales.C: Scopolamine is an anticholinergic patch mainly used for motion sickness prevention.
rationales.D: Metoclopramide is a dopamine antagonist/prokinetic; it can cause EPS/tardive dyskinesia and is not a 5-HT3 antagonist.
rationales.E: Aprepitant is an NK1 antagonist used for chemotherapy-induced nausea prevention (often in combination), not the classic single-agent post-op 5-HT3 choice.

takeaway: Ondansetron is the high-yield **5-HT3 antagonist** antiemetic—remember **QT prolongation** risk.

title: Diabetic gastroparesis
stem:
A 59-year-old man with long-standing type 2 diabetes has early satiety, bloating, and nausea after meals.
A gastric emptying study supports diabetic gastroparesis.
He is prescribed a medication that increases GI motility via dopamine antagonism.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Metoclopramide is a prokinetic dopamine antagonist used for gastroparesis (counsel about EPS and tardive dyskinesia risk, especially with prolonged use).
rationales.B: Ondansetron treats nausea but does not improve gastric emptying/motility.
rationales.C: Promethazine treats nausea but does not treat delayed gastric emptying.
rationales.D: Sucralfate coats ulcers and does not improve motility.
rationales.E: Dicyclomine is an antispasmodic for IBS and can worsen gastric emptying due to anticholinergic effects.

takeaway: Diabetic gastroparesis: **metoclopramide** is a high-yield prokinetic—watch for EPS/tardive dyskinesia and limit duration when possible.

title: Sedating antiemetic/antihistamine
stem:
A 33-year-old woman presents to urgent care with severe nausea from a migraine.
She receives an antiemetic that is also a first-generation antihistamine.
After the dose, she becomes very drowsy and asks if that is expected.
question: Which medication was most likely given?

rationales.A: Correct. Promethazine is a sedating first-generation antihistamine with antiemetic properties—drowsiness is common.
rationales.B: Ondansetron is generally less sedating; its key teaching point is 5-HT3 blockade and QT risk.
rationales.C: Aprepitant is typically used for chemotherapy-induced nausea prevention and is not the classic urgent care sedating antihistamine antiemetic.
rationales.D: Scopolamine is an anticholinergic patch primarily used for motion sickness prevention rather than acute migraine-associated nausea in urgent care.
rationales.E: Metoclopramide can cause fatigue and EPS, but the hallmark 'very sedating antihistamine antiemetic' in this list is promethazine.

takeaway: Promethazine is an effective antiemetic but is **sedating** (first-generation antihistamine)—counsel about drowsiness and safety.

title: Motion sickness prevention
stem:
A 26-year-old man is going on a 3-day cruise and has a history of severe motion sickness.
He wants a preventive medication and prefers a transdermal patch applied before travel.
PMH: no glaucoma.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Scopolamine is an anticholinergic **transdermal patch** used for motion sickness prevention (watch dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention).
rationales.B: Ondansetron is for postoperative/chemo nausea and is not the classic motion sickness preventive patch.
rationales.C: Promethazine can help nausea but is sedating and is not the classic preventive patch option.
rationales.D: Metoclopramide is used for gastroparesis and some nausea, not motion sickness patch prevention.
rationales.E: Sucralfate treats ulcers by coating and does not prevent motion sickness.

takeaway: Motion sickness prevention: **scopolamine patch** is high-yield (anticholinergic adverse effects and glaucoma caution).

title: Chronic constipation
stem:
A 58-year-old woman has chronic constipation (hard stools, straining) despite increased fiber and water.
She wants an OTC option that can be mixed into a beverage and taken daily as needed.
Her clinician recommends an osmotic laxative powder commonly sold as MiraLAX.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Polyethylene glycol (PEG 3350) is an osmotic laxative used for constipation and is commonly available OTC as MiraLAX.
rationales.B: Lactulose is also an osmotic agent but is more commonly used for hepatic encephalopathy and can cause significant gas/bloating; the classic OTC powder described is PEG 3350.
rationales.C: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative and can cause cramping; it is not the gentle daily osmotic powder described.
rationales.D: Docusate is a stool softener; it is often used to reduce straining but may be less effective as monotherapy for established chronic constipation.
rationales.E: Linaclotide is a prescription agent for IBS-C/chronic idiopathic constipation; the vignette describes the common OTC PEG powder.

takeaway: OTC chronic constipation option: **PEG 3350 (MiraLAX)** is a high-yield osmotic laxative.

title: Colonoscopy bowel prep
stem:
A 62-year-old man is scheduled for a screening colonoscopy tomorrow morning.
He is prescribed a large-volume bowel preparation solution to cleanse the colon.
The label states it contains polyethylene glycol with electrolytes.
question: Which medication is the best match from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Polyethylene glycol **with electrolytes** is a standard bowel prep solution for colonoscopy.
rationales.B: MiraLAX (PEG 3350) is an OTC constipation agent and is not the classic full bowel prep solution with electrolytes described.
rationales.C: Bisacodyl can be used as an adjunct but is not the main large-volume PEG-electrolyte bowel prep.
rationales.D: Loperamide is an antidiarrheal and would worsen constipation.
rationales.E: Linaclotide is for IBS-C/chronic constipation; it is not used for colonoscopy bowel prep.

takeaway: Bowel prep for colonoscopy: **PEG with electrolytes** is a high-yield standard option.

title: Stimulant laxative
stem:
A 47-year-old woman has constipation and wants an OTC medication that works by stimulating colonic motility.
After taking it, she develops abdominal cramping and has a bowel movement within hours.
question: Which medication is the best match from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Bisacodyl is a **stimulant laxative** that increases intestinal motility; cramping can occur.
rationales.B: Docusate is a stool softener and does not stimulate motility strongly.
rationales.C: PEG (MiraLAX) is an osmotic laxative and is less associated with stimulant cramping than bisacodyl.
rationales.D: Lactulose is an osmotic laxative and can cause gas/bloating; bisacodyl is the classic stimulant agent in this list.
rationales.E: Loperamide is an antidiarrheal and would worsen constipation.

takeaway: Bisacodyl is a high-yield **stimulant laxative**—effective but can cause **cramping**.

title: Stool softener to reduce straining
stem:
A 30-year-old woman is 2 days postpartum and has painful hemorrhoids.
She wants to avoid straining with bowel movements while her symptoms improve.
The clinician recommends a stool softener.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Docusate is a stool softener used to reduce straining (e.g., postpartum, hemorrhoids).
rationales.B: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative and may cause cramping; it is not simply a stool softener.
rationales.C: Loperamide is an antidiarrheal and can worsen constipation.
rationales.D: Diphenoxylate/atropine is also an antidiarrheal and can worsen constipation.
rationales.E: PEG with electrolytes is a bowel prep, not a stool softener for postpartum comfort.

takeaway: Docusate is a high-yield **stool softener** for situations where minimizing straining is the goal (e.g., postpartum, hemorrhoids).

title: Opioid constipation prevention
stem:
A 55-year-old man starts morphine for cancer pain.
Two days later he reports no bowel movement and significant straining.
The clinician adds a combination product that includes a **stimulant laxative plus stool softener** for opioid-induced constipation prevention.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Senna/docusate combines a stimulant laxative (senna) with a stool softener (docusate) and is commonly used for opioid-induced constipation prophylaxis/treatment.
rationales.B: Loperamide is an antidiarrheal and would worsen constipation.
