rationales.C: Diphenoxylate/atropine is also an antidiarrheal and would worsen constipation.
rationales.D: Ondansetron is an antiemetic and can actually cause constipation; it is not a constipation treatment.
rationales.E: Sucralfate coats ulcers and does not treat constipation.

takeaway: Opioids commonly cause constipation—pair opioids with a bowel regimen. **Senna/docusate** is a high-yield combo option.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: IBS with constipation
stem:
A 34-year-old woman has IBS with constipation (IBS-C): abdominal pain improves after bowel movements and she has infrequent hard stools.
Lifestyle changes and OTC polyethylene glycol provided incomplete relief.
Her clinician prescribes a medication that increases intestinal fluid secretion and can cause diarrhea.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Linaclotide is used for IBS-C/chronic idiopathic constipation and works by increasing intestinal fluid secretion; diarrhea is a common adverse effect.
rationales.B: Loperamide is an antidiarrheal and would worsen constipation.
rationales.C: Docusate is a stool softener and is often insufficient for IBS-C symptom control alone.
rationales.D: Diphenoxylate/atropine is an antidiarrheal and would worsen constipation.
rationales.E: Sucralfate treats ulcers and does not treat IBS-C.

takeaway: IBS-C with persistent symptoms: **linaclotide** is a high-yield prescription option (warn about diarrhea).

title: Acute non-bloody diarrhea
stem:
A 28-year-old man develops 6 episodes of watery diarrhea after traveling.
He has no fever, no blood in stool, and no severe abdominal pain.
He asks for an OTC medication that decreases intestinal motility and can reduce the number of stools.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Loperamide is an antidiarrheal that decreases intestinal motility and is appropriate for uncomplicated non-bloody diarrhea (avoid if fever/bloody diarrhea or suspected C. diff).
rationales.B: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative and would worsen diarrhea.
rationales.C: PEG is an osmotic laxative and would worsen diarrhea.
rationales.D: Lactulose is an osmotic laxative and would worsen diarrhea.
rationales.E: Linaclotide increases intestinal fluid secretion and can cause diarrhea—opposite of what is needed.

takeaway: For uncomplicated watery diarrhea, **loperamide** is a high-yield OTC option; avoid antimotility agents in dysentery or C. diff.

title: Antidiarrheal with atropine added to deter misuse
stem:
A 40-year-old man has diarrhea and is prescribed an antidiarrheal agent.
The label lists two ingredients: an opioid-related antidiarrheal plus a small amount of atropine to discourage abuse.
question: Which medication best matches this description from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Diphenoxylate/atropine is an antidiarrheal; atropine is included to deter misuse (anticholinergic adverse effects at high doses).
rationales.B: Loperamide is an antidiarrheal but does not contain atropine.
rationales.C: Linaclotide treats IBS-C/constipation and can cause diarrhea; it is not an opioid/atropine combo.
rationales.D: Senna/docusate is for constipation, not diarrhea.
rationales.E: PEG is for constipation, not diarrhea.

takeaway: Know the antidiarrheal combos: **diphenoxylate/atropine** includes atropine to discourage abuse.

title: Mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis
stem:
A 26-year-old woman has 6 weeks of intermittent bloody diarrhea and urgency.
Colonoscopy shows continuous inflammation starting at the rectum, consistent with ulcerative colitis.
Symptoms are mild-to-moderate and she is hemodynamically stable.
question: Which medication is a high-yield first-line option for mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Mesalamine (5-ASA) is a high-yield first-line therapy for mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis.
rationales.B: Infliximab is an anti-TNF biologic used for moderate-to-severe IBD or refractory disease, not first-line mild disease.
rationales.C: Loperamide may worsen disease complications and does not treat colonic inflammation in ulcerative colitis.
rationales.D: Systemic steroids can induce remission for more severe flares but are not preferred long-term maintenance for mild disease.
rationales.E: Azathioprine is an immunomodulator often used for maintenance in selected patients, not first-line for mild disease induction.

takeaway: Ulcerative colitis basics: mild-to-moderate disease often starts with **5-ASA therapy (mesalamine)**.

title: Moderate-to-severe Crohn’s/UC
stem:
A 34-year-old man with Crohn’s disease has persistent symptoms despite mesalamine and a steroid taper.
He has weight loss, elevated CRP, and moderate-to-severe endoscopic disease.
The gastroenterologist plans to start an **IV infusion** biologic that targets TNF-alpha and requires screening for latent TB and hepatitis B prior to initiation.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Infliximab is an **IV anti-TNF** biologic used for moderate-to-severe IBD; screen for TB and hepatitis B due to serious infection/reactivation risk.
rationales.B: Adalimumab is also anti-TNF but is given subcutaneously; the vignette specifically describes an IV infusion biologic.
rationales.C: Etanercept is not used for IBD (and is used in other inflammatory conditions); it is not the correct IBD infusion biologic here.
rationales.D: Azathioprine is an immunomodulator and may be used for maintenance in some patients but is not the described IV anti-TNF infusion agent.
rationales.E: Mesalamine is for mild-to-moderate disease and is not sufficient for the moderate-to-severe refractory presentation described.

takeaway: Before starting anti-TNF biologics (e.g., **infliximab**), screen for **latent TB and hepatitis B** due to reactivation risk.

title: Bacterial vaginosis
stem:
A 29-year-old woman presents with thin gray vaginal discharge and a “fishy” odor, worse after sex.
Vaginal pH is elevated and clue cells are seen on wet mount.
Diagnosis: bacterial vaginosis.
She asks if she can drink alcohol while on the medication.
question: Which medication is the best treatment choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Metronidazole is first-line therapy for bacterial vaginosis; counsel to avoid alcohol due to a disulfiram-like reaction and to expect possible metallic taste.
rationales.B: Fluconazole treats Candida vulvovaginitis, not bacterial vaginosis.
rationales.C: Nystatin treats candidiasis (e.g., oral thrush), not BV.
rationales.D: Acyclovir treats HSV infections, not BV.
rationales.E: Azithromycin treats certain bacterial infections (e.g., chlamydia in some regimens), not BV.

takeaway: BV = clue cells + fishy odor → **metronidazole**. High-yield counseling: **avoid alcohol** and metallic taste can occur.

title: Vulvovaginal candidiasis
stem:
A 27-year-old woman has intense vulvovaginal itching and thick white “cottage cheese” discharge.
Vaginal pH is normal and wet mount shows budding yeast.
She prefers a single-dose **oral** treatment option.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Fluconazole is a common single-dose oral treatment for uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis.
rationales.B: Metronidazole treats bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis, not Candida.
rationales.C: Nystatin treats candidiasis but is typically topical/oral suspension for thrush; the vignette asks for a single-dose oral option.
rationales.D: Acyclovir treats HSV infections, not yeast infections.
rationales.E: Ceftriaxone treats gonorrhea and other bacterial infections, not Candida.

takeaway: Yeast infection (itching + thick white discharge + yeast on wet mount): **fluconazole** is a high-yield single-dose oral option.

title: Oral candidiasis (thrush)
stem:
A 48-year-old man with inhaled corticosteroid use presents with white plaques on the tongue and buccal mucosa
that scrape off and leave an erythematous base.
He has mild symptoms and prefers a topical therapy with minimal systemic absorption.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Nystatin oral suspension (swish and swallow) is a topical therapy for oral candidiasis with minimal systemic absorption.
rationales.B: Fluconazole can treat thrush but is systemic; the vignette asks for a topical option with minimal absorption.
rationales.C: Terbinafine is used for dermatophyte infections (e.g., onychomycosis), not oral thrush.
rationales.D: Metronidazole treats anaerobic/protozoal infections, not Candida thrush.
rationales.E: Acyclovir treats HSV infections, not Candida.

takeaway: Oral thrush after inhaled steroids: treat with topical antifungals like **nystatin** and counsel to rinse mouth after ICS use.

title: Onychomycosis
stem:
A 52-year-old man has thickened, yellow, brittle toenails consistent with onychomycosis.
Topical therapies have failed.
The clinician prescribes an **oral antifungal** that is first-line for dermatophyte nail infections and recommends baseline liver function testing.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Terbinafine is a first-line oral therapy for onychomycosis due to dermatophytes; hepatotoxicity risk means LFT monitoring is important.
rationales.B: Fluconazole can be used for some fungal infections but is not the classic first-line dermatophyte nail infection agent compared with terbinafine.
rationales.C: Nystatin is for Candida infections (e.g., thrush) and is not used for onychomycosis.
rationales.D: Acyclovir is an antiviral and does not treat onychomycosis.
rationales.E: Metronidazole treats anaerobic/protozoal infections and does not treat onychomycosis.

takeaway: Onychomycosis often needs systemic therapy: **terbinafine** is high-yield and requires **liver safety** counseling/monitoring.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Suspected HSV encephalitis
stem:
A 57-year-old man presents with fever, confusion, and new-onset seizures.
MRI suggests temporal lobe involvement.
CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein.
The team suspects HSV encephalitis and wants to start empiric antiviral therapy immediately.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. IV acyclovir is first-line empiric therapy for suspected HSV encephalitis; early treatment improves outcomes (monitor renal function and ensure hydration).
rationales.B: Oseltamivir treats influenza, not HSV encephalitis.
rationales.C: Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is for early outpatient COVID-19 in high-risk patients, not HSV encephalitis.
rationales.D: Fluconazole treats fungal infections (e.g., Candida), not HSV encephalitis.
rationales.E: Ceftriaxone treats many bacterial infections and meningitis pathogens, but suspected HSV encephalitis requires acyclovir.

takeaway: If HSV encephalitis is suspected, **do not delay**—start **IV acyclovir** and monitor renal safety.

title: Herpes zoster (shingles) with convenient dosing
stem:
A 68-year-old woman has a painful vesicular rash in a dermatomal distribution on her left chest.
Symptoms started 36 hours ago.
She wants an oral antiviral with **better bioavailability and less frequent dosing** than acyclovir.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Valacyclovir is a prodrug of acyclovir with improved oral bioavailability and more convenient dosing for HSV/VZV (including shingles).
rationales.B: Acyclovir treats shingles but requires more frequent dosing; the vignette asks for improved bioavailability/convenience.
rationales.C: Oseltamivir treats influenza, not shingles.
rationales.D: Fluconazole treats fungal infections, not shingles.
rationales.E: Terbinafine treats dermatophyte infections like onychomycosis, not shingles.

takeaway: Shingles within 72 hours: oral antivirals help—**valacyclovir** is a high-yield convenient option (prodrug of acyclovir).

title: Influenza treatment in high-risk patient
stem:
A 76-year-old man presents with 24 hours of fever, myalgias, sore throat, and cough.
Rapid test is positive for influenza A.
He has COPD and is at higher risk for complications.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Oseltamivir is a neuraminidase inhibitor used to treat influenza, especially when started early and in high-risk patients.
rationales.B: Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is for COVID-19, not influenza.
rationales.C: Azithromycin is an antibiotic and does not treat influenza (a viral infection).
rationales.D: Acyclovir treats HSV/VZV, not influenza.
rationales.E: Fluconazole treats fungal infections, not influenza.

takeaway: Influenza (especially high-risk patients): start **oseltamivir** as soon as possible (best within 48 hours).

title: Early outpatient COVID-19 (high risk)
stem:
A 67-year-old woman tests positive for COVID-19 with mild symptoms that started 2 days ago.
PMH: CAD and diabetes.
Meds: simvastatin, metformin, lisinopril.
She qualifies for an oral antiviral that must be started within 5 days of symptom onset, but it has important CYP3A drug interaction considerations due to ritonavir.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is an oral antiviral for early outpatient COVID-19 in high-risk patients; ritonavir causes major drug–drug interactions (often requiring temporary statin hold or medication adjustment).
rationales.B: Oseltamivir is for influenza, not COVID-19.
rationales.C: Acyclovir is for HSV/VZV, not COVID-19.
rationales.D: Fluconazole is an antifungal, not a COVID-19 antiviral.
rationales.E: Azithromycin is an antibiotic and does not treat COVID-19.

takeaway: High-yield: **nirmatrelvir/ritonavir** helps prevent severe COVID-19 when started early, but always screen for **drug interactions** (e.g., statins).

title: Strep pharyngitis
stem:
A 16-year-old girl has sore throat, fever, tonsillar exudates, and tender anterior cervical lymph nodes.
Rapid strep test is positive.
She has no medication allergies.
question: Which antibiotic is a high-yield first-line option from the choices below?

rationales.A: Correct. Penicillin V is a classic first-line therapy for Group A streptococcal pharyngitis in patients without penicillin allergy.
rationales.B: Azithromycin is an alternative for patients with penicillin allergy, but penicillin V is preferred when tolerated.
rationales.C: Amoxicillin/clavulanate is broader than needed for uncomplicated strep pharyngitis.
rationales.D: Ceftriaxone is not necessary for routine strep pharyngitis and is typically used for other infections (e.g., gonorrhea, certain severe infections).
rationales.E: Doxycycline is not first-line for strep pharyngitis.

takeaway: Uncomplicated strep throat with no allergy: **penicillin V** is a high-yield narrow-spectrum first-line antibiotic.

title: Dog bite wound prophylaxis
stem:
A 37-year-old man is bitten on the hand by a dog.
The wound is deep and requires irrigation and closure.
He has no drug allergies.
The clinician wants oral antibiotic coverage for common bite pathogens, including Pasteurella and anaerobes.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Amoxicillin/clavulanate provides broad oral coverage for bite wounds, including Pasteurella species and anaerobes.
rationales.B: Cephalexin covers many skin flora but lacks reliable Pasteurella/anaerobe coverage for bite wounds.
rationales.C: Azithromycin is not the preferred empiric choice for dog bite prophylaxis.
rationales.D: Metronidazole covers anaerobes but does not cover Pasteurella well as monotherapy.
rationales.E: Penicillin V is too narrow for typical dog bite polymicrobial coverage.

takeaway: High-yield: animal bite wounds often need Pasteurella + anaerobe coverage—**amoxicillin/clavulanate** is a classic oral choice.

title: Gonorrhea
stem:
A 22-year-old man presents with dysuria and purulent urethral discharge.
NAAT is positive for Neisseria gonorrhoeae.
Chlamydia testing is pending.
question: Which medication is the best treatment choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ceftriaxone (typically IM) is the cornerstone therapy for uncomplicated gonorrhea; additional chlamydia coverage may be needed if not excluded.
rationales.B: Azithromycin is not adequate as monotherapy for gonorrhea in most current approaches due to resistance concerns; ceftriaxone is the key drug.
rationales.C: Metronidazole treats BV/trichomoniasis and anaerobes, not gonorrhea.
rationales.D: Penicillin V is not used for gonorrhea.
rationales.E: Fluconazole treats fungal infections, not gonorrhea.

takeaway: Uncomplicated gonorrhea: **ceftriaxone** is the high-yield treatment anchor (often IM).

title: Severe intra-abdominal sepsis
stem:
A 58-year-old man presents with fever, hypotension, and diffuse abdominal pain.
CT suggests perforated diverticulitis with intra-abdominal abscess.
Blood cultures are pending.
The team wants empiric broad-spectrum IV coverage including **anaerobes and Pseudomonas**.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Piperacillin/tazobactam provides broad empiric coverage for severe intra-abdominal infection, including many gram-negatives, anaerobes, and Pseudomonas.
rationales.B: Ceftriaxone covers many gram-negatives but does not reliably cover Pseudomonas and lacks anaerobe coverage unless combined with another agent.
rationales.C: Amoxicillin/clavulanate is oral and is not adequate for severe septic intra-abdominal infection requiring broad IV therapy.
rationales.D: Metronidazole covers anaerobes but does not cover Pseudomonas/most gram-negative aerobes as monotherapy.
rationales.E: IV vancomycin covers gram-positive organisms (including MRSA) but does not cover gram-negative organisms or anaerobes adequately for this scenario.

takeaway: Severe intra-abdominal sepsis often needs broad IV coverage: **piperacillin/tazobactam** is high-yield for gram-negatives + anaerobes + Pseudomonas.

title: COPD exacerbation adjunct
stem:
A 67-year-old man with COPD (FEV1 42% predicted) presents with increased dyspnea and wheezing for 2 days.
He is using his rescue albuterol inhaler every 2–3 hours with only partial relief.
Vitals: HR 102, BP 138/82, RR 24, SpO2 91% on room air.
The ED team wants to add a short-acting antimuscarinic bronchodilator to improve airflow during the exacerbation.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ipratropium is a short-acting muscarinic antagonist (SAMA) used as an inhaled bronchodilator, often combined with a SABA during COPD exacerbations.
rationales.B: Tiotropium is a long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) for maintenance therapy, not the typical short-acting adjunct used for acute symptom relief in the ED.
rationales.C: Levalbuterol is a SABA option, but he is already using a beta-agonist frequently; the question asks for a short-acting antimuscarinic add-on.
rationales.D: Inhaled budesonide is a controller medication and does not provide rapid bronchodilation for an acute COPD exacerbation.
rationales.E: Montelukast is a leukotriene receptor antagonist used mainly for asthma/allergic rhinitis, not acute COPD bronchodilation.

takeaway: COPD exacerbation bronchodilation: add SAMA (ipratropium) to SABA for short-term symptom improvement.

title: Rescue inhaler alternative
stem:
A 45-year-old woman with asthma needs a rescue inhaler for intermittent symptoms.
She previously used albuterol but experienced significant tremor and palpitations after each dose.
She asks if there is an alternative short-acting beta-agonist that may cause fewer beta-agonist side effects in some patients.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Levalbuterol is the R-enantiomer of albuterol and is a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) sometimes chosen when patients report intolerable tremor/palpitations with albuterol.
rationales.B: Albuterol is an appropriate rescue SABA, but she is specifically asking for an alternative due to side effects.
rationales.C: Salmeterol is a long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) and is not used as a rescue inhaler.
rationales.D: Tiotropium is a long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) used for maintenance, not rescue.
rationales.E: Inhaled budesonide is a controller (ICS) and does not provide immediate rescue relief.

takeaway: Rescue therapy requires a SABA; levalbuterol is a common alternative when albuterol side effects are problematic.

title: COPD maintenance LAMA
stem:
A 69-year-old man with COPD has chronic dyspnea on exertion and uses albuterol several times per day.
He has no history of asthma.
He needs a once-daily maintenance inhaler that improves symptoms and reduces exacerbations through long-acting antimuscarinic bronchodilation.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Tiotropium is a long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) used for COPD maintenance to improve symptoms and reduce exacerbations.
rationales.B: Ipratropium is short-acting (SAMA) and is more often used for acute symptom relief or as an ED exacerbation adjunct.
rationales.C: Albuterol is a rescue inhaler and does not replace long-acting maintenance therapy.
rationales.D: Inhaled budesonide is an inhaled corticosteroid and is not the primary first-choice monotherapy maintenance option for COPD symptoms without asthma features.
rationales.E: Montelukast is used for asthma/allergic rhinitis and is not a standard COPD maintenance bronchodilator.

takeaway: Persistent COPD symptoms → step up to long-acting bronchodilators. LAMA (tiotropium) is a high-yield first-line maintenance choice.

title: Persistent asthma controller
stem:
A 22-year-old woman has asthma symptoms 3–4 days per week and wakes up at night once per week.
She uses albuterol frequently and has no current controller medication.
Lung exam is clear today; peak flow is 80% of personal best.
The clinician recommends starting a daily inhaled corticosteroid to reduce airway inflammation and prevent exacerbations.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Inhaled budesonide is an inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) used as first-line controller therapy for persistent asthma.
rationales.B: Albuterol is a rescue SABA and does not control underlying airway inflammation.
rationales.C: LABA monotherapy is not appropriate for asthma control; LABAs are used with an ICS when indicated.
rationales.D: Ipratropium is a bronchodilator adjunct and is not first-line daily controller therapy for asthma.
rationales.E: Theophylline has narrow therapeutic index and is not first-line controller therapy in most patients.

takeaway: Persistent asthma requires controller therapy—start an inhaled corticosteroid like budesonide.

title: SMART therapy
stem:
A 30-year-old man has moderate persistent asthma with frequent symptoms and uses albuterol daily.
He has had 2 steroid-requiring exacerbations in the last year.
The clinician recommends a single inhaler strategy used for both maintenance and reliever therapy (SMART),
which requires an ICS/LABA combination containing formoterol because of its rapid onset.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Budesonide/formoterol provides both ICS and a rapid-onset LABA (formoterol), making it suitable for SMART (maintenance + reliever) strategies.
rationales.B: Fluticasone/salmeterol is an ICS/LABA, but salmeterol has a slower onset and is not used for SMART reliever dosing.
rationales.C: Salmeterol alone (LABA monotherapy) is not appropriate for asthma and is not a reliever.
rationales.D: Albuterol is a reliever but does not provide the ICS component needed for SMART therapy.
rationales.E: Budesonide alone is an ICS controller, but SMART requires a formoterol-containing ICS/LABA combination.

takeaway: SMART requires an ICS/formoterol inhaler—budesonide/formoterol is a high-yield choice.

title: Asthma controller identification
stem:
A 26-year-old woman with asthma is prescribed a daily controller inhaler.
She asks which medication on her list is the inhaled corticosteroid intended to reduce airway inflammation long term.
Current meds: albuterol as needed, no other inhalers.
question: Which medication is an inhaled corticosteroid controller from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Inhaled fluticasone is an ICS used as long-term controller therapy for asthma.
rationales.B: Albuterol is a rescue SABA and does not control inflammation long term.
rationales.C: Salmeterol is a LABA and should not be used alone in asthma.
rationales.D: Ipratropium is an antimuscarinic bronchodilator used mainly in COPD or as an adjunct in exacerbations.
rationales.E: Montelukast is an oral leukotriene receptor antagonist and is not an inhaled corticosteroid.

takeaway: ICS controllers (like inhaled fluticasone) reduce airway inflammation; SABAs are for rescue only.

title: Step-up asthma therapy
stem:
A 35-year-old woman with asthma has persistent daytime symptoms despite consistent use of low-dose inhaled fluticasone.
She needs step-up therapy with an inhaled corticosteroid plus long-acting beta-agonist (ICS/LABA) combination for better control.
She is counseled that this inhaler is NOT for immediate rescue relief.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Fluticasone/salmeterol is an ICS/LABA combination used for step-up asthma control when an ICS alone is inadequate.
rationales.B: Albuterol is a rescue SABA and does not provide the controller step-up combination needed.
rationales.C: LABA monotherapy (salmeterol alone) is not appropriate in asthma due to increased risk when used without an ICS.
rationales.D: Ipratropium is not standard step-up controller therapy for persistent asthma.
rationales.E: Montelukast can be an add-on in some patients but is not the classic ICS/LABA step-up in this scenario.

takeaway: Uncontrolled asthma on ICS → step up to an ICS/LABA combo like fluticasone/salmeterol (controller, not rescue).

title: Identify the mometasone + formoterol combination
stem:
A 29-year-old man with asthma is prescribed an ICS/LABA combination inhaler.
He is allergic to lactose-containing dry powder inhalers and prefers an HFA inhaler.
His clinician chooses the product that combines mometasone (ICS) with formoterol (LABA).
question: Which medication matches this inhaler combination from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Dulera contains mometasone (ICS) plus formoterol (LABA).
rationales.B: Advair contains fluticasone (ICS) plus salmeterol (LABA), not mometasone plus formoterol.
rationales.C: Symbicort contains budesonide (ICS) plus formoterol (LABA), not mometasone.
rationales.D: Flovent contains fluticasone alone (ICS only), not an ICS/LABA combination.
rationales.E: Tiotropium is a LAMA bronchodilator and is not an ICS/LABA inhaler.

takeaway: Know the ICS/LABA combos: Dulera = mometasone/formoterol; Symbicort = budesonide/formoterol; Advair = fluticasone/salmeterol.

title: Asthma + allergic rhinitis add-on
stem:
A 16-year-old boy has mild persistent asthma and significant seasonal allergic rhinitis.
Despite a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid, he still has nighttime cough during allergy season.
His clinician adds an oral leukotriene receptor antagonist that can help both asthma and allergic rhinitis symptoms.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Montelukast is a leukotriene receptor antagonist used as an add-on in asthma and for allergic rhinitis symptoms.
rationales.B: Omalizumab is for moderate-to-severe allergic asthma requiring specialist criteria (IgE/allergen sensitization) and is not first-line for mild persistent disease.
rationales.C: Tiotropium is a LAMA used mainly for COPD (and select asthma step-up), not the classic oral add-on for allergy-driven symptoms.
rationales.D: Salmeterol is a LABA and is not used alone in asthma; it does not address allergic rhinitis symptoms.
rationales.E: Theophylline is not a preferred add-on due to narrow therapeutic index and interactions.

takeaway: Montelukast is a high-yield oral add-on for asthma patients with prominent allergic rhinitis triggers.

title: Severe eosinophilic asthma + eczema
stem:
A 38-year-old woman has severe asthma despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroid/LABA therapy.
Labs: eosinophils 620 cells/µL.
She also has moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis requiring frequent topical steroid use.
Her IgE level is not elevated and allergy testing is negative.
The pulmonologist recommends a biologic targeting IL-4/IL-13 signaling.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Dupilumab targets IL-4/IL-13 signaling and is used for type 2 inflammatory disease, including eosinophilic asthma and atopic dermatitis.
rationales.B: Omalizumab targets IgE and is used for allergic asthma with elevated IgE and allergen sensitization—this patient does not fit that profile.
rationales.C: Roflumilast is a PDE-4 inhibitor used to reduce COPD exacerbations in chronic bronchitis; it is not an asthma biologic.
rationales.D: Theophylline is not preferred due to narrow therapeutic index and does not target the eosinophilic/eczema phenotype.
rationales.E: Montelukast can be an add-on for asthma/allergic rhinitis but is not adequate for severe eosinophilic asthma uncontrolled on high-dose therapy.

takeaway: Severe eosinophilic asthma with atopic dermatitis points toward dupilumab (IL‑4/IL‑13 pathway).

title: Allergic asthma biologic
stem:
A 46-year-old man has moderate-to-severe asthma with frequent exacerbations despite medium-high dose ICS/LABA therapy.
Allergy testing is positive to dust mites and cat dander.
Total IgE is elevated.
The allergist recommends an injectable biologic that binds IgE to reduce allergic inflammation.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Omalizumab is an anti-IgE monoclonal antibody used for allergic asthma with elevated IgE and allergen sensitization.
rationales.B: Dupilumab is used for eosinophilic/type 2 asthma and atopic dermatitis; the vignette specifically emphasizes elevated IgE and allergic sensitization—classic omalizumab criteria.
rationales.C: Montelukast can help mild-to-moderate asthma and allergic rhinitis, but it is not the biologic therapy described for refractory allergic asthma.
rationales.D: Roflumilast is for COPD chronic bronchitis with frequent exacerbations, not allergic asthma.
rationales.E: Theophylline is not first-line due to toxicity and does not specifically target IgE-mediated allergic asthma.

takeaway: Allergic asthma + elevated IgE + positive allergen testing → omalizumab (anti-IgE).

title: Severe COPD with chronic bronchitis + frequent exacerbations
stem:
A 64-year-old man has COPD with chronic bronchitis (daily productive cough).
Despite LAMA/LABA therapy, he has had 3 COPD exacerbations requiring steroids/antibiotics in the last year.
FEV1 is 38% predicted.
The clinician considers adding an oral medication to reduce future exacerbations.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Roflumilast is a PDE-4 inhibitor used to reduce COPD exacerbations in severe COPD with chronic bronchitis and frequent exacerbations.
rationales.B: Montelukast is primarily for asthma/allergic rhinitis, not COPD exacerbation prevention in chronic bronchitis.
rationales.C: Omalizumab is for allergic asthma, not COPD.
rationales.D: Theophylline may provide bronchodilation but is limited by toxicity and is not the classic add-on for reducing frequent COPD exacerbations in chronic bronchitis.
rationales.E: Inhaled corticosteroids may be used in select COPD patients (e.g., eosinophilic features), but the vignette specifically points to the oral PDE-4 inhibitor add-on for chronic bronchitis exacerbations.

takeaway: Roflumilast is a high-yield add-on for severe COPD with chronic bronchitis and frequent exacerbations.

title: Narrow therapeutic index bronchodilator
stem:
A 58-year-old man with COPD is taking a bronchodilator that requires serum level monitoring.
After starting clarithromycin for pneumonia, he develops nausea, vomiting, tremor, and palpitations.
His serum medication level returns elevated.
question: Which medication is most likely responsible from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Theophylline has a narrow therapeutic index and levels can rise with CYP inhibitors (e.g., certain macrolides), causing GI and cardiac/CNS toxicity.
rationales.B: Tiotropium is inhaled and does not require serum level monitoring; it is not associated with classic theophylline toxicity symptoms.
rationales.C: Montelukast does not require serum drug-level monitoring and is not known for this toxicity pattern.
rationales.D: Inhaled budesonide does not require serum level monitoring and does not cause this toxicity pattern.
rationales.E: Roflumilast can cause weight loss and GI effects, but it is not the classic narrow-therapeutic-index drug with level monitoring and macrolide interactions.

takeaway: Theophylline = narrow therapeutic index. Watch interactions (e.g., macrolides) and toxicity (GI, tremor, arrhythmias).

title: Non-sedating allergy relief
stem:
A 34-year-old commercial truck driver has seasonal allergic rhinitis with sneezing, itchy eyes, and runny nose.
He must avoid sedation while driving.
He previously tried diphenhydramine and felt very drowsy.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below to minimize sedation?

rationales.A: Correct. Fexofenadine is a second-generation antihistamine that is minimally sedating and is a good choice when avoiding drowsiness is important.
rationales.B: Diphenhydramine is a first-generation antihistamine that commonly causes significant sedation and anticholinergic effects.
rationales.C: Cetirizine is effective but can still cause some sedation in many patients.
rationales.D: Levocetirizine may also cause some sedation; it is not as reliably non-sedating as fexofenadine.
rationales.E: Pseudoephedrine is a decongestant—not an antihistamine—and can cause stimulation, insomnia, and increased heart rate/BP.

takeaway: When sedation matters (drivers, pilots, students), fexofenadine is a high-yield ‘least sedating’ antihistamine choice.

title: Onychomycosis
stem:
A 52-year-old man has thickened, yellow, brittle toenails with subungual debris.
KOH prep is positive for dermatophytes.
He requests the most effective oral medication for toenail onychomycosis.
Baseline liver enzymes are normal.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Oral terbinafine is a first-line, high-efficacy option for dermatophyte onychomycosis (counsel about liver toxicity risk and monitoring when indicated).
rationales.B: Fluconazole can treat some fungal infections and is sometimes used off-label for onychomycosis, but terbinafine is a classic first-line oral option for dermatophytes.
rationales.C: Nystatin is mainly used for Candida (e.g., oral thrush) and is not effective for dermatophyte nail infections.
rationales.D: Systemic ketoconazole is generally avoided due to hepatotoxicity risk; topical ketoconazole is not effective for onychomycosis.
rationales.E: Valacyclovir is an antiviral used for HSV/VZV infections, not fungal nail disease.

takeaway: Dermatophyte toenail onychomycosis → oral terbinafine is a high-yield first-line therapy; remember liver toxicity counseling.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: Allergic rhinitis best for congestion
stem:
A 28-year-old woman has allergic rhinitis with prominent nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, and sneezing.
She wants the most effective medication class for nasal symptoms and congestion with minimal systemic effects.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Intranasal corticosteroids (like mometasone nasal) are among the most effective treatments for allergic rhinitis, especially nasal congestion.
rationales.B: Diphenhydramine can relieve symptoms but is sedating and anticholinergic and is not preferred for chronic management.
rationales.C: Pseudoephedrine can help congestion short term but can cause stimulant side effects and is not as effective for the full spectrum of rhinitis symptoms as intranasal steroids.
rationales.D: Fexofenadine helps sneezing/itching/runny nose but intranasal steroids are typically best for congestion.
rationales.E: Montelukast can help, especially with comorbid asthma, but is generally not first-line over intranasal steroids for rhinitis.

takeaway: Allergic rhinitis with congestion: intranasal steroids (mometasone) are high-yield first-line options.

title: Oral decongestant
stem:
A 31-year-old man has a viral upper respiratory infection with bothersome nasal congestion.
PMH: no hypertension, no arrhythmia, no glaucoma.
He wants an oral medication that shrinks nasal mucosal swelling and improves congestion.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Pseudoephedrine is an oral sympathomimetic decongestant that reduces nasal congestion by vasoconstriction (counsel on insomnia, jitteriness, ↑BP/HR).
rationales.B: Fexofenadine is an antihistamine and is better for allergy symptoms than viral congestion.
rationales.C: Diphenhydramine may help rhinorrhea/sneezing but causes sedation and is not the best targeted oral decongestant.
rationales.D: Mometasone nasal is effective for allergic rhinitis congestion, but the question asks specifically for an oral decongestant option for a viral URI.
rationales.E: Montelukast is not used for viral congestion relief.

takeaway: Pseudoephedrine is a high-yield oral decongestant—avoid/monitor in HTN, arrhythmias, and insomnia-prone patients.

title: Peptic ulcer mucosal protection
stem:
A 59-year-old man has epigastric burning and is diagnosed with a duodenal ulcer on endoscopy.
He asks for a medication that works by coating the ulcer base and providing a protective barrier (rather than primarily suppressing acid).
He takes levothyroxine each morning and wants to avoid drug-binding interactions as much as possible.
question: Which medication best matches this ‘ulcer coating’ mechanism from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Sucralfate forms a protective barrier over ulcer craters and promotes healing; it can bind other oral meds, so dosing separation is important.
rationales.B: Famotidine reduces acid via H2 blockade; it does not primarily coat ulcers.
rationales.C: Omeprazole suppresses gastric acid via proton pump inhibition; it does not primarily coat ulcers.
rationales.D: Bismuth can provide mucosal protection in some regimens, but the classic ‘forms a viscous protective barrier over ulcers’ teaching point is sucralfate.
rationales.E: Ondansetron is an antiemetic and does not treat peptic ulcers.

takeaway: Sucralfate ‘coats’ ulcers—counsel empty-stomach dosing and spacing from other oral meds (binding interactions).
module_tags: Module 8a Acid Suppression (PPIs, H2 Blockers, Mucosal Protection)

title: Delayed chemotherapy-induced nausea
stem:
A 55-year-old woman is starting highly emetogenic chemotherapy.
Her oncologist prescribes a multi-drug antiemetic regimen to prevent both acute and delayed nausea/vomiting.
One of the medications blocks neurokinin-1 (NK1) receptors and is particularly helpful for delayed chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting.
question: Which medication is the NK1 antagonist from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Aprepitant is an NK1 receptor antagonist used for prevention of chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting, especially delayed CINV.
rationales.B: Ondansetron is a 5-HT3 antagonist and is excellent for acute CINV but is not an NK1 antagonist.
rationales.C: Metoclopramide is a dopamine antagonist/prokinetic and is not an NK1 antagonist.
rationales.D: Promethazine is an antihistamine with antiemetic properties; it is not an NK1 antagonist.
rationales.E: Scopolamine is an anticholinergic used for motion sickness; it is not an NK1 antagonist.

takeaway: Aprepitant = NK1 antagonist for delayed CINV (often paired with 5‑HT3 antagonist + steroid).

title: Post-op nausea/vomiting
stem:
A 41-year-old woman develops nausea and vomiting after outpatient surgery with general anesthesia.
Vitals are stable; abdominal exam is benign.
The clinician chooses an antiemetic that blocks 5-HT3 receptors and is commonly used for postoperative nausea and vomiting.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Ondansetron is a 5-HT3 antagonist commonly used for postoperative and chemotherapy-related nausea/vomiting (watch QT prolongation risk).
rationales.B: Scopolamine is used primarily for motion sickness and can be used for prevention, but the classic acute post-op antiemetic is ondansetron.
rationales.C: Promethazine can be used for nausea but is more sedating and anticholinergic than ondansetron.
rationales.D: Metoclopramide is useful in gastroparesis and some nausea cases but is not the classic first-line agent for PONV compared with ondansetron.
rationales.E: Famotidine is an H2 blocker for acid suppression and does not treat nausea/vomiting.

takeaway: Ondansetron (5‑HT3 antagonist) is a high-yield first-line antiemetic for PONV and CINV; remember QT risk.

title: Diabetic gastroparesis
stem:
A 57-year-old man with long-standing type 2 diabetes has early satiety, bloating, and nausea after meals.
A gastric emptying study confirms gastroparesis.
The clinician prescribes a dopamine antagonist that increases GI motility and can relieve nausea.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Metoclopramide is a prokinetic antiemetic used for gastroparesis; counsel about extrapyramidal symptoms and tardive dyskinesia risk.
rationales.B: Ondansetron treats nausea but does not improve gastric emptying/motility in gastroparesis.
rationales.C: Promethazine can treat nausea but does not address impaired gastric emptying and is sedating.
rationales.D: Sucralfate coats ulcers and does not treat gastroparesis.
rationales.E: Aprepitant is used for chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting prophylaxis, not diabetic gastroparesis.

takeaway: Gastroparesis needs motility help—metoclopramide is a high-yield prokinetic (watch EPS/tardive dyskinesia).

title: Antiemetic with strong sedation
stem:
A 26-year-old woman presents with acute nausea and vomiting from viral gastroenteritis.
She is given an antiemetic that is very sedating and has strong anticholinergic properties (dry mouth, urinary retention).
question: Which medication best matches this description from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Promethazine is an antihistamine antiemetic that is notably sedating and anticholinergic.
rationales.B: Ondansetron is generally less sedating and is not strongly anticholinergic.
rationales.C: Aprepitant is mainly used for chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting prophylaxis, not routine viral gastroenteritis; it is not known for strong anticholinergic sedation.
rationales.D: Metoclopramide can cause EPS but is not classically described as the strongly anticholinergic sedating antiemetic.
rationales.E: Scopolamine is anticholinergic and can cause dry mouth and sedation, but it is primarily used for motion sickness prevention rather than acute viral gastroenteritis treatment.

takeaway: Promethazine is a potent, sedating antiemetic—counsel on drowsiness and anticholinergic side effects.
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: Motion sickness prevention
stem:
A 29-year-old woman gets severe motion sickness on cruises.
She wants a medication that can be applied as a transdermal patch before travel to prevent nausea.
She has no history of glaucoma.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Transdermal scopolamine is an anticholinergic commonly used for prevention of motion sickness and vestibular nausea.
rationales.B: Ondansetron treats nausea but is not the classic best prophylactic option for motion sickness in this list.
rationales.C: Aprepitant is used for chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting prophylaxis, not motion sickness.
rationales.D: Sucralfate treats ulcers and does not prevent motion sickness.
rationales.E: Loperamide treats diarrhea and does not prevent motion sickness.

takeaway: Motion sickness prevention: think scopolamine patch (anticholinergic) and counsel on dry mouth/blurred vision.

title: First-line osmotic laxative
stem:
A 44-year-old woman has chronic constipation with hard stools 2–3 times per week.
She has tried increasing fiber and water intake with limited benefit.
She wants a medication that draws water into the colon to soften stool and improve bowel frequency,
and can be used as an over-the-counter osmotic laxative.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Polyethylene glycol is an osmotic laxative that increases water in the stool and is commonly used for chronic constipation.
rationales.B: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative often used for short-term constipation or bowel prep; it is not the classic first-line daily osmotic option.
rationales.C: Docusate is a stool softener with variable effectiveness and is not as reliably effective as polyethylene glycol for chronic constipation.
rationales.D: Senna/docusate contains a stimulant (senna) plus softener; it is often used in opioid-related constipation but is not the classic first-line osmotic option.
rationales.E: Loperamide is an antidiarrheal and would worsen constipation.

takeaway: Chronic constipation: polyethylene glycol is a high-yield first-line osmotic laxative option.

title: Colonoscopy bowel prep
stem:
A 60-year-old man is scheduled for a screening colonoscopy tomorrow morning.
He needs an effective bowel cleansing regimen that includes a large-volume osmotic solution with electrolytes to prevent significant fluid/electrolyte shifts.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Polyethylene glycol with electrolytes is a standard colonoscopy bowel preparation solution designed to cleanse the colon while limiting electrolyte disturbances.
rationales.B: MiraLAX (PEG without electrolytes) can be used in some constipation regimens, but the classic dedicated colonoscopy prep in this list is PEG with electrolytes.
rationales.C: Lactulose is used for constipation and hepatic encephalopathy, not as the primary colonoscopy prep solution.
rationales.D: Bisacodyl can be an adjunct, but it is not the main large-volume electrolyte-containing bowel prep solution described.
rationales.E: Loperamide is an antidiarrheal and would interfere with bowel cleansing.

takeaway: For colonoscopy prep, PEG with electrolytes is a high-yield choice for effective cleansing with electrolyte support.

title: Stimulant laxative for short-term constipation
stem:
A 51-year-old woman has not had a bowel movement in 4 days and feels bloated.
She wants a medication that directly stimulates colonic peristalsis for short-term relief.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative that increases intestinal motility and is commonly used for short-term constipation relief.
rationales.B: Polyethylene glycol is an osmotic laxative and is often used for chronic constipation; it does not directly stimulate peristalsis as the primary mechanism.
rationales.C: Loperamide is an antidiarrheal and would worsen constipation.
rationales.D: Diphenoxylate/atropine is an antidiarrheal and would worsen constipation.
rationales.E: Dicyclomine is an antispasmodic used for IBS cramping and is not a laxative.

takeaway: Bisacodyl is a high-yield stimulant laxative for short-term constipation or as bowel-prep adjunct.

title: Oral thrush after inhaled steroids
stem:
A 63-year-old man with COPD/asthma overlap uses an inhaled corticosteroid daily.
He now has white, curd-like plaques on the tongue and buccal mucosa that scrape off with mild bleeding underneath.
He is otherwise well and immunocompetent.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Nystatin oral suspension (swish-and-swallow) is a classic first-line treatment for uncomplicated oral candidiasis (thrush).
rationales.B: Fluconazole can treat thrush, but topical therapy like nystatin is often first-line for uncomplicated cases in an immunocompetent patient.
rationales.C: Acyclovir treats HSV infections and does not treat Candida thrush.
rationales.D: Valacyclovir treats HSV/VZV infections and does not treat Candida thrush.
rationales.E: Terbinafine treats dermatophyte infections (e.g., onychomycosis) and does not treat oral candidiasis.

takeaway: Thrush (often from inhaled steroids) → nystatin is a high-yield first-line option; counsel rinsing mouth after ICS use.

title: Opioid-induced constipation prevention
stem:
A 56-year-old man is started on oxycodone for severe chronic back pain.
He has a history of constipation and wants to prevent opioid-induced constipation from the start.
The clinician prescribes a combination product containing a stimulant laxative plus stool softener.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Senna/docusate combines a stimulant laxative (senna) with a stool softener (docusate) and is commonly used to prevent/treat opioid-induced constipation.
rationales.B: Loperamide treats diarrhea and would worsen constipation.
rationales.C: Ondansetron is an antiemetic and can worsen constipation; it is not used to prevent opioid-induced constipation.
rationales.D: Diphenoxylate/atropine treats diarrhea and would worsen constipation.
rationales.E: Dicyclomine is an antispasmodic and can worsen constipation.

takeaway: Opioids often require a bowel regimen—senna/docusate is a high-yield prophylaxis choice.
module_tags: Module 5b Opioids & Pain Management Basics + Adjuncts

title: Hepatic encephalopathy
stem:
A 63-year-old man with cirrhosis is brought in for confusion and asterixis.
Labs: ammonia elevated; electrolytes normal.
The team starts a medication that acidifies the colon and traps ammonia as ammonium, promoting excretion via increased stool frequency.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Lactulose is used for hepatic encephalopathy by reducing ammonia absorption and increasing fecal nitrogen excretion (goal: multiple soft stools/day).
rationales.B: Polyethylene glycol is an osmotic laxative for constipation but does not specifically treat hyperammonemia/hepatic encephalopathy.
rationales.C: Loperamide reduces stool frequency and would worsen ammonia clearance.
rationales.D: Ondansetron is an antiemetic and does not treat hepatic encephalopathy.
rationales.E: Dicyclomine is an antispasmodic and does not treat hepatic encephalopathy.

takeaway: Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose is first-line—dose to 2–3 soft stools/day to reduce ammonia burden.

title: IBS-C with abdominal pain
stem:
A 37-year-old woman has irritable bowel syndrome with constipation (IBS-C).
She reports chronic abdominal pain that improves after bowel movements and has hard stools with straining.
Fiber and polyethylene glycol have helped stool frequency only partially, but pain persists.
The clinician prescribes a medication that increases intestinal fluid secretion and can improve both constipation and abdominal pain.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Linaclotide is used for IBS-C and chronic idiopathic constipation and can improve bowel movements and abdominal pain (diarrhea is a key adverse effect).
rationales.B: Loperamide treats diarrhea and would worsen constipation.
rationales.C: Dicyclomine can reduce cramping but does not address constipation and can worsen constipation due to anticholinergic effects.
rationales.D: Bisacodyl can provide short-term constipation relief but is not the targeted IBS-C agent improving pain described here.
rationales.E: Diphenoxylate/atropine treats diarrhea and would worsen constipation.

takeaway: IBS‑C with pain: linaclotide is a high-yield option (counsel diarrhea risk).

title: Acute non-bloody diarrhea
stem:
A 29-year-old man returns from travel with 2 days of watery diarrhea (6 episodes/day).
He has no fever, no blood in stool, and is well hydrated.
He wants symptomatic relief to reduce stool frequency while he continues oral rehydration.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Loperamide is an antidiarrheal that decreases intestinal motility and can provide symptomatic relief in uncomplicated, non-bloody diarrhea without systemic signs.
rationales.B: Lactulose is a laxative (and hepatic encephalopathy therapy) and would worsen diarrhea.
rationales.C: Linaclotide increases intestinal fluid secretion and can cause diarrhea—would worsen symptoms.
rationales.D: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative and would worsen diarrhea.
rationales.E: Polyethylene glycol is an osmotic laxative and would worsen diarrhea.

takeaway: For uncomplicated watery diarrhea (no fever/blood), loperamide can be used for symptomatic relief with hydration.

title: Prescription antidiarrheal with atropine
stem:
A 40-year-old woman has chronic functional diarrhea and wants a prescription antidiarrheal.
Her clinician chooses a medication that is an opioid derivative combined with atropine to discourage misuse/overdose.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Diphenoxylate is an opioid derivative antidiarrheal and is combined with atropine to discourage abuse.
rationales.B: Loperamide is an OTC antidiarrheal and does not contain atropine.
rationales.C: Dicyclomine is an antispasmodic used for IBS cramping, not a primary antidiarrheal with atropine.
rationales.D: Lactulose is a laxative and would worsen diarrhea.
rationales.E: Ondansetron is an antiemetic and does not treat chronic diarrhea as described.

takeaway: Diphenoxylate/atropine is a classic prescription antidiarrheal—atropine is included to deter misuse.
module_tags: Module 10e Eye/Ear + Dermatology + Misc "Can't Miss" Top 300

title: IBS abdominal cramping
stem:
A 33-year-old man has irritable bowel syndrome with episodic abdominal cramping and urgency.
Stool studies and colonoscopy are normal.
He requests a PRN medication that reduces intestinal smooth muscle spasm.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Dicyclomine is an anticholinergic antispasmodic used for IBS-related abdominal cramping.
rationales.B: Linaclotide treats IBS-C and constipation; it is not the primary PRN antispasmodic for cramping described here.
rationales.C: Lactulose is a laxative and would not treat cramping and urgency.
rationales.D: Loperamide can reduce diarrhea frequency but does not directly treat smooth muscle spasm/cramping as the main mechanism.
rationales.E: Sucralfate coats ulcers and does not treat IBS cramping.

takeaway: IBS cramping: dicyclomine is a high-yield antispasmodic option (counsel anticholinergic effects).

title: Mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis
stem:
A 24-year-old man has 6 weeks of bloody diarrhea, urgency, and crampy abdominal pain.
Colonoscopy shows continuous colonic mucosal inflammation starting at the rectum, consistent with ulcerative colitis.
He has mild-to-moderate disease without systemic toxicity.
question: Which medication is the best first-line choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Mesalamine (5-ASA) is a first-line therapy for mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis to reduce colonic inflammation.
rationales.B: Infliximab is a biologic used for moderate-to-severe disease or refractory cases, not first-line for mild-to-moderate UC.
rationales.C: Loperamide can reduce diarrhea but is not a treatment for UC inflammation and can be risky in active severe colitis.
rationales.D: Dicyclomine treats IBS cramping and does not treat IBD inflammation.
rationales.E: Metronidazole is not standard first-line therapy for ulcerative colitis inflammation.

takeaway: Ulcerative colitis (mild–moderate): mesalamine is a high-yield first-line anti-inflammatory therapy.

title: Moderate-to-severe IBD escalation
stem:
A 29-year-old woman has Crohn disease with persistent abdominal pain and diarrhea despite corticosteroid induction therapy.
She has had 2 hospitalizations for flares in the past year.
Her gastroenterologist recommends starting an anti-TNF biologic and orders TB screening before the first infusion.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Infliximab is an anti-TNF monoclonal antibody used for moderate-to-severe Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis; TB/HBV screening is important before therapy.
rationales.B: Mesalamine is most commonly used for mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis and is generally insufficient alone for moderate-to-severe Crohn disease.
rationales.C: Loperamide provides symptomatic relief but does not treat underlying inflammatory disease and may be unsafe in severe colitis.
rationales.D: Sucralfate treats peptic ulcers and does not treat IBD.
rationales.E: Dicyclomine may help cramping but does not treat Crohn inflammatory activity.

takeaway: Escalation for moderate–severe IBD often includes anti‑TNF therapy like infliximab—screen for TB/HBV before starting.

title: Nonpurulent cellulitis
stem:
A 48-year-old man develops redness, warmth, and tenderness on his lower leg after a minor abrasion.
There is no abscess or purulent drainage.
He has no history of MRSA, no injection drug use, and no recent hospitalization.
Vitals are normal.
question: Which oral antibiotic is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Cephalexin is a first-line oral option for uncomplicated nonpurulent cellulitis targeting streptococci and MSSA.
rationales.B: TMP/SMX has good MRSA activity but limited streptococcal coverage when used alone for nonpurulent cellulitis; it is not the best single agent here.
rationales.C: Doxycycline covers MRSA but is less reliable for streptococci as monotherapy for nonpurulent cellulitis.
rationales.D: Clindamycin can cover streptococci and MRSA, but cephalexin is a cleaner first-line choice when MRSA risk is low and C. difficile risk is a concern.
rationales.E: Ciprofloxacin is not a preferred agent for typical skin/soft tissue cellulitis and has unnecessary broad gram-negative coverage.

takeaway: Nonpurulent cellulitis (low MRSA risk) → cephalexin is a high-yield first-line oral choice.

title: Neutropenic fever
stem:
A 62-year-old woman receiving chemotherapy presents with fever (39.1°C) and chills.
Labs: ANC 280 cells/µL.
She is hemodynamically stable but meets criteria for neutropenic fever.
The team wants an IV antipseudomonal beta-lactam; the attending asks specifically for the 4th-generation cephalosporin option.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Cefepime is a 4th-generation cephalosporin with antipseudomonal coverage and is a high-yield first-line option for empiric neutropenic fever.
rationales.B: Ceftriaxone is a 3rd-generation cephalosporin but does not reliably cover Pseudomonas for neutropenic fever empiric therapy.
rationales.C: Cephalexin is an oral 1st-generation cephalosporin used for skin infections, not neutropenic fever.
rationales.D: Vancomycin may be added for specific indications (e.g., line infection, MRSA concern) but is not adequate monotherapy for neutropenic fever empiric gram-negative coverage.
rationales.E: Azithromycin is not appropriate empiric monotherapy for neutropenic fever.

takeaway: Neutropenic fever empiric therapy often starts with an antipseudomonal beta-lactam—cefepime is a classic 4th-gen cephalosporin choice.

title: Broad intra-abdominal coverage
stem:
A 54-year-old man presents with severe abdominal pain and fever.
CT suggests perforated appendicitis with peritonitis.
The surgeon requests empiric IV antibiotics that cover gram-negatives, anaerobes, and Pseudomonas while awaiting surgery.
question: Which medication is the best single-agent choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Piperacillin/tazobactam provides broad coverage including many gram-negatives, anaerobes, and Pseudomonas—high-yield for severe intra-abdominal infection.
rationales.B: Ceftriaxone has good gram-negative coverage but lacks adequate anaerobic coverage as a single agent for perforated appendicitis.
rationales.C: Cefepime covers Pseudomonas but does not provide reliable anaerobic coverage as monotherapy.
rationales.D: Azithromycin is not appropriate monotherapy for intra-abdominal sepsis/peritonitis.
rationales.E: Vancomycin targets gram-positive organisms and does not cover anaerobes/gram-negative pathogens required here.

takeaway: Severe intra-abdominal infection/perforation: piperacillin/tazobactam is a high-yield broad empiric single-agent option.

title: Strep pharyngitis
stem:
A 19-year-old college student has sore throat, fever, tender anterior cervical nodes, and tonsillar exudates.
Rapid strep test is positive.
He has no drug allergies.
The clinician chooses the narrowest effective oral antibiotic.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Penicillin V is a classic first-line narrow-spectrum option for confirmed group A strep pharyngitis in patients without penicillin allergy.
rationales.B: Amoxicillin/clavulanate is broader than needed and increases adverse effect risk (e.g., diarrhea).
rationales.C: Ciprofloxacin is not recommended for strep pharyngitis and is unnecessarily broad.
rationales.D: Clindamycin is typically reserved for penicillin-allergic patients or specific situations; it also carries C. difficile risk.
rationales.E: IV vancomycin is inappropriate for outpatient strep pharyngitis.

takeaway: When strep throat is confirmed and no allergy exists, penicillin V is high-yield ‘narrow and effective.’

title: Bacterial vaginosis
stem:
A 27-year-old woman reports thin gray vaginal discharge with a fishy odor.
Vaginal pH is 5.5 and wet mount shows clue cells.
Pregnancy test is negative.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Metronidazole is first-line therapy for bacterial vaginosis.
rationales.B: Fluconazole treats vulvovaginal candidiasis (thick white discharge, normal pH), not BV.
rationales.C: Nitrofurantoin treats uncomplicated cystitis, not BV.
rationales.D: Azithromycin is used for certain bacterial STIs/respiratory infections, not BV first-line treatment.
rationales.E: Valacyclovir treats HSV/VZV infections, not BV.

takeaway: BV findings (clue cells, fishy odor, ↑pH) → metronidazole is high-yield first-line therapy; counsel to avoid alcohol.

title: Influenza treatment
stem:
A 72-year-old woman with COPD develops abrupt fever, myalgias, headache, and cough.
Rapid influenza testing is positive.
Symptoms began 24 hours ago.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below to treat influenza?

rationales.A: Correct. Oseltamivir is a neuraminidase inhibitor used for influenza treatment and is most effective when started early (and is recommended for high-risk patients).
rationales.B: Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is used for COVID-19, not influenza.
rationales.C: Acyclovir treats HSV/VZV infections, not influenza.
rationales.D: Azithromycin is an antibiotic and does not treat influenza (a viral illness).
rationales.E: Fluconazole treats fungal infections and does not treat influenza.

takeaway: Confirmed influenza in a high-risk patient → start oseltamivir as early as possible.

title: Early COVID-19 outpatient therapy
stem:
A 68-year-old man with diabetes and coronary artery disease tests positive for COVID-19.
Symptoms started 2 days ago (mild cough, sore throat, fatigue) and he is not hypoxic.
His clinician wants an oral antiviral for high-risk outpatients that must be started within a few days of symptom onset.
Medication list includes simvastatin and apixaban.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below (noting that drug–drug interactions must be reviewed/managed)?

rationales.A: Correct. Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is an oral antiviral used for early COVID-19 in high-risk outpatients; ritonavir causes major CYP3A interactions, so concomitant meds must be reviewed and managed.
rationales.B: Oseltamivir treats influenza, not COVID-19.
rationales.C: Acyclovir treats HSV/VZV infections, not COVID-19.
rationales.D: Fluconazole treats fungal infections and does not treat COVID-19.
rationales.E: Metronidazole is an antibiotic and does not treat COVID-19.

takeaway: High-risk outpatient COVID early in illness: nirmatrelvir/ritonavir is high-yield—always check major CYP3A drug interactions.

title: HSV encephalitis
stem:
A 57-year-old man presents with fever, headache, confusion, and new focal seizures.
MRI suggests temporal lobe involvement.
CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein.
The team suspects HSV encephalitis and wants immediate antiviral therapy.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Acyclovir (IV in severe disease) is the standard therapy for suspected HSV encephalitis and should be started promptly.
rationales.B: Valacyclovir is used for HSV/VZV but is typically used orally for less severe outpatient infections; suspected encephalitis requires urgent acyclovir therapy.
rationales.C: Oseltamivir treats influenza, not HSV encephalitis.
rationales.D: Fluconazole treats fungal infections and does not treat HSV.
rationales.E: Metronidazole is an antibiotic and does not treat HSV encephalitis.

takeaway: Suspected HSV encephalitis is an emergency—start (IV) acyclovir promptly.

title: Herpes zoster outpatient therapy
stem:
A 61-year-old woman develops a painful vesicular rash in a dermatomal distribution on her right torso.
The rash began yesterday.
She is immunocompetent and has normal renal function.
The clinician prescribes an oral antiviral with improved bioavailability compared with acyclovir.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Valacyclovir is a prodrug of acyclovir with better oral bioavailability and is commonly used for shingles when started early.
rationales.B: Acyclovir can treat shingles, but valacyclovir is often preferred orally due to improved bioavailability and convenient dosing.
rationales.C: Oseltamivir treats influenza, not shingles.
rationales.D: Fluconazole treats fungal infections and does not treat VZV.
rationales.E: Terbinafine treats dermatophyte fungal infections, not VZV.

takeaway: Shingles within 72 hours: start an antiviral—valacyclovir is a high-yield convenient oral choice.

title: Vulvovaginal candidiasis
stem:
A 30-year-old woman has intense vulvar itching and thick, white “cottage cheese” vaginal discharge.
Vaginal pH is 4.0.
Wet mount shows budding yeast.
She is not pregnant.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Fluconazole is a common oral treatment for uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis (often a single dose).
rationales.B: Metronidazole treats bacterial vaginosis/trichomoniasis, not yeast infection.
rationales.C: Nitrofurantoin treats uncomplicated cystitis, not yeast infection.
rationales.D: Valacyclovir treats HSV/VZV, not yeast infection.
rationales.E: Nystatin is used for oral candidiasis; fluconazole is the classic oral agent for vulvovaginal candidiasis.

takeaway: Normal pH + thick white discharge + yeast on microscopy → treat vulvovaginal candidiasis with fluconazole (if not pregnant).

title: OA pain with sulfonamide allergy
stem:
A 63-year-old woman has knee osteoarthritis with daily pain and morning stiffness <30 minutes.
PMH: anaphylaxis to trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (sulfonamide antibiotic).
She previously had dyspepsia with high-dose ibuprofen and wants an NSAID option for chronic OA pain.
The clinician wants to avoid celecoxib due to concern about sulfonamide-related allergy history.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Etodolac is an NSAID option used for chronic osteoarthritis pain; it can be considered when an NSAID is desired and celecoxib is being avoided.
rationales.B: Celecoxib is a COX-2 selective NSAID that can reduce GI risk, but the clinician is intentionally avoiding it due to the patient’s sulfonamide allergy history.
rationales.C: Ketorolac has high GI/renal toxicity risk and is limited to short-term use—poor choice for chronic OA pain.
rationales.D: Indomethacin has a higher adverse-effect burden and is not typically preferred for chronic OA pain (more commonly associated with acute gout treatment).
rationales.E: Acetaminophen may help pain, but the question asks for an NSAID option for inflammatory OA pain control.

takeaway: When chronic OA pain needs an NSAID and celecoxib is avoided, agents like etodolac are reasonable alternatives (still counsel GI/renal/CV risks).

title: Nitrate paired with hydralazine in HFrEF
stem:
A 57-year-old man with HFrEF (EF 25%) remains symptomatic despite guideline-directed therapy.
He is started on hydralazine for afterload reduction.
The cardiologist adds the nitrate that is classically paired with hydralazine to improve outcomes in select HFrEF patients.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Isosorbide dinitrate is the classic long-acting nitrate used with hydralazine in HFrEF to provide vasodilation and symptom/outcome benefit in appropriate patients.
rationales.B: Isosorbide mononitrate is used for chronic angina, but the classic HFrEF combination is hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate.
rationales.C: Sublingual nitroglycerin is used for acute angina relief and is not the long-term HFrEF combination agent.
rationales.D: Ranolazine is an antianginal agent and is not the nitrate paired with hydralazine in HFrEF.
rationales.E: Sildenafil (PDE5 inhibitor) treats ED and pulmonary hypertension; it is not the nitrate paired with hydralazine and is contraindicated with nitrates.

takeaway: Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate is a high-yield HFrEF combo—know isosorbide dinitrate as the nitrate component.

title: Overactive bladder with trouble swallowing pills
stem:
A 66-year-old woman has overactive bladder with urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence.
She has difficulty swallowing pills due to esophageal strictures.
She wants a medication available as a transdermal system that reduces bladder detrusor overactivity via antimuscarinic effects.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic used for overactive bladder and is available in transdermal formulations (patch/gel) useful when oral pills are difficult.
rationales.B: Solifenacin is an oral antimuscarinic, but the question emphasizes transdermal availability.
rationales.C: Mirabegron is a beta-3 agonist and can treat OAB, but it is not the classic antimuscarinic transdermal option described.
rationales.D: Tamsulosin treats BPH-related urinary obstruction (hesitancy, weak stream), not overactive bladder urgency/incontinence.
rationales.E: Finasteride treats BPH and androgenic alopecia, not overactive bladder symptoms.

takeaway: Overactive bladder antimuscarinics: oxybutynin is high-yield and has transdermal options—counsel dry mouth/constipation.
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: Once-daily bladder-selective antimuscarinic
stem:
A 70-year-old woman has overactive bladder with urgency and urge incontinence.
She previously tried immediate-release oxybutynin but stopped due to severe dry mouth and confusion.
She prefers a once-daily antimuscarinic that is considered more bladder-selective (M3) and may be better tolerated.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Solifenacin is an antimuscarinic (more M3-selective) used once daily for overactive bladder and may be better tolerated than oxybutynin in some patients.
rationales.B: Oxybutynin commonly causes strong anticholinergic side effects (dry mouth, constipation, confusion), which she already could not tolerate.
rationales.C: Mirabegron is an effective alternative (beta-3 agonist), but the question asks for an antimuscarinic option specifically.
rationales.D: Tamsulosin treats BPH outlet obstruction symptoms, not overactive bladder urgency.
rationales.E: Phenazopyridine is a urinary analgesic for dysuria and does not treat detrusor overactivity.

takeaway: If oxybutynin is intolerable, consider alternative OAB antimuscarinics like solifenacin (still monitor for anticholinergic effects).
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics

title: One medication for BPH + erectile dysfunction
stem:
A 58-year-old man has lower urinary tract symptoms from BPH (weak stream, nocturia, hesitancy) and erectile dysfunction.
He is not taking nitrates.
He wants a single daily medication that can improve both BPH symptoms and erectile function.
question: Which medication is the best choice from the options below?

rationales.A: Correct. Tadalafil is a PDE5 inhibitor approved for erectile dysfunction and can also improve BPH lower urinary tract symptoms (often dosed daily).
rationales.B: Sildenafil treats erectile dysfunction but is not the classic ‘single daily medication’ for both ED and BPH symptoms in this list.
rationales.C: Tamsulosin improves BPH urinary symptoms but does not treat erectile dysfunction.
rationales.D: Finasteride reduces prostate size over months and can worsen sexual function; it does not treat ED.
rationales.E: Isosorbide mononitrate is a nitrate for angina and is contraindicated with PDE5 inhibitors.

takeaway: BPH + ED: tadalafil is a high-yield option that can address both (avoid with nitrates).
module_tags: Module 10a BPH + Overactive Bladder + Urinary Analgesics
